title: Structure and Function of the TPR-Domain Immunophilins FKBP51 and FKBP52 in Normal Physiology and Disease
summary: FKBP51 and FKBP52 are TPR-domain immunophilins that function as Hsp90 cochaperones with opposing effects on steroid hormone receptor signaling and cellular proliferation. Both proteins interact with Hsp90 and Hsp70 to facilitate steroid hormone receptor maturation and are involved in critical cellular processes including protein folding, cell cycle regulation, neuronal signaling, and intracellular calcium release through peptide bond isomerization. However, they exhibit distinct and often antagonistic regulatory roles: FKBP52 positively regulates androgen, glucocorticoid, and progesterone receptors while enhancing hormone binding and nuclear transport, whereas FKBP51 negatively regulates glucocorticoid and progesterone receptor activity while inhibiting hormone binding and nuclear translocation. FKBP51 uniquely sequesters Cdk4 in the Hsp90 complex to prevent Cdk4-cyclin D1 complex formation, thereby inhibiting cell proliferation and promoting myogenesis, an effect mediated through cis-trans isomerization of a specific peptide bond that FKBP52 cannot catalyze. The selective FKBP51 inhibitor SAFit binds to a transient pocket unique to FKBP51 and can block its function, while immunosuppressor FK506 similarly inhibits FKBP51, offering potential therapeutic strategies for conditions involving dysregulated FKBP51 signaling.

title: RNA-binding proteins in pain
summary:
# Summary: RNA-binding Proteins in Pain Signaling

RNA-binding proteins (RBPs) orchestrate pain regulation through multiple post-transcriptional mechanisms including RNA modification, splicing, stability, and translation control. The m6A methylation pathway plays a central role, where METTL3-mediated m6A deposition is recognized by reader proteins (YTHDF1/2/3) to regulate pain-related gene expression; notably, METTL3 overexpression reduces inflammatory pain sensitization while its knockdown causes mechanical and thermal hypersensitivity. Alternative splicing of pain-associated genes—including pro-nociceptive VEGF-A isoforms regulated by SRSF1—and FUS-mediated transcriptional control in dorsal root ganglia following nerve injury exemplify how RBPs fine-tune nociceptor excitability. Local protein synthesis at nerve terminals emerges as a critical pain mechanism, with eIF4E phosphorylation by MNK1 specifically driving inflammatory and migraine pain, while its prevention blocks CFA-induced sensitization. These findings demonstrate that post-transcriptional gene control through RBP-mediated pathways represents a major convergence point for chronic pain development and suggests multiple therapeutic targets for pain intervention.

title: IGF2BP2 regulates the proliferation and migration of endometrial stromal cells through the PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling pathway in Hu sheep
summary: IGF2BP2, an RNA-binding protein highly expressed in the endometrium with phase-dependent regulation, acts as a suppressor of endometrial stromal cell proliferation and migration in Hu sheep. IGF2BP2 knockdown paradoxically enhanced both cell proliferation and migration while upregulating pro-migratory factors including MMP9, vascular endothelial growth factor, prolactin, and integrin β4 (ITGB4). This knockdown effect is mediated through ITGB4-dependent activation of the PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling pathway, a key metabolic regulator implicated in cell growth and proliferation. Importantly, targeted downregulation of ITGB4 attenuated the enhanced proliferation and migration induced by IGF2BP2 loss, indicating that ITGB4 functions as the critical downstream node linking IGF2BP2 to pathway activation. These findings reveal a regulatory axis where IGF2BP2 suppresses endometrial stromal cell function by constraining ITGB4 expression and subsequent PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling, suggesting that dysregulation of this pathway may contribute to endometrial dysfunction and reproductive disorders.

title: The Role of Collagen Triple Helix Repeat Containing 1 (CTHRC1) in Cancer Development and Progression
summary:
# Summary

CTHRC1 (Collagen Triple Helix Repeat Containing 1) is a secreted glycoprotein with pleiotropic roles in tissue remodeling and cancer progression. The protein facilitates cell migration and reduces collagen deposition in the extracellular matrix while promoting actin polymerization and cell polarity, functions that correlate with its expression in remodeling tissues including myocardium, renal arteries, and epithelium. In cancer, CTHRC1 is consistently upregulated across multiple tumor types—including melanoma, pancreatic, prostate, breast, colorectal, and hepatocellular carcinoma—where elevated expression associates with enhanced proliferation, invasion, metastasis, and poor prognosis. CTHRC1 promotes oncogenic signaling through multiple pathways: it activates TGF-β signaling by directly binding and stabilizing TGF-β receptor complexes, engages WNT/β-catenin signaling via FZD/ROR2 interactions, and activates Src-FAK and ERK cascades that control cell adhesiveness and angiogenesis. Additionally, CTHRC1 downregulates the tumor suppressor TP53 while upregulating MMP9, and activates PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling to promote tumor angiogenesis. These multifaceted pro-tumorigenic functions position CTHRC1 as a critical driver of cancer progression and a potential therapeutic target.

title: Aldo-Keto Reductase (AKR) Superfamily Website and Database: an Update
summary:
# Summary

The aldo-keto reductase (AKR) superfamily represents an ancient, evolutionarily conserved group of phase I oxidoreductase enzymes found across all domains of life, from archebacteria to eukaryotes. These enzymes primarily catalyze the NAD(P)H-dependent reduction of carbonyl groups on endogenous and xenobiotic substrates, converting aldehydes to primary alcohols and ketones to secondary alcohols, though specialized AKR members also reduce nitro-groups, steroid double bonds, and other substrates. Structurally, AKRs share a distinctive (α/β)8-barrel fold and contain a conserved catalytic tetrad (Y55, L84, H117, D50) that performs a push-pull mechanism for hydride transfer, with refined substrate specificity achieved through variations such as replacement of H117 with glutamic acid in certain members like AKR1D1. Beyond their classical metabolic roles, AKRs have emerged as significant factors in oncology, chemotherapeutic drug resistance, endocrinology, and toxicology, with the AKR superfamily website and database providing a comprehensive resource reflecting over 900 research articles published between 2019 and 2023 documenting their diverse biological functions.

title: Prediagnostic plasma proteomics profile for hepatocellular carcinoma
journal: JNCI Journal of the National Cancer Institute
summary: This prediagnostic plasma proteomics study identified a signature of 56 elevated proteins associated with hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) risk, with chitinase-3-like protein 1, growth differentiation factor 15, interleukin-1 receptor antagonist protein, and E-selectin among the most prominent biomarkers. Pathway analysis revealed that these proteins are enriched in functional networks governing cell viability, adhesion, proteolysis, apoptosis, and inflammatory responses, suggesting that HCC development involves coordinated dysregulation of multiple cellular processes. The identification of these prediagnostic protein signatures may enable earlier detection and risk stratification of individuals at high risk for HCC development. These findings highlight the utility of plasma proteomics as a discovery tool for understanding the biological mechanisms underlying hepatocellular carcinogenesis and identifying potential diagnostic or therapeutic targets.

title: Hepatic stearoyl-CoA desaturase-1 deficiency induces fibrosis and hepatocellular carcinoma-related gene activation under a high carbohydrate low fat diet
journal: Biochimica et biophysica acta. Molecular and cell biology of lipids
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that hepatic stearoyl-CoA desaturase-1 (SCD1) deficiency promotes hepatic fibrosis and hepatocellular carcinoma-related gene activation in mice fed a high-carbohydrate, low-fat diet. SCD1 catalyzes the synthesis of monounsaturated fatty acids (MUFAs) from saturated fatty acids; its deletion increases hepatic lipid saturation, accumulation of long-chain saturated fatty acyl chains, and endoplasmic reticulum stress and inflammation. SCD1-deficient mice develop progressive hepatic fibrosis characterized by perivascular, perisinusoidal, pericellular, and bridging patterns, accompanied by elevated expression of profibrotic markers including transforming growth factor-β1, osteopontin, collagen types I and III, and hepatocellular carcinoma markers such as alpha-fetoprotein. Notably, supplementation with oleate, an SCD1-generated MUFA, restored normal gene expression and prevented fibrosis development, indicating that MUFA deprivation itself drives liver injury. These findings establish lipid saturation dysregulation as a critical mechanism in metabolic-associated fatty liver disease pathogenesis and suggest that adequate MUFA availability is protective against hepatic fibrosis and carcinogenic transformation.

title: Molecular insights and clinical implications for the tumor suppressor role of SCFFBXW7 E3 ubiquitin ligase
journal: Biochimica et biophysica acta. Reviews on cancer
summary:
# Summary

The SCFFBXW7 E3 ubiquitin ligase complex, assembled from FBXW7, SKP1, CUL1, and RBX1, functions as a critical tumor suppressor by mediating the ubiquitin-proteasome-dependent degradation of multiple oncogenic proteins including cyclin E, c-MYC, c-JUN, NOTCH, and MCL1. FBXW7 mutations or downregulation have been identified across numerous malignancies such as endometrial, colorectal, lung, and breast cancers, where loss of FBXW7 function promotes cancer cell proliferation, invasion, migration, and drug resistance. The three FBXW7 isoforms (α, β, γ) localize to distinct cellular compartments and regulate substrate degradation through phosphorylation-dependent mechanisms, exemplified by GSK3β and CDK2-mediated phosphorylation of cyclin E, which enables FBXW7-binding and G1-S phase checkpoint control. Dysregulation of FBXW7-dependent proteolysis contributes to chromosomal instability and leukemogenesis, particularly through accumulation of c-MYC in B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia. Oncogenic signaling pathways such as RAS/MAPK suppress FBXW7 function and can impair substrate degradation, while post-translational modifications and adaptor proteins like RACK1 modulate FBXW7 activity and substrate interactions.

title: LINC00513 promotes colorectal cancer malignant progression by binding with IGF2BP1 to enhance the stability of connective tissue growth factor mRNA
summary: This study identifies LINC00513, a long noncoding RNA, as a critical promoter of colorectal cancer progression through a regulatory pathway involving IGF2BP1 and connective tissue growth factor (CTGF). LINC00513 directly interacts with the RNA-binding protein IGF2BP1 to enhance the stability of CTGF mRNA, thereby increasing CTGF expression levels in cancer cells. All three molecules—LINC00513, IGF2BP1, and CTGF—are significantly upregulated in colorectal cancer tissues and correlate with malignant progression. Knockdown of LINC00513 substantially inhibits the malignant behaviors of colorectal cancer cells, while overexpression of either IGF2BP1 or CTGF can reverse this inhibitory effect. This work establishes the LINC00513/IGF2BP1/CTGF axis as a novel oncogenic pathway that drives colorectal cancer malignancy, suggesting potential therapeutic targets for intervention in this disease.

title: Netrin-4: Focus on Its Role in Axon Guidance, Tissue Stability, Angiogenesis and Tumors
summary: Netrin-4, a secreted protein member of the Netrins family, functions as a multifaceted regulator of axon guidance, axonal outgrowth, and migration orientation through its association with laminin β-chain components. Beyond its classical role in promoting neurite growth, Netrin-4 plays significant roles in angiogenesis and tissue development, including kidney and alveolar development. The protein is also implicated in tumor biology, regulating both tumor occurrence and metastasis through its involvement in angiogenic processes. These findings suggest that Netrin-4 operates as a key molecular orchestrator linking developmental processes with pathological conditions including tumorigenesis. The broad involvement of Netrin-4 across axonal, vascular, and neoplastic pathways highlights its importance as a potential therapeutic target in conditions characterized by aberrant angiogenesis or tumor progression.

title: Chromatin remodelers as therapeutic targets
summary:
# Summary: Chromatin Remodelers as Therapeutic Targets

SWI/SNF chromatin remodeling complexes, which utilize ATP hydrolysis to reposition nucleosomes and regulate gene expression and transcriptional memory, represent critical therapeutic targets because mutations in their subunits occur in over 20% of human cancers and multiple neurodevelopmental disorders. The three major SWI/SNF subfamilies—CBAF, PBAF, and ncBAF—localize to distinct genomic regions and contain specialized subunits that recognize acetylated histones and interact with transcription factors through liquid-liquid phase separation mechanisms. Inactivation of key subunits such as SMARCB1, ARID1A, SMARCA4, and PBRM1 promotes cancer development, while their loss specifically impairs enhancer accessibility and destabilizes complex assembly, as demonstrated by rhabdoid tumor models where SMARCB1 re-expression restores differentiation gene expression. Beyond point mutations, oncogenic mechanisms include fusion proteins like SS18-SSX that abnormally prevent SMARCB1 incorporation, and gene amplifications like BRD9 in acute myeloid leukemia that drive aberrant enhancer activation. Other chromatin remodelers including ISWI, CHD, and INO80/SWR complexes are similarly implicated in cancer, establishing the broader importance of chromatin regulation in disease pathogenesis and its potential as a therapeutic avenue.

title: Repurposing of triamterene as a histone deacetylase inhibitor to overcome cisplatin resistance in lung cancer treatment
journal: Journal of Cancer Research and Clinical Oncology
summary: Triamterene, an FDA-approved diuretic, was repurposed as a histone deacetylase (HDAC) inhibitor to overcome cisplatin resistance in non-small cell lung cancer. The compound inhibits HDACs and increases histone acetylation, which promotes the transcription factor Sp1 and enhances expression of the cisplatin transporter hCTR1 and the cell cycle inhibitor p21. Through these mechanisms, triamterene enhances cellular cisplatin accumulation and potentiates cisplatin-induced cell cycle arrest, DNA damage, and apoptosis in resistant cancer cells. In vivo studies confirmed that triamterene significantly potentiates the anti-cancer efficacy of cisplatin in cisplatin-resistant patient-derived xenografts. This work demonstrates a novel drug repurposing strategy that addresses a critical clinical challenge in lung cancer chemotherapy by combining epigenetic modulation with improved drug delivery.

title: Plasma membrane and nuclear phosphatidylinositol 4,5-bisphosphate signalling in cancer
summary:
# Summary

Phosphatidylinositol 4,5-bisphosphate (PI(4,5)P2) and related phosphoinositides serve as critical regulators of both plasma membrane and nuclear signaling in cancer progression. At the plasma membrane, PI(4,5)P2 controls actin dynamics through recruitment of N-WASP and regulation of actin-binding proteins, promoting the formation of invasive structures like invadopodia and podosomes that enable extracellular matrix degradation and cell migration. Dysregulation of phosphoinositide metabolism—characterized by elevated PI(3,4,5)P3 through PIK3CA mutations or PTEN loss, and reduced PI(4,5)P2 levels—drives cancer cell invasiveness by activating pro-migratory signaling pathways including PI3K/Akt and Src kinase. In the nucleus, PI(4,5)P2 regulates transcription factors and chromatin organization, controlling gene expression programs associated with cell motility and invasion. Complementary roles of membrane lipids including sphingomyelin and cholesterol establish lipid raft microdomains that organize signaling complexes, while alterations in membrane composition are associated with metastatic progression and nuclear morphological changes characteristic of invasive cancer cells.

title: Brain infiltration of breast cancer stem cells is facilitated by paracrine signaling by inhibitor of differentiation 3 to nuclear respiratory factor 1
journal: Journal of Cancer Research and Clinical Oncology
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals a paracrine signaling mechanism by which inhibitor of differentiation 3 (ID3) and nuclear respiratory factor 1 (NRF1) facilitate breast cancer stem cell (BCSC) brain infiltration. NRF1 overexpression promotes BCSC migration, adhesion to brain endothelial cells, and acquisition of stem cell properties, while ID3-expressing endothelial stem cells (ESCsID3) actively support this process by facilitating BCSC adhesion, blood-brain barrier crossing, colonization, and proliferation. The study demonstrates that exosomal ID3 released from endothelial cells further enhances NRF1-induced BCSC growth, and that environmental signals such as PCB153 can amplify these tumorigenic effects both in vitro and in vivo. These findings establish a novel cross-talk between cancer stem cells and brain endothelial cells mediated by the ID3-NRF1 axis, identifying new therapeutic targets for preventing breast cancer brain metastasis.

title: A novel metastatic promoter CEMIP and its downstream molecular targets and signaling pathway of cellular migration and invasion in SCLC cells based on proteome analysis
journal: Journal of Cancer Research and Clinical Oncology
summary:
# Summary

CEMIP (cell migration inducing hyaluronidase) is significantly elevated in small cell lung cancer (SCLC) tissues and functions as a novel metastatic promoter by triggering cellular migration and invasion through downstream signaling pathways. Knockdown of CEMIP expression in SCLC H446 cells reduced both migration and invasion capacity while altering the proteome, with notable changes including upregulation of SPICE1 and CRYAB and downregulation of S100A6. Proteomic analysis revealed that differentially expressed proteins upon CEMIP reduction were predominantly involved in TGF-β, MAPK, and GABAergic synapse signaling pathways, suggesting these are key mechanisms through which CEMIP promotes metastatic phenotypes. The enriched biological processes related to calcium ion binding and epithelial cell migration further implicate CEMIP in remodeling the cellular environment to facilitate metastatic potential in SCLC.

title: Long non-coding RNAs (lncRNAs) in cancer development: new insight from STAT3 signaling pathway to immune evasion
summary:
# Summary

Long non-coding RNAs (lncRNAs) regulate the STAT3 signaling pathway to control critical cancer-promoting processes including proliferation, apoptosis, metastasis, angiogenesis, and drug resistance. STAT3, a key transcription factor activated by multiple tyrosine kinases (including VEGFR, EGFR, and Src) and phosphorylated by serine kinases like p38MAPK and mTOR, promotes cancer progression while being negatively regulated by SOCS and PIAS proteins. Dysregulation of this lncRNA/STAT3 axis is particularly associated with colorectal cancer, where specific lncRNAs (H19, GAS5, MALAT1, HOTAIR) and microRNAs are dysregulated, with elevated phosphorylated STAT3 levels correlating with poor prognosis. STAT3 suppression or knockdown increases apoptotic markers (TP53 and caspase-3) and decreases cell proliferation, demonstrating the therapeutic potential of targeting this pathway. The JAK/STAT cascade also integrates immunosuppressive signals from tumor-associated cells (Th2, Th17, MDSCs) that secrete inhibitory cytokines, thereby contributing to immune evasion and tumor progression.

title: Emerging roles of mitochondrial sirtuin SIRT5 in succinylation modification and cancer development
summary:
# Summary

SIRT5, a mitochondrial NAD-dependent desuccinylase, emerges as a critical regulator of lysine succinylation—a post-translational modification that significantly impacts metabolic pathways, gene transcription, DNA damage responses, and protein folding. By desuccinylating key metabolic enzymes, SIRT5 modulates diverse physiological processes including glucose oxidation, fatty acid oxidation, ammonia detoxification, and reactive oxygen species scavenging, thereby influencing energy metabolism, glycolysis, and mitochondrial function. The protein exhibits dual and context-dependent roles in cancer biology: it can promote tumor proliferation, metastasis, drug resistance, and metabolic reprogramming, while conversely functioning as a tumor suppressor by inhibiting tumor cell growth and inducing apoptosis. SIRT5 knockout studies reveal that over 90% of succinylation sites show increased modification levels, predominantly affecting proteins involved in energy metabolism and β-oxidation, underscoring SIRT5's central role in metabolic regulation. These findings position SIRT5 as a key player in cancer development and metabolic reprogramming, with potential implications for understanding disease progression and therapeutic intervention strategies.

title: Mettl3/Eed/Ythdc1 regulatory axis controls endometrial receptivity and function
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that the Mettl3/Eed/Ythdc1 regulatory axis controls endometrial receptivity by coordinating m6A RNA methylation with H3K27me3 histone modification. Mettl3 catalyzes m6A deposition, which is recognized by the reader protein Ythdc1; Ythdc1 then recruits Eed, a component of the Polycomb Repressive Complex 2, to suppress H3K27me3 and maintain open chromatin architecture. Recurrent implantation failure is associated with reduced m6A levels and decreased expression of critical endometrial function genes including Lif, Hoxa10, and Hand2. Mettl3 deletion in mouse endometrium impairs decidualization, reduces uterine weight and litter size, promotes apoptosis, and accelerates endometrial aging through concurrent loss of m6A modification and elevation of repressive H3K27me3 marks. Overexpression of Eed partially rescues m6A levels and restores expression of endometrial receptivity markers in Mettl3-deficient cells, indicating that the Mettl3/Eed/Ythdc1 axis functions as an integrated epigenetic regulator essential for normal endometrial function and fertility.

title: KIF2C/MCAK a prognostic biomarker and its oncogenic potential in malignant progression, and prognosis of cancer patients: a systematic review and meta-analysis as biomarker
journal: Critical reviews in clinical laboratory sciences
summary:
# Summary

KIF2C, the most well-characterized member of the kinesin-13 family, plays a critical role in regulating microtubule dynamics and is involved in multiple hallmarks of cancer including genome instability, cell death resistance, invasion and metastasis, and immune evasion. High KIF2C expression is significantly elevated in at least 33 cancer types, including breast cancer, non-small cell lung cancer, and hepatocellular carcinoma, where it promotes proliferation, migration, invasion, DNA repair, and therapy resistance through modulation of primary cilium signaling and cell-extracellular matrix interactions. Meta-analyses consistently demonstrate that elevated KIF2C expression is associated with poor prognosis, decreased overall survival, and reduced disease-free survival in cancer patients. KIF2C expression is regulated by multiple mechanisms including kinases (Aurora A/B, Plk1, Cdk1), epigenetic modifications (DNA methylation), and non-coding RNAs (lncRNAs and miRNAs), making it a potent prognostic biomarker of malignant progression. Notably, KIF2C contributes to resistance against microtubule-targeting chemotherapies such as taxanes, suggesting that targeting KIF2C could enhance therapeutic efficacy and improve patient outcomes.

title: Rapamycin Increases the Development Competence of Yak (Bos grunniens) Oocytes by Promoting Autophagy via Upregulating 17β-Estradiol and HIF-1α During In Vitro Maturation
journal: Animals : an Open Access Journal from MDPI
summary: Rapamycin at optimal concentrations (0.1-1.0 nM) enhances yak oocyte developmental competence during in vitro maturation by promoting autophagy through a coordinated upregulation of 17β-estradiol (E2) and hypoxia-inducible factor 1-alpha (HIF-1α). Rapamycin activates the autophagy pathway, as evidenced by increased expression of key autophagy markers (ATG5, BECN1, and LC3-II), while simultaneously upregulating steroidogenic enzymes including aromatase and CYP17A1 that drive E2 synthesis. E2 and HIF-1α function in a mutually regulatory network to sustain rapamycin-induced autophagy, with HIF-1α promoting E2 synthesis in granulosa cells and also preventing oocyte apoptosis through VEGF upregulation. Inhibition of either E2 signaling or HIF-1α activity substantially diminishes the beneficial effects of rapamycin on oocyte maturation and subsequent embryonic development rates, demonstrating their obligatory roles in this pathway. These findings establish that rapamycin-mediated improvement in mammalian oocyte quality depends on crosstalk between metabolic autophagy activation and steroid hormone signaling, with implications for reproductive biotechnology and understanding metabolic reprogramming in female germ cells.

title: cGAS/STING signaling pathway in gynecological malignancies: From molecular mechanisms to therapeutic values
summary:
# Summary

The cGAS/STING signaling pathway serves as a critical innate immune mechanism that detects cytosolic DNA and triggers robust antitumor responses through production of type I interferons and inflammatory cytokines. Upon recognition of double-stranded DNA, cGAS synthesizes the immunotransmitter cGAMP, which activates STING at the endoplasmic reticulum, initiating a signaling cascade involving TBK1 and IRF3 phosphorylation that leads to type I interferon production and NF-κB activation. However, in gynecological malignancies including cervical, endometrial, and ovarian cancers, multiple mechanisms suppress this pathway—including cGAS promoter methylation, IDH3α-mediated suppression, and microRNA inhibition—allowing tumors to evade immune surveillance. Therapeutic strategies targeting this pathway, such as STING agonists, IDH3α inhibitors, and SETDB1-TRIM28 complex inhibition, enhance antitumor immunity and CD8+ T cell infiltration. Paradoxically, chronic cGAS/STING activation in endometriosis and adenomyosis promotes pathological inflammation through enhanced cytokine production, demonstrating the pathway's context-dependent role in disease pathogenesis. Understanding these dual mechanisms positions cGAS/STING modulation as a promising therapeutic target for overcoming immune evasion in gynecological cancers.

title: Deciphering the stromal molecular landscape: the correlation between p16 and α-SMA in epithelial ovarian cancer
journal: Journal of Cancer Research and Clinical Oncology
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals a critical inverse relationship between p16 and α-SMA stromal expression in epithelial ovarian cancer, with important implications for BRCA mutational status and disease prognosis. p16, a cyclin-dependent kinase inhibitor that promotes cellular senescence and acts as a tumor suppressor, is significantly more prevalent in early-stage disease (stage I-II) and wild-type BRCA tumors, whereas α-SMA, a marker of cancer-associated fibroblast activation, is predominantly expressed in advanced disease and BRCA-mutated cancers. The tumor microenvironment, particularly cancer-associated fibroblasts expressing α-SMA, actively promotes tumor initiation, progression, and metastasis while simultaneously suppressing p16-mediated stromal senescence, thereby creating a pro-tumorigenic environment. Notably, p16 absence correlates with significantly shorter survival and is nearly absent in BRCA-mutated advanced-stage tumors, suggesting that loss of stromal senescence drives aggressive disease progression. These findings establish p16 and α-SMA expression patterns as potential biomarkers for predicting BRCA status and clinical outcomes in epithelial ovarian cancer.

title: The N6‐methyladenosine landscape of ovarian development and aging highlights the regulation by RNA stability and chromatin state
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that N6-methyladenosine (m6A) modifications regulate ovarian aging through a coordinated epigenetic cascade involving chromatin remodeling and retrotransposon activation. During ovarian aging, elevated m6A levels—driven by increased METTL16 expression and reduced FTO demethylase activity—suppress the histone methyltransferase SUV39H1, thereby decreasing repressive H3K9me3 marks. This chromatin relaxation subsequently activates endogenous retrovirus elements (LTR7, LTR12, and ERV1), which paradoxically inhibit oocyte proliferation and promote aging phenotypes. FTO knockout experiments confirmed this mechanism's physiological importance, showing that loss of FTO function accelerates ovarian aging through impaired autophagy (reduced ATG3, ATG5, ATG7, ATG12, and p62) and increased DNA damage markers (γ-H2AX foci), ultimately reducing oocyte numbers, follicle counts, and reproductive capacity. These findings establish m6A-mediated RNA stability and chromatin state regulation as critical drivers of ovarian senescence, with potential implications for age-related infertility.

title: Bifunctional roles of survivin-ΔEx3 and survivin-2B for susceptibility to apoptosis in endometrial carcinomas
journal: Journal of Cancer Research and Clinical Oncology
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that two alternatively spliced survivin isoforms, survivin-ΔEx3 and survivin-2B, play opposing roles in regulating apoptosis susceptibility in endometrial carcinoma cells. Both variants reduce cell proliferation and upregulate the cell cycle inhibitor p21waf1, yet they exhibit distinct responses to apoptotic stimuli: survivin-ΔEx3-expressing cells show increased apoptosis following TNF-α treatment, while survivin-2B-expressing cells are sensitized to doxorubicin-induced apoptosis accompanied by bcl-2 downregulation. The three survivin variants (wild-type, ΔEx3, and 2B) regulate each other reciprocally, with overexpression of one isoform suppressing expression of the others. In clinical endometrial carcinoma samples, survivin-ΔEx3 is significantly elevated and positively correlates with apoptosis, whereas wild-type survivin correlates with cell proliferation, suggesting that the balance among survivin isoforms modulates cell kinetics and drug sensitivity in endometrial cancer.

title: Overexpression and ratio disruption of ΔNp63 and TAp63 isoform equilibrium in endometrial adenocarcinoma: correlation with obesity, menopause, and grade I/II tumors
journal: Journal of Cancer Research and Clinical Oncology
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that disruption of p63 isoform balance significantly contributes to endometrial adenocarcinoma pathogenesis, with ΔNp63 showing marked overexpression (4.3-fold) compared to TAp63 (1.8-fold) in malignant specimens. ΔNp63, which functions as a dominant negative inhibitor of the p53 family and exerts oncogenic effects, was specifically correlated with lower-grade (I/II) tumors, while TAp63—which retains p53-like tumor-suppressive functions—showed associations with obesity and late menopause as clinical risk factors. Both p63 isoforms play critical roles in endometrial adenocarcinoma development and progression through their opposing effects on transcription activation and apoptosis, establishing the p63 isoform equilibrium as a key molecular mechanism in this malignancy. The significant overexpression of the oncogenic ΔNp63 variant suggests that disrupting the balance between pro-tumorigenic and tumor-suppressive isoforms is a central feature of endometrial cancer pathogenesis.

title: Evaluation of Genes and Molecular Pathways Common between Diffuse Large B-cell Lymphoma (DLBCL) and Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE): A Systems Biology Approach
journal: Medical Journal of the Islamic Republic of Iran
summary:
# Summary

This systems biology study identifies 146 shared genes between diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL) and systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), explaining why SLE patients have elevated cancer risk. The shared genes predominantly participate in viral defense responses and interferon signaling, with five hub genes—IFIT3, IFIT1, DDX58, CCL2, and OASL—emerging as central regulatory nodes in both diseases. Elevated interferons (IFN-α and IFN-β) in SLE activate the JAK/STAT pathway, which upregulates IFIT genes and promotes lymphocyte proliferation through IL-2 signaling and c-myc activation, ultimately driving uncontrolled cell cycle progression via cyclin upregulation. The circUBAP2/miR-4756/IFIT axis further amplifies proliferation by increasing inflammatory cytokines IL-17 and IL-1β, which activate pro-growth signaling through NF-κB and suppress the PTEN tumor suppressor. A neural network model using these hub genes achieved exceptional diagnostic accuracy in distinguishing between SLE and DLBCL, suggesting these shared molecular pathways represent a mechanistic link between autoimmune dysregulation and lymphoma development.

title: Intercellular Adhesion Molecule 3: Structure, Cellular Functions, and Emerging Role in Human Diseases
summary:
# Summary

Intercellular Adhesion Molecule 3 (ICAM3) is a transmembrane glycoprotein abundantly expressed on leukocytes, T cells, B cells, endothelial cells, dendritic cells, and macrophages, where it binds to its receptor LFA-1 to mediate critical immune functions including T-cell motility, apoptotic neutrophil clearance, and immune cell activation. Beyond its canonical immunological roles, ICAM3 is elevated in multiple malignancies—including breast cancer, non-small cell lung cancer, cervical cancer, B-cell lymphoma, and multiple myeloma—where it promotes cancer cell proliferation, migration, epithelial-mesenchymal transition, and metastasis while inhibiting apoptosis. ICAM3 drives these pro-tumoral effects through activation of the Src/PI3K/AKT signaling pathway, which culminates in NF-κB nucleation and inflammatory factor secretion, along with promotion of cancer stemness through OCT4 pathway interactions. Notably, ICAM3 expression correlates with TNM staging, invasiveness, and radiotherapy resistance in cervical cancer, establishing it as a critical driver of cancer progression. Therapeutic strategies targeting ICAM3, including the LFA-1 antagonist lifitegrast and anti-inflammatory agents like aspirin and ibuprofen, effectively inhibit cancer cell migration and metastasis, offering promising interventions for malignant disease management.

title: Targeting NLRC5 in cardiomyocytes protects postinfarction cardiac injury by enhancing autophagy flux through the CAVIN1/CAV1 axis
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that NLRC5, a nucleotide-binding oligomerization domain-like receptor family protein, protects cardiomyocytes from postinfarction injury by enhancing autophagic flux through the CAVIN1/CAV1 regulatory axis. NLRC5 is upregulated in ischemic cardiomyocytes following myocardial infarction and promotes autophagy by interacting with CAVIN1 to facilitate its degradation, which subsequently downregulates CAV1 and permits activation of the ATG12-ATG5 complex. NLRC5 overexpression markedly improves cardiac function, reduces infarct size and fibrosis, and increases autophagic markers (LC3-II, Beclin-1) while decreasing inflammatory cytokines and fibrosis-related genes. Conversely, NLRC5 knockdown impairs autophagy, exacerbates cardiomyocyte death, enlarges infarct size, and worsens cardiac dysfunction, confirming that autophagy is essential for NLRC5-mediated cardioprotection. Pharmacological autophagy inhibition with chloroquine or bafilomycin A1 blocks the cardioprotective effects of NLRC5 overexpression, establishing that enhanced autophagic flux is the critical mechanism underlying NLRC5's therapeutic benefits in myocardial infarction.

title: Expression of E-CADHERIN and miR-200b in Different Forms of Endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that epithelial–mesenchymal transition (EMT) plays a central role in endometriosis pathogenesis through differential regulation of the miR-200b-ZEB1-E-CADHERIN regulatory axis in different endometriosis subtypes. E-CADHERIN expression is substantially reduced in endometriotic cysts compared to eutopic endometrium, a hallmark of EMT that enhances endometriotic cell migration and invasion. The miR-200b-ZEB1 feedback loop is differentially regulated between endometriosis forms: miR-200b is downregulated in ovarian endometriosis but upregulated in deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIE), while both subtypes show increased miR-200b in endometriotic cysts. Loss of E-CADHERIN activates the Wnt/β-catenin pathway, which further promotes cell motility and invasion while inhibiting ZEB1 expression, creating a cascade that generates hybrid epithelial/mesenchymal phenotypes characteristic of endometriotic lesions. These findings demonstrate that EMT regulation is controlled by local microenvironmental factors and that distinct molecular pathways distinguish DIE from ovarian endometriosis, suggesting form-specific mechanisms underlying the disease pathogenesis of pelvic pain and infertility.

title: Significance of Malic Enzyme 1 in Cancer: A Review
summary:
# Summary

Malic enzyme 1 (ME1) is a multifunctional metabolic enzyme that plays a critical role in cancer progression through its regulation of energy metabolism and cellular reprogramming. ME1 catalyzes malate conversion and generates NADPH, which maintains redox balance, suppresses cell death, and supports antioxidant defenses—processes central to cancer cell survival. The enzyme promotes epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) and enhances cancer stem cell properties via glutaminolysis and metabolic reprogramming, while simultaneously reducing reactive oxygen species, senescence, and apoptosis. ME1 expression is dysregulated in multiple cancer types including hepatocellular carcinoma, gastric cancer, breast cancer, and non-small-cell lung cancer, where its overexpression correlates with poor prognosis, drug resistance, and aggressive tumor behavior including metastasis and vascular invasion. Notably, ME1 is also hypomethylated in endometriosis, suggesting relevance beyond malignancy. ME1 activity is dynamically controlled by post-translational modifications, transcriptional regulators (including p53, KRAS, and NRF2), RNA methylation (NSUN2), and microRNA targeting (miR-30a, miR-612), positioning it as a central metabolic node in cancer progression.

title: Exploring the Role of Hypoxia and HIF-1α in the Intersection of Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus and Endometrial Cancer
summary:
# Summary

Type 2 diabetes mellitus and endometrial cancer share a multifaceted pathogenic relationship centered on hypoxia, oxidative stress, and metabolic reprogramming. Insulin resistance and obesity, both hallmarks of Type 2 diabetes, promote endometrial cancer development through multiple interconnected mechanisms: oxidative stress upregulates NF-κB to impair pancreatic β-cells, while adipose tissue secretes inflammatory cytokines and adipokines that increase estrogen production and promote tumor growth. Hypoxia plays a vital role by stabilizing HIF-1α, a transcription factor that is overexpressed in endometrial cancer cells and drives the Warburg effect—a metabolic shift toward glycolysis—through upregulation of glucose transporters and glycolytic enzymes. Notably, in Type 2 diabetes patients, elevated glucose paradoxically inhibits HIF-1α stabilization, whereas mutations in tricarboxylic acid cycle enzymes like fumarate hydratase stabilize HIF-1α by preventing its degradation, resulting in increased tumor size and poor clinical outcomes. These findings underscore how hypoxia and HIF-1α orchestrate metabolic reprogramming at the intersection of diabetes and endometrial cancer, positioning HIF-1α inhibition as a potential therapeutic target.

title: Differential Expression of Small Non-Coding RNAs in Uterine Leiomyomas
summary:
# Summary

This study systematically characterized differential expression of 2,189 small non-coding RNAs (sncRNAs) in uterine leiomyomas, identifying MED12 mutations as a key driver of sncRNA dysregulation. MED12 mutations, present in up to 80% of leiomyomas, activate aberrant Wnt/β-catenin and sex steroid receptor signaling, with seventeen MED12-associated sncRNAs enriched in pathways controlling growth factor receptor signaling and PI3K-Akt activation. The study confirmed downregulation of specific miRNAs (miR-19a-3p, miR-99a-5p) and piRNAs (piR-009295, piR-020326, piR-020365) in leiomyomas compared to normal myometrium, with functional implications in cell proliferation, migration, and inflammatory signaling. Notably, thirty-one sncRNAs showed race/ethnicity-associated differential expression unique to Black women, enriched in transcription factor activity and interleukin signaling, suggesting genetic or environmental disparities in leiomyoma pathogenesis. These findings establish sncRNA dysregulation as a critical mechanism linking MED12 mutations to leiomyoma pathology and reveal previously unrecognized molecular heterogeneity across racial groups.

title: Suture-Mediated Delivery System Reduces the Incidence of Uterine Scarring Through the TGF-β Pathway
summary: Cesarean section frequently results in uterine scarring, which compromises fertility, causes prolonged menstrual bleeding, and triggers chronic abdominal pain. The pathological mechanism involves transforming growth factor-β1 (TGF-β1) overexpression, which promotes excessive fibroblast synthesis and collagen deposition in the extracellular matrix. This study presents a novel suture-mediated delivery system combining recombinant human type III collagen (RhCol III) and TGF-β3 gene therapy that shifts the TGF-β balance toward scarless healing by reducing TGF-β1 expression by 62.8% while increasing TGF-β3 expression by 39%. The therapeutic sutures release both RhCol III and TGF-β3 at the wound site, where RhCol III promotes granulation tissue growth and facilitates cell migration and proliferation, ultimately reducing fibrosis rates by 16.8%. By modulating the TGF-β3-to-TGF-β1 ratio toward a scarless microenvironment, this biocompatible suture system offers a promising approach to prevent uterine scarring complications following cesarean delivery without requiring additional surgical intervention.

title: A new strategy for the treatment of advanced ovarian cancer: utilizing nanotechnology to regulate the tumor microenvironment
summary:
# Summary

Advanced ovarian cancer exploits multiple immunosuppressive pathways within the tumor microenvironment, which nanoparticles (NPs) can therapeutically target. The tumor microenvironment is characterized by activation of immunosuppressive cells including M2-TAMs, MDSCs, and Tregs, while simultaneously inhibiting immune effector cells such as dendritic cells, M1-TAMs, and T cells through mechanisms including the PD-1/PD-L1 axis, adenosine signaling, and CTLA-4 expression. NPs reverse this immunosuppressive state by stimulating M1-TAMs, dendritic cells, and T cells while reducing infiltration of M2-TAMs and Tregs. Tumor growth is promoted by multiple signaling cascades including the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway (which inhibits apoptosis and promotes proliferation and angiogenesis), the WNT/β-catenin pathway (which induces immunosuppressive VEGF-A), and NF-κB signaling (which regulates the MDSC-recruiting chemokine CXCL2). This nanotechnology-based approach offers a promising strategy to reprogram the tumor microenvironment from an immunosuppressive to an immune-activating state, potentially overcoming the multiple adaptive resistance mechanisms that advanced ovarian cancers utilize for progression.

title: A novel mechanism of FTO modulating the progression of endometriosis through mediating the m6A methylation of GEF-H1 in a YTHDF1-dependent manner
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies FTO as a critical regulator of endometriosis progression through m6A RNA methylation modifications. Estrogen and inflammatory factors upregulate FTO expression in endometrial stromal cells, leading to decreased m6A methylation levels at the 3'UTR of GEF-H1, a Rho guanine nucleotide exchange factor. Through YTHDF1-dependent recognition of the hypomethylated GEF-H1 mRNA, FTO increases GEF-H1 stability and expression, which subsequently activates the RhoA signaling pathway to promote endometrial stromal cell proliferation, migration, and invasion. Both genetic deletion and pharmacological inhibition of FTO impeded ectopic lesion growth in vivo, suggesting that targeting the FTO-mediated GEF-H1 m6A methylation axis represents a promising therapeutic strategy for endometriosis.

title: MEIS1-mediated Apoptosis via TNFR1 in Endometriosis
summary: This study identifies MEIS1, a homeobox transcription factor, as a critical regulator of apoptosis in endometriosis that functions through the TNFR1-caspase8 pathway. MEIS1 expression is significantly reduced in both eutopic and ectopic endometrial tissues from endometriosis patients compared to normal endometrium, and this downregulation contributes to disease pathogenesis by promoting endometrial stromal cell proliferation and apoptosis resistance. MEIS1 overexpression directly activates apoptosis in endometrial stromal cells by upregulating TNFR1 and triggering the TNFR1-caspase8-caspase3 apoptotic cascade. In a mouse endometriosis model, lentiviral delivery of MEIS1 significantly reduced endometriotic lesion size and weight while promoting apoptosis, suggesting therapeutic potential. The authors propose that loss of nuclear MEIS1 permits ectopic endometrial cells to evade apoptosis through impaired TNFR1 signaling, allowing abnormal cell proliferation and lesion formation. These findings position MEIS1 restoration as a potential therapeutic strategy to restore apoptotic capacity and inhibit endometriotic lesion development.

title: Role of epigenetic regulation in diminished ovarian reserve
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary:
# Summary

This article examines how epigenetic modifications regulate ovarian reserve and oocyte development, with implications for diminished ovarian reserve (DOR). DNA methylation, histone modifications, and non-coding RNAs (long noncoding RNAs and microRNAs) coordinately regulate granulosa cell function, hormone production, and oocyte maturation through control of gene expression. DNA methyltransferases (particularly DNMT3a and DNMT3b) mediate methylation during oocyte development, while dysregulation—such as TET3 overexpression and TDG downregulation—is associated with age-related oocyte senescence and N6-methyladenosine modifications. Environmental exposures like arsenic can silence steroidogenic factor 1 (SF-1), suppressing expression of key enzymes (CYP11A1, CYP19A1, STAR) and decreasing estradiol production while increasing granulosa cell apoptosis. The study identifies DNA methylation patterns in peripheral blood as potential biomarkers for age acceleration and DOR severity, and highlights therapeutic targets including HDAC6 for primordial follicle activation and the PI3K/AKT pathway for regulating cell proliferation and apoptosis in ovarian function.

title: Role of the integrin‑β1/TGF‑β1 signaling pathway in the pathogenesis of pelvic organ prolapse: A study on vaginal wall tissue alterations and molecular dysfunction
summary:
# Summary

Pelvic organ prolapse is characterized by irregular extracellular matrix production and breakdown in vaginal wall tissues, with significantly reduced collagen (COL1A1 and COL3A1) and elastic fiber content compared to normal tissue. The integrin-β1/TGF-β1 signaling pathway plays a central mechanotransductive role in maintaining vaginal tissue integrity by mediating fibroblast migration, suppressing apoptosis, and promoting collagen synthesis while inhibiting matrix metalloproteinase-1 (MMP-1). In POP tissues, integrin-β1, TGF-β1, and TIMP-1 (a matrix protease inhibitor) are significantly reduced, while MMP-1 is elevated, disrupting the balance between matrix synthesis and degradation. Mechanical forces normally preserve this equilibrium by upregulating integrin-β1 expression and enhancing the integrin-β1/TGF-β1 pathway, but excessive or sustained mechanical stress from vaginal delivery, obesity, and aging can trigger fibroblast apoptosis and pathway dysfunction. These findings demonstrate that impaired integrin-β1/TGF-β1 signaling represents a key molecular mechanism underlying POP pathogenesis, suggesting potential therapeutic targets for restoring vaginal wall tissue integrity.

title: Significant Association of Matrix Metalloproteinase‐9 Polymorphisms With Triple Negative Breast Cancer Risk
summary:
# Summary

Matrix metalloproteinase-9 (MMP-9) plays a critical role in breast cancer development and progression through its involvement in extracellular matrix remodeling, which facilitates tumor cell proliferation, invasion, migration, and angiogenesis. A specific MMP-9 polymorphism, rs3918242, shows a particularly strong association with triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) risk, with the TT genotype conferring a 2.49-fold increased risk in the Taiwanese population; the T allele exhibits elevated promoter activity that translates to increased circulating MMP-9 levels. The rs3918242 CT genotype is additionally linked to lymph node metastasis, while high MMP-9 expression correlates with poor chemotherapy response. The expression of MMP-9 is predominantly detected in endothelial cells and stromal fibroblasts, and TNFα-induced TNBC cell invasion and migration involve MMP-9 upregulation alongside CDKN1A/p21. These findings suggest that MMP-9 rs3918242 genotyping could serve as a predictive biomarker for TNBC risk assessment, with potential clinical applications in patient stratification and personalized treatment approaches.

title: Regulation of histone H3K27 methylation in inflammation and cancer
summary:
# Summary

Aberrant histone H3K27 methylation represents a critical epigenetic mechanism regulating inflammation, immune responses, and tumor progression. The methyltransferase EZH2 catalyzes H3K27 dimethylation and trimethylation, promoting chromatin compaction and transcriptional repression, while demethylases JMJD3 and UTX reverse these modifications by converting H3K27me2 and H3K27me3 to the transcriptionally active H3K27me1 state. EZH2 mutations and overexpression are linked to various cancers and can induce peritoneal metastasis, whereas the NF-κB-upregulated JMJD3 demethylase counteracts repressive marks and promotes inflammatory gene expression through IL-4/STAT6 signaling. Chronic inflammation-driven dysregulation of H3K27 methylation facilitates tumorigenesis and inflammatory diseases, establishing H3K27me3 as a key regulator of genes like IL-4, IL-13, and IL-12 that control immune responses. The EZH2 inhibitor Tazemetostat demonstrates the therapeutic potential of targeting this epigenetic axis in cancer treatment.

title: Lactylation modification in cancer: mechanisms, functions, and therapeutic strategies
summary: Lactylation, a post-translational modification driven by lactate metabolism, emerges as a critical epigenetic mechanism in cancer progression. Lactate, abundant in the tumor microenvironment, serves as a substrate for histone and non-histone protein lactylation catalyzed by multiple acetyltransferases (EP300, HBO1, KAT8, CBP, TIP60, AARS1, and AARS2), directly regulating gene expression and signal transduction pathways. These lactylation events promote cancer hallmarks including enhanced protein synthesis (via eEF1A2 lactylation), activated autophagy (PIK3C3 lactylation), improved DNA repair and chemotherapy resistance (NBS1 and MRE11 lactylation), and sustained proliferation through oncogenic signaling (Wnt/β-catenin, NF-κB, and YAP pathways). The bidirectional metabolic-epigenetic axis revealed here—where tumor metabolites influence epigenetic modifications while epigenetic changes regulate metabolic enzyme expression—establishes lactylation as a central hub integrating metabolic reprogramming with gene regulation in cancer. This mechanism provides novel therapeutic targets by disrupting specific lactyltransferase activities or lactate metabolism to overcome cancer cell adaptation and resistance mechanisms.

title: The role of m6A modification during macrophage metabolic reprogramming in human diseases and animal models
summary:
# Summary: m6A Modification and Macrophage Metabolic Reprogramming in Disease

N6-methyladenosine (m6A) modification orchestrates macrophage metabolic reprogramming through writer proteins (METTL3, METTL14, WTAP) and reader proteins (YTHDF1/2/3, IGF2BP2) that direct distinct inflammatory outcomes. In pro-inflammatory M1 macrophages, METTL3 enhances glycolysis while suppressing oxidative phosphorylation and the TCA cycle through STAT1-mediated upregulation of key glycolytic enzymes, simultaneously promoting M1 polarization via NF-κB and HIF-1α signaling pathways. METTL3-driven m6A modifications regulate disease pathogenesis in atherosclerosis, endometriosis, and inflammatory bowel disease by increasing reactive oxygen species production and mitochondrial damage. Conversely, the eraser protein FTO facilitates M2 polarization through fatty acid oxidation, while lactate-induced Trib1 stabilization and YTHDF3-mediated phosphoglycolate phosphatase upregulation promote alternative metabolic programs supporting oxidative metabolism. These findings establish m6A modification as a critical metabolic rheostat in macrophage immunometabolism, offering therapeutic targets through small molecule inhibition of m6A writers to ameliorate inflammatory diseases including endometriosis.

title: The Role of Yes‐Associated Protein in Inflammatory Diseases and Cancer
summary:
# Summary

Yes-associated protein (YAP), a central mediator in the Hippo signaling pathway, plays a critical role in governing cell proliferation, differentiation, and apoptosis, with dysregulation contributing to both inflammatory diseases and cancer development. YAP activation is tightly regulated through phosphorylation by the MST1/2-LATS1/2 kinase cascade; unphosphorylated YAP translocates to the nucleus where it interacts with TEAD transcription factors to initiate gene expression programs that promote inflammation, tumor cell proliferation, and metastasis. Multiple inflammatory stimuli including TNF-α, interleukins (IL-6, IL-13, IL-17A), lipopolysaccharide, and hypoxia enhance YAP activity and nuclear localization, while extracellular matrix stiffness provides mechanical regulation of YAP activation. In inflammatory airway diseases and cancers, elevated YAP promotes pathological outcomes including enhanced macrophage inflammation, epithelial barrier dysfunction, goblet cell hyperplasia, and fibroblast proliferation, while YAP inhibition via compounds such as verteporfin and tripterine alleviates disease progression. These findings establish YAP as a potential therapeutic target in managing both inflammatory and malignant diseases through pathway-specific interventions.

title: Pan-cancer analysis of Methyltransferase-like 16 (METTL16) and validated in colorectal cancer
summary: This pan-cancer analysis reveals that METTL16, a methyltransferase involved in m6A RNA methylation and S-adenosylmethionine homeostasis, functions as a critical regulator of cancer progression across multiple malignancies. METTL16 is significantly overexpressed in gastrointestinal and hepatobiliary cancers including colorectal cancer, cholangiocarcinoma, and liver hepatocellular carcinoma, while showing lower expression in several other cancer types such as breast and lung cancers. In colorectal cancer specifically, METTL16 downregulation reduces cell proliferation and migration, and high METTL16 expression correlates with shorter overall survival and increased epithelial-mesenchymal transition marker expression. METTL16 expression levels strongly correlate with immune cell infiltration, proliferation markers (MKI67 and PCNA), and multiple EMT-associated genes, suggesting its role in both tumor progression and immune modulation. Genetic alterations in METTL16 are associated with poor prognosis, and the protein localizes to both the nucleoplasm and cytoplasm, enabling its multi-functional roles in cancer biology. These findings establish METTL16 as a potential biomarker and therapeutic target for cancer prognosis and treatment.

title: Causal inference of inflammatory proteins in infertility: a Mendelian randomization study
summary: This Mendelian randomization study identifies causal relationships between circulating inflammatory proteins and infertility in both sexes. The chemokine CXCL6 emerged as the strongest causal factor in male infertility, functioning through neutrophil recruitment and immune activation while also being regulated by the HIF-1α and JAK-STAT pathways. In female infertility, inflammatory pathways showed more complex effects, with IL5, OSM, and ARTN demonstrating protective associations while CASP8, LIFR, IL18, PLAU, and IL20 had deleterious effects. Broader pathway analyses revealed that pro-inflammatory mediators, profibrotic mediators, and cytokine-cytokine receptor interactions represent key mechanisms in both male and female infertility, with male infertility additionally linked to oxidative stress and leukocytospermia. Drug repurposing analysis identified pioglitazone, manganese, leupeptin, and histamine as potential therapeutic candidates targeting these inflammatory pathways.

title: METTL3 is aberrantly expressed in endometriosis and suppresses proliferation, invasion, and migration of endometrial stromal cells
journal: The Kaohsiung Journal of Medical Sciences
summary: This study demonstrates that METTL3, an N6-methyladenosine (m6A) methyltransferase, is aberrantly downregulated in ectopic endometrial tissues and cells from endometriosis patients and functions as a suppressor of endometrial stromal cell pathology. METTL3 overexpression inhibits proliferation, invasion, and migration of ectopic endometrial stromal cells by enhancing m6A modification and stabilizing WIF1 mRNA, a negative regulator of Wnt signaling that restrains stromal cell growth and mobility. The study identifies a regulatory axis where elevated miR-21-5p in endometriosis directly binds and downregulates METTL3, subsequently reducing WIF1 expression and unleashing stromal cell proliferation and invasive behaviors. Conversely, miR-21-5p downregulation restores METTL3 levels and WIF1 expression, suppressing pathogenic stromal cell functions. These findings establish m6A-mediated gene regulation as a critical mechanism controlling endometrial stromal cell behavior in endometriosis and suggest the miR-21-5p/METTL3/WIF1 axis as a potential therapeutic target.

title: Exosomal CLIC1 released by CLL promotes HUVECs angiogenesis by regulating ITGβ1‐MAPK/ERK axis
journal: The Kaohsiung Journal of Medical Sciences
summary:
# Summary

Chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL) cells release exosomes enriched with CLIC1 protein at elevated levels compared to healthy individuals, which are subsequently transferred into and accumulate within human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs). Upon uptake, exosomal CLIC1 promotes multiple angiogenic phenotypes in HUVECs, including enhanced proliferation, migration, invasion, and new vessel formation through upregulation of the integrin β1 (ITGβ1) receptor. The pro-angiogenic effects of exosomal CLIC1 depend critically on ITGβ1 activation of the MAPK/ERK signaling cascade, which subsequently increases expression of angiogenesis-related proteins VEGF and MMP-9. Pharmacological inhibition of MAPK/ERK signaling with PD98059 effectively blocks these CLIC1-mediated angiogenic responses without affecting CLIC1 or ITGβ1 expression levels directly. These findings identify the exosomal CLIC1/ITGβ1/MAPK/ERK axis as a key mechanism by which CLL tumors promote pathological angiogenesis and suggest that targeting this pathway could potentially limit tumor-associated vascular development in leukemia patients.

title: TIAM2S Operates Multifaced Talents to Alleviate Radiosensitivity, Restrict Apoptosis, Provoke Cell Propagation, and Escalate Cell Migration for Aggravating Radioresistance-Intensified Cervical Cancer Progression
summary: This study demonstrates that TIAM2S (short-form T-cell lymphoma invasion and metastasis 2) is a critical driver of radioresistance in cervical cancer, operating through multiple pro-survival mechanisms. TIAM2S is substantially elevated in radioresistant cervical cancer cell lines (HeLa, CaSki, and C33A) and directly promotes radioresistance by suppressing apoptosis, enhancing cell proliferation and migration, and protecting cells from radiation-induced death. Mechanistically, TIAM2S acts as an anti-apoptotic factor that restricts cell death while simultaneously promoting aggressive cellular behaviors including migration and metastasis, as evidenced by increased lung localization in radioresistant xenograft models. Conversely, TIAM2S suppression restores radiosensitivity, enhances apoptosis in a dose-dependent manner, and reduces cell motility and metastatic potential. The therapeutic significance is underscored by findings that combined TIAM2S inhibition and conventional radiotherapy effectively prevents both tumor growth and metastasis, suggesting that targeting TIAM2S represents an innovative strategy to overcome radiotherapy resistance in cervical cancer patients.

title: Endometriosis and Cytoskeletal Remodeling: The Functional Role of Actin-Binding Proteins
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic, estrogen-dependent disorder characterized by aberrant cytoskeletal remodeling driven by multiple signaling pathways and actin-binding protein dysregulation. Growth factors including TGF-β and EGF induce epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) through activation of Wnt, Notch, and other signaling cascades, leading to transcriptional changes involving Snail, ZEB, SMAD, and Twist that reduce E-cadherin while elevating N-cadherin, vimentin, and fibronectin—hallmarks of ectopic endometrial invasiveness. Key actin-binding proteins including ACTN1, CNN1, CFL1, EZR, and MSN are significantly upregulated in ectopic and eutopic endometrium, where they regulate stress fiber formation, cell migration, and invasion through phosphorylation cascades mediated by kinases such as FAK, LIMK1, PKCι, and TESK1. The estrogen-dependent pathway particularly regulates CFL1 phosphorylation via LIMK1-mediated mechanisms, while EZR activation promotes EMT and is phosphorylated by PKCι in an S1PR3-dependent manner. Additionally, endometriotic stromal cell-derived exosomes containing elevated MSN levels promote migration in normal stromal cells, suggesting a paracrine mechanism of disease propagation. Together, these cytoskeletal remodeling events facilitate the pathological migration and invasion of endometrial cells to ectopic sites through coordinated signaling involving Wnt/β-catenin, NF-κB, MAPK/ERK, PI3K/Akt/mTOR, and Rho/

title: Crip2 affects vascular development by fine-tuning endothelial cell aggregation and proliferation
journal: Cellular and Molecular Life Sciences: CMLS
summary:
# Summary

Crip2 is a critical regulator of vascular development that functions by fine-tuning endothelial cell behavior through interactions with cytoskeletal proteins and transcriptional machinery. Crip2 localizes to the cytoskeleton and cell-cell junctions where it interacts with the intermediate filament proteins KRT8 and VIM, as well as the transcription factor SRF, to maintain proper endothelial cell aggregation and proliferation. Loss of Crip2 disrupts the VEGFA/CDC42 signaling pathway and impairs the PDGF/JAK/STAT cascade, resulting in reduced endothelial cell migration, enhanced adhesion, and decreased proliferation, ultimately causing hypoplasia of major vascular structures including the posterior cardinal vein and caudal vein plexuses. These defects in endothelial cell migration and proliferation lead to aberrant cell aggregation and vascular abnormalities that can contribute to arteriovenous fistulas and other circulatory disorders. The findings demonstrate that Crip2-mediated regulation of cytoskeletal dynamics and growth signaling pathways is essential for proper vascular morphogenesis and endothelial cell homeostasis during development.

title: Lactylation: a promising therapeutic target in ischemia-reperfusion injury management
summary:
# Summary

Lactylation, a recently discovered post-translational modification induced by lactic acid accumulation, emerges as a critical regulatory mechanism in ischemia-reperfusion injury (IRI) and multiple pathological conditions. During IRI triggered by clinical scenarios such as sepsis, organ transplantation, myocardial infarction, and stroke, lactate accumulates through anaerobic glycolysis, serving not only as an energy substrate but also as a signaling molecule that drives lactylation of histone proteins at sites including H3K9, H3K18, and H4K5, as well as nonhistone proteins like HMGB1. Lactate and its resulting lactylation modify cellular processes through multiple mechanisms including modulation of immune cell behavior (macrophages and T cells), regulation of signaling pathways (NF-κB, HIF1), and epigenetic remodeling that controls gene expression such as Arg1. The pathological consequences of lactate accumulation during IRI include intracellular pH decrease, calcium overload, and enhanced inflammatory responses, while lactate simultaneously induces hypoxic responses and cell proliferation through both metabolic and signaling functions. Targeting lactylation represents a promising therapeutic strategy for managing IRI and related diseases including cancer, sepsis, and neurodegenerative conditions, leveraging the central role of lactate-driven post-translational modifications in disease pathogenesis.

title: CXCL14 regulates ovarian endometriosis progression by targeting PCNA
summary: This study identifies CXCL14 as a key regulator of ovarian endometriosis progression through direct interaction with PCNA. CXCL14 is highly elevated in ovarian endometriosis and positively correlates with increased proliferation and migration of endometrial stromal cells (ESCs). Mechanistically, CXCL14 protects PCNA from ubiquitin-mediated degradation, thereby maintaining PCNA levels required for promoting cell proliferation. When CXCL14 is silenced, PCNA undergoes enhanced ubiquitination and subsequent degradation, which suppresses ESC proliferative capacity and inhibits endometriosis progression. PCNA overexpression can partially rescue the inhibitory effects of CXCL14 silencing, confirming PCNA as a critical downstream effector. These findings suggest that targeting the CXCL14-PCNA axis may represent a therapeutic strategy for managing ovarian endometriosis.

title: Analysis of MMP‐7 and TIMP‐2 gene polymorphisms in coronary artery disease and myocardial infarction: A Turkish case‐control study
journal: The Kaohsiung Journal of Medical Sciences
summary: This Turkish case-control study investigated the role of matrix metalloproteinase (MMP) and tissue inhibitor of metalloproteinase (TIMP) gene polymorphisms in coronary artery disease and myocardial infarction. Matrix metalloproteinases, which are responsible for extracellular matrix degradation, contribute significantly to tissue remodeling and acute coronary syndrome, while TIMPs serve as their natural inhibitors to maintain the critical MMP-TIMP balance necessary for myocardial structure preservation. The study identified the MMP-7 C-153T polymorphism, particularly the T-allele, as a notable risk factor for myocardial infarction in coronary artery disease patients, possibly through increased transcriptional activity and elevated MMP-7 expression in macrophages and cardiomyocytes following myocardial injury. However, the MMP-7 A-181G polymorphism and TIMP-2 G-418C polymorphism showed no significant associations with coronary artery disease or myocardial infarction in this Turkish population, suggesting population-specific genetic influences. These findings support the established role of altered MMP-TIMP balance in post-MI left ventricular remodeling, and highlight the complex relationship between functional single nucleotide polymorphisms in matrix remodeling genes and cardiovascular disease susceptibility.

title: Targeting Metastasis in Head and Neck Squamous Cell Carcinoma Using Follistatin mRNA Lipid Nanoparticles
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that activin A, a transforming growth factor-beta superfamily member, drives head and neck squamous cell carcinoma progression through multiple mechanisms including tumor growth, metastatic spread, angiogenesis, and immune evasion, while also promoting cancer-associated cachexia through muscle wasting and inflammatory cytokine upregulation. The researchers developed follistatin mRNA lipid nanoparticles (Fst mRNA LNPs) to antagonize activin A, leveraging follistatin's endogenous inhibitory function. In a murine HPV-positive HNSCC model, Fst mRNA LNPs successfully downregulated serum activin A levels by 2.5-fold, reduced tumor burden threefold, suppressed metastatic spread, and preserved muscle and adipose tissue integrity while maintaining favorable tolerability with minimal systemic reactogenicity. The therapeutic benefit correlated with decreased activin A signaling, as follistatin overexpression reduced MLM3 cancer cell migration and invasive characteristics in vitro. This lipid nanoparticle-based approach offers a promising strategy for targeting activin A-driven malignancies while mitigating cancer-associated cachexia.

title: Identification and analysis of oxidative stress-related genes in endometriosis
summary: This study identifies oxidative stress-related genes as key drivers of endometriosis pathogenesis through multiple signaling cascades. Excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) activates the NF-κB signaling pathway, which upregulates adhesion molecules (ICAM-1) and pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-8 and TGF-β), promoting lesion establishment and inflammation. Additionally, ROS stimulates the MAPK/ERK pathway through superoxide dismutase-mediated signaling, leading to ERK1/2 phosphorylation that increases matrix metalloproteinases (MMP-2 and MMP-9), facilitating extracellular matrix degradation and ectopic endometrial invasion. The study also identifies four critical genes—NR3C1, ENO2, NGF, and CYP17A1—as dysregulated in ectopic endometrium, with elevated expression of NR3C1, ENO2, and NGF linked to epithelial-mesenchymal transition, cell cycle progression, and metabolic reprogramming through E2F targets and KRAS signaling. Notably, CYP17A1 downregulation in ectopic lesions disrupts steroid hormone synthesis and immune regulation, while immune profiling reveals accumulation of pro-inflammatory M2 macrophages and plasma cells alongside depletion of protective immune populations including regulatory T cells and natural killer cells, collectively creating an oxidatively stressed microenvironment that perpetuates endometriosis progression.

title: Down Expression of Zyxin is Associated with Down Expression of p53 in Colorectal Cancer
journal: International Journal of Molecular and Cellular Medicine
summary: This study demonstrates that Zyxin (ZYX) and p53 protein expression are significantly reduced in colorectal cancer tumor tissues compared to non-cancerous tissues, with a strong positive correlation between ZYX and p53 protein levels (r=0.746, P=0.013). The findings suggest that ZYX may regulate p53 stability and function indirectly through the HIPK2-p53 signaling pathway, where ZYX controls HIPK2 stability via Siah-1-mediated mechanisms, and HIPK2 in turn phosphorylates and stabilizes p53 to enhance its pro-apoptotic effects. ZYX appears to function as a tumor suppressor in colorectal cancer by preventing cell migration, proliferation, and invasion, roles consistent with its involvement in transcriptional regulation and cellular differentiation. The coordinated downregulation of both ZYX and p53 in cancer tissues implicates their functional relationship in colorectal cancer pathogenesis, suggesting that loss of ZYX contributes to tumor development by reducing p53-mediated tumor suppression. These findings propose ZYX as a potential therapeutic target for restoring p53-dependent apoptotic pathways in colorectal cancer.

title: The effects of coagulation factors on the risk of Alzheimer’s disease: A Mendelian randomization study
summary:
# Summary

This Mendelian randomization study identifies specific coagulation factors as causal contributors to Alzheimer's disease risk. Elevated levels of Protein C, coagulation factor X, and activated partial thromboplastin time (aPTT), along with decreased coagulation factor XI, demonstrate statistically significant causal associations with AD in both UK Biobank and IGAP datasets. The study proposes mechanistic pathways whereby coagulation dysfunction promotes neurodegeneration through multiple routes: microvascular injury and cerebral microbleeds, amyloid-beta deposition, and neuroinflammatory responses. Protein C exerts neuroprotective effects through its anti-inflammatory properties, and soluble endothelial protein C receptor inhibits Protein C activation in AD patients, potentially explaining elevated circulating Protein C levels. Notably, activated Protein C treatment improves cognitive function and reduces amyloid-beta burden in transgenic AD models, suggesting therapeutic potential. These findings establish coagulation system dysregulation as a key pathogenic mechanism in Alzheimer's disease and highlight potential targets for intervention.

title: Association between TGF-β1 and β-catenin expression in the vaginal wall of patients with pelvic organ prolapse
summary:
# Summary

This study investigates the molecular mechanisms underlying pelvic organ prolapse (POP) by examining the relationship between TGF-β1 and β-catenin signaling in vaginal wall tissue. The researchers found significantly reduced levels of TGF-β1, β-catenin, and collagen I (COL1A1) in POP patients compared to controls, while matrix metalloproteinase-2 (MMP2) was elevated and its inhibitor TIMP2 was reduced. TGF-β1 activates the Wnt/β-catenin signaling pathway by suppressing DKK1, which promotes collagen synthesis and prevents collagen degradation through upregulation of TIMP2 and downregulation of MMP2. The dysregulation of these pathways in POP results in reduced collagen synthesis, increased collagen decomposition, sparse and disorganized collagen fibers, and elevated apoptosis in the vaginal wall. These findings suggest that impaired TGF-β1/Wnt-β-catenin signaling leads to extracellular matrix remodeling and structural failure in pelvic floor tissues, providing insight into potential therapeutic targets for POP treatment.

title: Selective targeting of genes regulated by zinc finger proteins in endometriosis and endometrioid adenocarcinoma by zinc niflumato complex with neocuproine
summary: Endometriosis is characterized by uncontrolled angiogenesis driven by hypoxia-inflammatory conditions, where zinc finger proteins regulate key pro-angiogenic factors including HIF-1α, VEGF-A, TGF-β1, and COX2. The authors demonstrate that a novel zinc niflumato complex [Zn(neo)(nif)2] selectively targets zinc finger protein-regulated genes by intercalating into their target DNA sequences, resulting in downregulation of HIF-1α, VEGF-A, TGF-β1, and COX2 while simultaneously reducing reactive oxygen species formation and mitochondrial dysfunction. Treatment with [Zn(neo)(nif)2] upregulates the antioxidant defense system through increased expression of Nrf2, GPx1, and SOD1 antioxidant enzymes, while also promoting vascular stabilization via ANG1 upregulation. These multifaceted effects indicate that [Zn(neo)(nif)2] addresses the pathological angiogenesis, oxidative stress, and inflammatory signaling underlying endometriosis and its progression toward endometrioid adenocarcinoma. This approach represents a promising therapeutic strategy that combines NSAID-like activity with selective gene regulation to suppress pro-angiogenic pathways while enhancing endogenous antioxidant defenses.

title: Rediscovery of PHI-1/PPP1R14B: Emerging Roles of Cellular PP1 Signaling Mediated by the PPP1R14B Gene Product in Multiple Cancers and Beyond
summary: PHI-1, encoded by the PPP1R14B gene, is a phosphorylation-dependent inhibitor of protein phosphatase-1 (PP1) that regulates multiple cellular processes including smooth muscle contraction, cell motility, apoptosis, and metabolic regulation. PKC, ROCK, and integrin-linked kinase phosphorylate PHI-1 at threonine 57, enabling it to inhibit myosin phosphatase and PP1 activity in response to agonists like angiotensin II and thrombin. PHI-1 is ubiquitously expressed across tissues and localizes to juxtamembrane regions and the trailing edges of migrating cells, where it controls Raf-1 proteostasis through PP1 modulation, with PHI-1 knockdown resulting in increased ERK1/2 phosphorylation. Dysregulation of PHI-1 is associated with multiple cancers including bladder urothelial carcinoma, endometrial carcinoma, and triple-negative breast cancer, with PPP1R14B gene amplification linked to poor survival outcomes. PHI-1 expression is regulated by competing endogenous RNA mechanisms involving miR-137 and miR-194, and the related lncRNA PPP1R14B-AS enhances mitochondrial respiration and contributes to cancer progression and ferroptosis-related signatures. Additionally, PHI-1 undergoes lysine lactylation at Lys140, a modification elevated in cervical cancer and associated with poor prognosis.

title: 5-Aminolaevulinic Acid-Mediated Photodynamic Therapy Combined with Tirapazamine Enhances Efficacy in Ovarian Cancer
summary: This study demonstrates that combining 5-aminolaevulinic acid-mediated photodynamic therapy (5-ALA-PDT) with tirapazamine (TPZ) produces synergistic anticancer effects in ovarian cancer by exploiting the hypoxic tumor microenvironment. 5-ALA is metabolized into the photosensitizer protoporphyrin IX, which generates reactive oxygen species upon light activation to trigger apoptosis, while simultaneously creating acute hypoxia within tumor tissues. TPZ, which is selectively activated under hypoxic conditions, becomes metabolized into reactive free radicals that induce DNA strand breaks and cell death—a mechanism that is further enhanced by PDT-induced hypoxia. The combination therapy suppresses tumor growth through multiple pathways: it increases pro-apoptotic markers (Bax and Cleaved Caspase-3) while decreasing anti-apoptotic Bcl2 expression, and critically inhibits the HIF-1α/VEGF axis and PI3K/Akt/mTOR signaling pathway that normally promote tumor survival and proliferation. In vivo experiments confirmed substantial tumor necrosis and growth inhibition with combination treatment beginning at day 12 post-inoculation, with significantly greater efficacy than either agent alone.
