Additionally, FABP4 involvement in endometriosis pathogenesis suggests shared metabolic mechanisms between endometrial disease and ovarian malignancy, potentially explaining obesity-associated risk for epithelial ovarian carcinoma.

title: NAC1/ACOX2 Axis as a Novel Therapeutic Target for Endometriosis-Related Ovarian Neoplasms
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that the NAC1/ACOX2 axis drives endometriosis-related ovarian neoplasms (ERONs), particularly ovarian clear cell carcinoma, through metabolic reprogramming. NAC1, a transcription factor overexpressed in ERONs, directly represses ACOX2 expression by binding to the proximal CATG6 site in the ACOX2 promoter, shifting cellular metabolism away from fatty acid oxidation toward anabolic processes including enhanced fat synthesis via FASN upregulation. High NAC1 expression combined with low ACOX2 expression associates with worse patient prognosis, shorter progression-free and overall survival, and increased chemotherapy resistance. Notably, NAC1 knockdown restores chemotherapy sensitivity and induces apoptosis in OCCC cells while simultaneously increasing ACOX2 expression, identifying the NAC1/ACOX2 axis as a promising therapeutic target for ERONs, a class of tumors arising from benign endometriotic lesions. This work establishes a mechanistic link between metabolic dysfunction and endometriosis-associated malignant transformation.

title: Vacuolar Proteases of Candida auris from Clades III and IV and Their Relationship with Autophagy
summary: This study identifies and characterizes eight vacuolar protease genes (PEP4, PRB1, PRC1, ATG42, CPS, LAP4, APE3, and DAP2) in Candida auris clades III and IV, demonstrating their conservation as orthologs of well-characterized Saccharomyces cerevisiae vacuolar peptidases. Vacuolar proteases play a critical role in autophagy by degrading senescent proteins and non-functional organelles within autophagic bodies, enabling fungal survival under adverse conditions such as nutritional stress and starvation. Gene expression and proteolytic activity of these enzymes increase significantly under nutritional stress and rapamycin treatment, which inhibits the TOR pathway and triggers autophagy through upregulation of ATG8 and formation of autophagic bodies. The findings establish vacuolar proteases as essential mediators of C. auris autophagy and survival mechanisms, contributing to the pathogen's virulence alongside other factors including adhesins and lipases. These results provide molecular insights into how this multidrug-resistant, high-mortality fungal pathogen adapts to hostile environments, potentially informing therapeutic strategies targeting autophagy-dependent survival pathways.

title: Single-cell and spatial transcriptome analyses reveal tumor heterogeneity and immune remodeling involved in pituitary neuroendocrine tumor progression
summary:
# Summary

This single-cell and spatial transcriptome study reveals distinct tumor heterogeneity and immune remodeling in pituitary neuroendocrine tumors, with critical implications for understanding aggressive progression. The research identifies multiple tumor subpopulations characterized by different molecular signatures: aggressive ACTH-secreting tumors show elevated p53-mediated proliferation and express genes like CASC5, GTSE1, ADCYAP1, and ANXA1 that drive aggressive behavior, while stem-like tumor cells are marked by upregulation of SOX4, LY6H, and WFDC2. Mechanistically, SPP1-expressing tumor-associated macrophages promote tumor invasion through the ITGAV/ITGB1 signaling pathway, establishing a critical immune-stromal interaction in the microenvironment. Notably, the TP53 Arg248Gln hotspot mutation enhances tumor cell motility and invasive potential, and patients with both p53 positivity and high Ki67 indices experience significantly shorter recurrence-free intervals. The study also identifies distinct gene signatures separating regular from aggressive tumors, with genes like GAL and ZBTB49 characterizing indolent disease while proliferation markers including PTTG1, CENPF, and TOP2A associate with aggressive phenotypes.

title: Loss of GATA2 promotes invasion and predicts cancer recurrence and survival in uterine serous carcinoma
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that GATA2 expression serves as a critical predictor of clinical outcomes in uterine serous carcinoma (USC), with high GATA2 levels strongly associated with improved recurrence-free and overall survival, particularly in early-stage disease. Loss of GATA2 expression promotes USC invasion and metastasis through multiple mechanisms, including reduced expression of the SIN3B histone deacetylase complex component, which normally enforces cellular differentiation and cell cycle control. GATA2 functions in an autoregulatory feedback loop with progesterone receptor and binds to multiple transcriptional motifs to regulate invasion-suppressive programs. Notably, GATA2 status independently predicts survival in non-metastatic USC but has no prognostic value in metastatic disease, and GATA2 expression does not affect chemotherapy sensitivity. The findings suggest that GATA2 immunohistochemistry could identify approximately one-third of stage I USC patients with exceptionally favorable prognosis (100% five-year recurrence-free survival), potentially allowing treatment de-escalation in this low-risk subgroup.

title: Hippo signaling in mammalian reproduction
summary: The Hippo signaling pathway is a conserved regulator of mammalian reproduction that functions through multiple upstream signals including extracellular ligands, steroids, and mechanical cues to control the phosphorylation and nuclear localization of YAP1 and WWTR1 transcriptional co-factors. In the ovary, active Hippo signaling prevents premature follicle activation and maintains granulosa cell identity, while dysregulation is associated with polycystic ovarian syndrome and primary ovarian insufficiency. In the uterus, elevated YAP1 in endometrial stem cells from women with endometriosis promotes stromal cell proliferation while simultaneously suppressing autophagy through the YAP1/TEAD1 complex, contributing to the pathogenic metabolic reprogramming characteristic of the disease. During early embryonic development, YAP1 and WWTR1 are required for both zygotic genome activation and trophoblast cell invasiveness, while later pregnancy complications including preeclampsia and recurrent loss are linked to Hippo pathway dysfunction. In males, Hippo signaling is essential for spermatogenesis and testis development through regulation of Sertoli and Leydig cell identity. These findings establish Hippo signaling as a critical hub integrating mechanical and hormonal signals to coordinate reproductive cell fate decisions and metabolic adaptation across multiple reproductive tissues.

title: Mechanistic Exploration of Shugan Jianpi Formula for Treating Triple-Negative Breast Cancer Under Chronic Stress: A Network Pharmacology-Guided Experimental Study
summary:
# Summary

This study investigated the pharmacological mechanisms of Shugan Jianpi Formula (SGJPF), a traditional Chinese medicine formulation derived from Xiaoyao Powder, in treating triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) under chronic psychological stress. SGJPF contains over 800 identified compounds including flavonoids, polyphenols, and saponins, with paeoniflorin identified as a primary active component. In vitro experiments demonstrated that SGJPF effectively inhibited TNBC cell proliferation, migration, and invasion while promoting apoptosis in stress-stimulated MDA-MB-231 and SUM159PT cells, with molecular docking revealing that paeoniflorin binds to key targets SRC, MAPK1 (ERK), and STAT3. Both in vitro and in vivo studies confirmed that SGJPF exerts its therapeutic effects through modulation of the SRC/ERK/STAT3 signaling axis, a pathway central to TNBC pathogenesis. In a chronic stress-induced TNBC mouse model, SGJPF significantly alleviated tumor progression and reduced tumor growth, supporting its therapeutic potential as a stress-responsive treatment strategy for TNBC.

title: MBD2 promotes B cell differentiation and BCR signaling in systemic lupus erythematosus by regulating the LEF-1-PTEN-PI3K axis
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that MBD2, a methyl-CpG-binding protein highly expressed in B cells from systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) patients, promotes disease pathogenesis through the LEF-1-PTEN-PI3K axis. MBD2 binds methylated CpG sites in the Lef-1 promoter (induced by IFN-α) to suppress its transcription, leading to reduced PTEN expression and consequently enhanced PI3K-Akt-mTOR signaling. This mechanism drives aberrant B cell differentiation, germinal center formation, and increased production of pathogenic autoantibodies. B cell-specific MBD2 knockout significantly attenuated lupus-like symptoms, including reduced germinal center responses, anti-dsDNA antibodies, splenomegaly, and renal injury. The findings establish MBD2 as a critical epigenetic regulator linking DNA methylation to BCR signaling dysregulation in SLE, suggesting it represents a potential therapeutic target for treating this autoimmune disease.

title: Targeting the MDK/c‐Myc complex to overcome temozolomide resistance in glioma
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies midkine (MDK) as a critical driver of temozolomide (TMZ) resistance in glioblastoma through its interaction with the c-Myc oncogene. MDK, highly expressed in gliomas and associated with poor prognosis, promotes tumor cell proliferation, invasion, metastasis, and angiogenesis while conferring resistance to standard chemotherapy. The MDK/c-Myc complex activates pro-survival signaling pathways and epithelial-mesenchymal transition via enhanced c-Myc ubiquitination, which drives downstream cell cycle progression and metabolic reprogramming. The authors developed ACT001, a blood-brain barrier-penetrant small molecule that disrupts the MDK/c-Myc interaction and promotes c-Myc degradation through enhanced ubiquitination. ACT001 sensitizes resistant glioma cells to TMZ, suppresses cell proliferation, and synergistically inhibits tumor growth when combined with TMZ, while simultaneously controlling the Wnt/β-catenin signaling pathway. This work establishes the MDK/c-Myc axis as a therapeutic target for overcoming TMZ resistance in glioblastoma patients.

title: Post-translational modifications of collagen and its related diseases in metabolic pathways
summary:
# Article Summary

Collagen, the most abundant structural protein in the extracellular matrix, maintains tissue integrity across multiple organ systems and is essential for forming the extracellular scaffold. Post-translational modifications (PTMs) are critical regulatory steps in collagen metabolism, and their aberrant regulation drives pathological conditions including hepatic, renal, cardiac, pulmonary, and cutaneous fibrosis, as well as keloid formation. Multiple signaling pathways—including TGF-β/Smad, PI3K/AKT, MAPK, Wnt, NF-κB, integrin, and JAK/STAT—coordinately regulate collagen metabolism, with TGF-β playing a particularly central role by stimulating collagen mRNA expression, inhibiting collagenase secretion, enhancing proline hydroxylation, and suppressing intracellular degradation. PTM abnormalities trigger aberrant fibroblast activation, leading to excessive collagen accumulation in various tissues and contributing to fibrotic disease pathogenesis. Additionally, PTM changes in collagen and its metabolic pathways regulate tumor cell behavior within the microenvironment and are closely associated with epithelial-mesenchymal transition, linking collagen metabolism to both fibrotic and malignant disease.

title: Research note: Unveiling the impact of ovotransferrin on chicken primordial germ cells biological processes
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that ovotransferrin, an iron-transport protein abundant in chicken serum, significantly enhances chicken primordial germ cell (PGC) proliferation, adhesion, and survival at an optimal concentration of 0.5 mg/mL. Ovotransferrin promotes PGC proliferation by increasing PCNA positivity and S-phase cell cycle progression while simultaneously suppressing apoptosis through downregulation of pro-apoptotic BCL-2 family members (Bim and Bad) and upregulation of anti-apoptotic BCL-2. The protein exerts robust antioxidant protection by reducing reactive oxygen species (ROS) and malondialdehyde (MDA) levels while enhancing superoxide dismutase (SOD) activity and glutathione (GSH) levels, thereby increasing cellular tolerance to oxidative stress. Additionally, ovotransferrin upregulates tight junction proteins ZO-1, Occludin, and JAM, strengthening cell adhesion and barrier function in PGCs. These findings establish ovotransferrin as a critical serum component that supports germ cell development through coordinated promotion of proliferation, inhibition of apoptosis, and enhancement of antioxidant capacity—mechanisms relevant to understanding cellular survival in reproductive tissues.

title: Deciphering Deleterious nsSNPs in MUC16's SEA Domain: Structural and Functional Implications in Cancer Metastasis via Computational Analysis
journal: Journal of Cellular and Molecular Medicine
summary:
# Summary

This computational study investigates the structural and functional consequences of nonsynonymous single nucleotide polymorphisms (nsSNPs) in MUC16's SEA5 domain, a mucin protein overexpressed across multiple cancer types including ovarian, breast, endometrial, and pancreatic cancers. MUC16 overexpression promotes cancer cell differentiation, proliferation, invasion, and metastasis, partly through interaction with JAK2, which phosphorylates STAT3 to activate c-Jun signaling. Molecular dynamics simulations reveal that four deleterious variants located in conserved regions of the SEA5 domain—L151P, Y144N, C111Y, and D108Y—significantly alter protein properties; notably, Y144N induces greater structural flexibility, lower solvent-accessible surface area, and increased hydrogen bonding deviations, while also increasing susceptibility to binding with mesothelin, a functional partner in cancer development. These findings suggest that certain MUC16 variants may enhance metastatic potential through altered protein stability and structural dynamics. The study also highlights MUC16's association with endometriosis and other mucosal conditions, indicating broader pathophysiological relevance beyond malignancy.

title: Chinese medicine targets cellular autophagy against cardiovascular diseases: research progress and future prospects
summary:
# Summary

Autophagy plays a critical protective role in cardiovascular disease, functioning as a defense mechanism for cardiomyocytes while abnormal autophagy contributes to myocardial dysfunction and abnormal metabolism. The process is tightly regulated by key signaling pathways including the AMPK/mTOR axis and the PI3K/Akt pathway, with mTOR suppressing autophagy while AMPK and GSK-3β activate it through regulation of upstream complexes like ULK1/Atg1 and Beclin-1. Traditional Chinese Medicine compounds have emerged as promising therapeutic agents that modulate cardiovascular disease through autophagy regulation, with exemplars like chikusetsusaponin IVa activating autophagy via the AMPK/mTOR/ULK1 axis. In diabetic cardiomyopathy and myocardial ischemia/reperfusion injury, dysregulated autophagy—manifested as either excessive inhibition or pathological activation—exacerbates cardiac damage by increasing apoptosis, necrosis, and reactive oxygen species accumulation. These findings highlight autophagy modulation as a central therapeutic target for cardiovascular diseases and establish the mechanistic basis for developing TCM-derived interventions that restore proper autophagic flux.

title: YAP, TAZ, and Hippo-Dysregulating Fusion Proteins in Cancer
summary:
# Summary

This review examines how dysregulation of the Hippo pathway transcriptional effectors YAP and TAZ drives cancer through gene fusions and pathway alterations. The Hippo pathway normally constrains YAP and TAZ activity through a phosphorylation cascade initiated by MST1/MST2 kinases, which activate LATS1/LATS2 to phosphorylate YAP and TAZ, sequestering them in the cytoplasm via 14-3-3 binding; when Hippo signaling is suppressed, unphosphorylated YAP and TAZ translocate to the nucleus and activate TEAD transcription factors to promote proliferation and cancer stem cell traits. Loss of NF2, which spatially organizes the kinase cascade, represents a major mechanism of YAP/TAZ hyperactivation in cancer. Recurrent gene fusions involving YAP1 or WWTR1 (encoding TAZ) occur across diverse tumor types including epithelioid hemangioendothelioma, ependymomas, meningiomas, and poromas, where fusion partners retain TEAD-binding domains while escaping normal Hippo regulation. Activated YAP/TAZ promotes hepatocellular carcinoma, cholangiocarcinoma, rhabdomyosarcoma, and uveal melanoma in mouse models and enables immune evasion by upregulating PD-L1, establishing these pathway components as major cancer drivers and promising therapeutic targets.

title: Mechanism of action of IHH in ameliorating thin endometrium
journal: The Journal of Reproduction and Development
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that Indian hedgehog (IHH) ameliorates ethanol-induced thin endometrium and restores fertility by activating the Hedgehog signaling pathway. IHH overexpression increased endometrial thickness and promoted both glandular and vascular regeneration while reducing endometrial fibrosis through suppression of the fibroblast marker α-SMA. The therapeutic effects of IHH involved upregulation of key markers including angiogenic protein vWF, proliferation markers PCNA and vim, and epithelial integrity markers CK19 and MUC-1, which support endothelial cell proliferation, cell proliferation, and epithelial cell protection respectively. IHH activated the canonical Hedgehog signaling cascade by upregulating downstream effectors SMO, GLI1, and GLI3, ultimately improving fertility as evidenced by increased gestational sacs in treated animals. These findings establish IHH as a promising therapeutic approach for restoring endometrial function and fertility in patients with thin endometrium, a condition associated with poor pregnancy outcomes and high miscarriage rates.

title: PBX3-HMGCR Axis Promotes Hepatocellular Carcinoma Progression Through Enhancing De Novo Cholesterol Biosynthesis
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies PBX3 as a critical oncogenic transcription factor that promotes hepatocellular carcinoma progression through direct activation of HMGCR-mediated cholesterol biosynthesis. PBX3 directly binds to the HMGCR promoter and upregulates this rate-limiting cholesterol synthesis enzyme, driving de novo lipid metabolic reprogramming that fuels tumor cell proliferation and growth. PBX3 expression is significantly elevated in HCC tissue and positively correlates with poor clinical prognosis, while PBX3 knockdown suppresses HCC cell proliferation, lipid droplet accumulation, and xenograft tumor growth—effects that can be partially rescued by HMGCR overexpression or cholesterol supplementation. Beyond lipid metabolism, PBX3 promotes tumorigenesis through multiple additional mechanisms including activation of MAPK/ERK and PI3K/Akt signaling, suppression of p53, upregulation of anti-apoptotic Bcl-2, and enhanced expression of matrix metalloproteinases. These findings establish the PBX3-HMGCR axis as a central metabolic driver of HCC progression and suggest therapeutic potential for targeting cholesterol biosynthesis in this disease.

title: HSF1 Activation Mechanisms, Disease Roles, and Small Molecule Therapeutics
summary:
# Summary

Heat shock factor 1 (HSF1) is a master transcriptional regulator of the heat shock response that activates heat shock proteins and other cytoprotective genes by binding to heat shock elements in DNA. HSF1 activity is tightly controlled by a complex network of post-translational modifications and protein interactions: under normal conditions, HSP90, HSP70, and TRiC maintain HSF1 in an inactive cytoplasmic state, while phosphorylation at Ser230 and Ser326 promotes activation, and phosphorylation at Ser303 and Ser307 attenuates activity. Additionally, acetylation, SUMOylation, and ubiquitination by E3 ligases like FBXW7 and NEDD4 regulate HSF1 stability and function, while deacetylases such as SIRT1 and phosphatases including PP2A enhance HSF1 activity. HSF1 is significantly upregulated across multiple cancer types and promotes tumor progression through diverse mechanisms, including enhancement of protein-folding chaperones, stimulation of the tumor microenvironment in breast cancer, and activation of immunosuppressive pathways via PD-L1 in hepatocellular carcinoma. HSP90 inhibitors represent a therapeutic strategy to activate HSF1 and induce stress-responsive protective pathways, offering potential applications in cancer and other diseases characterized by proteostatic imbalance.

title: The SOX18-OTUB1-YAP1 axis: a new endometriosis target
summary: This study identifies the SOX18-OTUB1-YAP1 signaling axis as a critical regulator of endometriosis pathogenesis. SOX18, a transcription factor significantly upregulated in ectopic endometrial tissues, directly activates OTUB1 transcription by binding its promoter region, which in turn deubiquitinates and stabilizes YAP1 protein, thereby activating the Hippo/YAP1 pathway. This axis promotes ectopic endometrial stromal cell proliferation and anti-apoptosis while driving epithelial-mesenchymal transition, as demonstrated by SOX18 overexpression in Ishikawa cells enhancing proliferation, migration, and invasion alongside characteristic EMT marker changes (decreased E-cadherin and increased N-cadherin/vimentin). In vivo, SOX18 overexpression exacerbated endometriosis in mouse models with notable pathological deterioration, while SOX18 knockdown reversed these cellular phenotypes. These findings establish the SOX18-OTUB1-YAP1 axis as a potential therapeutic target for endometriosis management.

title: Non-hormone replacement therapy to overcome premature ovarian insufficiency: advances in natural products and stem cells targeting autophagy
summary:
# Summary

Premature ovarian insufficiency (POI), a leading cause of female infertility with serious comorbidities including osteoporosis and cardiovascular disease, currently relies on hormone replacement therapy that carries risks of endometrial and breast cancer. This review identifies autophagy as a critical regulatory mechanism in ovarian function, where proper autophagic flux maintains ovarian primordial follicle reserves, supports granulosa cell differentiation, and prevents ovarian aging. Disruption of autophagy through impaired protein complexes (such as Epg5 deficiency), dysregulation of key kinases (ULK1, AMPK, mTOR signaling), or imbalanced transcription factors (WT1, NRF2) directly precipitates POI-like pathology. Novel non-hormonal therapeutic approaches utilizing natural products and mesenchymal stem cells can effectively treat POI by modulating autophagy pathways and enhancing oxidative stress resistance, offering safer alternatives to conventional hormone replacement. The mechanistic understanding of autophagy in oocyte development and ovarian homeostasis thus provides multiple therapeutic targets for developing regenerative medicine strategies to overcome POI.

title: Advances in research regarding epithelial-mesenchymal transition and prostate cancer
journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
summary:
# Summary

Epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) drives prostate cancer progression, metastasis, and therapeutic resistance through coordinated changes in cell adhesion molecules and metabolic reprogramming. The process involves loss of epithelial markers (E-cadherin, β-catenin) and gain of mesenchymal markers (N-cadherin, vimentin) controlled by transcription factors including Snail, ZEB1, and Twist, which are activated by TGF-β, Wnt, and Notch signaling pathways. EMT-associated metabolic rewiring increases glutamine dependency via GLS1 upregulation and enhances aerobic glycolysis through HIF-1α-driven GLUT1 and LDHA expression, with lactate production sustaining mesenchymal phenotypes while suppressing anti-tumor immunity. Epigenetic modifications including CDH1 promoter hypermethylation, H3K27ac enrichment, and long non-coding RNA MALAT1 expression reinforce EMT and couple it with cancer stem cell properties, driving resistance to androgen deprivation therapy and chemotherapy. Targeting components of this pathway—such as EZH2 inhibitors, TGF-β receptor antagonists, and restoration of E-cadherin expression—shows promise in reversing EMT-driven drug resistance and improving therapeutic outcomes.

title: The Impact of Potent Addictive Substances on Angiogenic Behavior: A Comprehensive Review
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive review examines the molecular mechanisms governing sprouting angiogenesis, the formation of new blood vessels critical to both normal physiological processes and pathological conditions including cancer progression, tumor recurrence, and metastasis. Angiogenesis is orchestrated through sequential stages involving endothelial cell proliferation, migration, and invasion, coordinated by multiple signaling pathways including VEGF, Angiopoietins, Wnt, and Notch signaling, along with supporting factors such as PDGF, FGF-2, and inflammatory cytokines (TNF, IL-6). The review highlights how tip and stalk endothelial cells are selected and differentiated through VEGF receptors, Notch ligands (JAGGED1, DLL4), and Neuropilins, which regulate the formation of vessel lumens and subsequent stabilization through pericyte recruitment via ANG-1, PDGF-B, and TGF-β signaling. Notably, the article documents that nicotine, a potent addictive substance, paradoxically enhances angiogenic capacity by increasing VEGF and FGF release through α7-nicotinic acetylcholine receptor signaling, promoting endothelial cell proliferation and reducing apoptosis. These findings suggest that blocking α7-nAChR antagonists can suppress angiogenic responses, offering potential therapeutic strategies for conditions where angiogenesis suppression is beneficial, such as tumor management and wet age-related macular degeneration.

title: Junction Plakoglobin – A Dual-Role Player in Cancer Biology
summary: Junction plakoglobin (JUP) functions as a dual-role player in cancer biology with context-dependent effects on tumor progression. JUP serves as a critical cell adhesion molecule that stabilizes the cytoskeleton through desmosome formation and mediates cadherin-dependent cell-cell adhesion, with its cytoplasmic localization generally associated with anticancer effects including reduced tumor cell growth and inhibition of migration and invasion. Conversely, nuclear JUP promotes carcinogenesis by binding transcription factors like TCF/LEF and p53, activating oncogenic Wnt pathway target genes and cooperating with p53 to enhance 14-3-3σ expression, which regulates the cell cycle. JUP's role becomes tissue and cancer-type specific: it suppresses prostate cancer tumorigenesis while promoting ovarian cancer metastasis and contributing to circulating tumor cell cluster formation. Reduced JUP expression via epigenetic silencing by DNMTs impairs cell adhesion and enhances cancer cell invasion, while loss of JUP correlates with squamous-differentiated endometrial cancer. The subcellular localization and expression level of JUP therefore represent critical determinants of its tumorigenic versus tumor-suppressive functions across different cancer types.

title: ZNF432 suppresses endometrial cancer progression by promoting UPF1 ubiquitination and inducing apoptosis
summary:
# Summary

ZNF432 functions as a tumor suppressor in endometrial cancer, with expression progressively declining as tumor stage advances. ZNF432 exerts its anti-cancer effects through a mechanism involving direct interaction with UPF1, a critical regulator of mRNA stability and nonsense-mediated decay. ZNF432 promotes UPF1 ubiquitination, leading to UPF1 protein degradation and subsequent induction of apoptosis in endometrial cancer cells. Functionally, ZNF432 overexpression inhibits cancer cell proliferation, enhances apoptotic markers (Bax and caspase-3), and suppresses tumor growth in vivo, while reducing anti-apoptotic proteins (Bcl-2) and proliferation markers (PCNA). Conversely, ZNF432 knockdown promotes cell proliferation and survival, and this effect is partially dependent on UPF1, as demonstrated by rescue experiments showing that UPF1 modulation counteracts ZNF432's effects. These findings establish ZNF432 as a potential therapeutic target for endometrial cancer through the ubiquitin-proteasome degradation pathway.

title: Helicobacter Pylori-Induced Apoptosis in Gastric Diseases: Mechanisms, Implications, and Diagnostic Applications
summary:
# Summary

Helicobacter pylori causes gastritis, ulcers, and gastric cancer primarily through apoptosis induction via its major virulence factors, cytotoxin-associated gene A (CagA) and vacuolating cytotoxin A (VacA). These bacterial toxins activate both intrinsic and extrinsic apoptotic pathways: VacA disrupts mitochondrial membrane potential and releases pro-apoptotic BAX proteins, while CagA, once phosphorylated by host kinases, activates death receptor signaling and downstream pathways including MAPK/ERK and NF-κB. H. pylori infection simultaneously elevates reactive oxygen species and pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β and TNF-α) that further promote apoptosis in gastric epithelial cells by upregulating death receptors (Fas, TNFR1, and TRAIL receptors). Additionally, the bacteria manipulate host autophagy through VacA-induced autophagosome formation and CagA-mediated disruption of autophagosome maturation, allowing bacterial persistence while driving chronic pathology. The dual role of apoptosis—serving as both a host defense mechanism and a promoter of pathological cell death—underlies the progression from infection to tissue damage and malignant transformation.

title: Proteostasis signatures in human diseases
summary:
# Summary

The proteostasis network, comprising molecular chaperones, degradation pathways, and regulatory systems, coordinates protein synthesis, folding, trafficking, and degradation to maintain cellular function and organismal health. Dysregulation of this network is associated with diverse human diseases, with distinct proteostasis signatures characterizing different disease classes: cancers show early perturbations primarily in the ubiquitin-proteasome system, neurodegenerative diseases exhibit extensive perturbation of both the ubiquitin-proteasome and autophagy-lysosome pathways alongside unfolded protein response activation, while autoimmune, cardiovascular, endocrine, and reproductive diseases predominantly involve deregulation of extracellular proteostasis. Protein misfolding, damage, and impaired degradation contribute to disease pathogenesis through accumulation of aggregated proteins, as exemplified in Alzheimer's and Parkinson's diseases. This proteostasis-centered framework reveals disease-specific molecular signatures and identifies early biomarkers such as YAP1 in Alzheimer's disease, providing insights into how distinct proteostasis perturbation patterns underlie different disease pathologies.

title: EMMPRIN Correlated with β-CATENIN in Various t Stages of Colorectal Adenocarcinoma
journal: Asian Pacific Journal of Cancer Prevention : APJCP
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that EMMPRIN and β-catenin are significantly correlated across different tumor stages of colorectal adenocarcinoma and work synergistically to promote cancer progression. EMMPRIN activates the canonical Wnt/β-catenin signaling pathway by inhibiting GSK-3β, thereby preventing β-catenin degradation and allowing its nuclear accumulation. Both proteins independently stimulate tumor cell proliferation, migration, and invasion, while together they promote epithelial-mesenchymal transition and metastasis through downregulation of E-cadherin and activation of transcription factors like Snail. EMMPRIN additionally stimulates matrix metalloproteinase secretion (MMP-1, MMP-3, and MMP-9) and hyaluronan production, facilitating basement membrane degradation and supporting tumor growth, angiogenesis, and invasion. β-catenin expression correlates strongly with TNM stage, lymph node metastasis, and tumor size, establishing it as a reliable prognostic marker. These findings suggest that the EMMPRIN/Wnt/β-catenin axis represents a critical mechanism driving colorectal adenocarcinoma progression and invasive behavior.

title: Rational design matrix materials for organoid development and application in biomedicine
summary:
# Summary

This article reviews the rational design of extracellular matrix-inspired biomaterials for organoid development and biomedical applications. Organoids represent a powerful tool for organ development, personalized medicine, disease modeling, and drug screening, with their success dependent on appropriate matrix culture systems. While Matrigel has been the predominant organoid culture substrate, it suffers from batch-to-batch heterogeneity, driving development of engineered alternatives including hydrogels and biomimetic materials that recapitulate native ECM composition and function. The extracellular matrix fundamentally controls cell behavior through multiple mechanisms: biochemical signals via adhesion ligands (collagen, laminin, fibronectin) binding to integrin receptors, physical cues including stiffness and stress relaxation, and structural features such as porosity and fiber alignment that collectively regulate cell proliferation, adhesion, migration, and differentiation. Key design parameters including matrix stiffness, viscoelastic properties, pore structure, and bioactive peptide sequences (RGD, YIGSR) can be precisely tuned to promote desired cellular outcomes, exemplified by applications ranging from cerebral and intestinal organoids to stem cell differentiation and neural regeneration. These rational design approaches enable creation of superior culture platforms that better recapitulate tissue microenvironments and improve organoid quality for both research and clinical applications.

title: Platelet-derived growth factor (PDGF) and its impact in fibroid pathogenesis: A narrative review
summary: Platelet-derived growth factor (PDGF) emerges as a central mediator in uterine fibroid pathogenesis, with markedly elevated expression in fibroid tissue compared to normal myometrium. PDGF exists as multiple isoforms that bind to tyrosine kinase receptors (PDGFR-α and PDGFR-β), activating downstream signaling cascades including PI3K/AKT, RAS/MAPK, and JAK/STAT pathways that collectively promote smooth muscle cell proliferation, fibrotic remodeling, and extracellular matrix deposition through increased collagen and fibronectin production. PDGF signaling also regulates cell migration and survival while conferring resistance to apoptosis, and synergizes with transforming growth factor-beta to amplify fibrogenic effects. The hormone-dependent nature of fibroid growth is underscored by estrogen and progesterone upregulating PDGFR expression and independently stimulating smooth muscle cell proliferation and matrix accumulation. These findings position PDGF dysregulation as a key pathogenic mechanism in fibroids, with broader implications for understanding dysregulated growth in cancers, fibrotic disorders, and vascular diseases.

title: Disruption of the Wnt/β-Catenin and PI3K-AKT-mTOR Crosstalk in Endometrial Stromal Cells: A Case Report of Impaired Decidualization Leading to Recurrent Implantation Failure and Potential Pathway-Specific Therapeutic Interventions
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a patient with recurrent implantation failure caused by disrupted crosstalk between the Wnt/β-catenin and PI3K-AKT-mTOR signaling pathways in endometrial stromal cells. The patient exhibited markedly reduced β-catenin nuclear translocation (only 30% compared to 85% in healthy endometrium), 65% decreased AKT phosphorylation, and 50% reduced mTOR phosphorylation, leading to a 49% reduction in TCF/LEF transcriptional activity. These signaling defects cascaded into impaired decidualization, evidenced by 45% reductions in prolactin and IGFBP-1 expression, decreased integrin αvβ3 adhesion molecules, and 40% reduced Ki-67 cellular proliferation markers. The compromised pathway signaling prevented adequate endometrial receptivity despite normal hormonal levels, demonstrating that implantation failure can result from intracellular signaling defects rather than systemic hormonal dysfunction. The findings suggest that pathway-specific therapeutic interventions targeting Wnt/β-catenin and PI3K-AKT-mTOR restoration may offer novel treatment strategies for patients with unexplained recurrent implantation failure.

title: High-Throughput Drug Screening of Clear Cell Ovarian Cancer Organoids Reveals Vulnerability to Proteasome Inhibitors and Dinaciclib and Identifies AGR2 as a Therapeutic Target
summary: High-throughput drug screening of clear cell ovarian cancer organoids identified proteasome inhibitors (particularly bortezomib and carfilzomib) and the cyclin-dependent kinase inhibitor dinaciclib as potent therapeutic agents, with both drug classes demonstrating significant tumor growth inhibition in vitro and in vivo. These drugs exert their anti-tumor effects through distinct mechanisms: proteasome inhibitors enhance endoplasmic reticulum stress and activate the unfolded protein response (UPR) through multiple pathways (PERK, IRE1α, and XBP1s), while dinaciclib induces a more selective UPR response via PERK and ATF6. The study identified AGR2, an endoplasmic reticulum-resident protein involved in UPR regulation, as a key therapeutic target; AGR2 knockout suppressed cancer cell proliferation, enhanced sensitivity to proteasome inhibitors and platinum agents, and upregulated the DNA helicase SLFN11 to restore platinum sensitivity. Additionally, AGR2 knockout suppressed multiple pro-tumorigenic pathways including Wnt-β-catenin signaling, epithelial-mesenchymal transition, and Notch signaling while triggering enhanced ER stress-induced cell death. These findings suggest that targeting AGR2 or the UPR represents a promising therapeutic strategy for clear cell ovarian cancer, which frequently arises from endometriosis and exhibits intrinsic resistance to conventional platinum-based chemotherapy.

title: Research Progress on the Interaction Between SIRT1 and Mitochondrial Biochemistry in the Aging of the Reproductive System
summary:
# Summary

SIRT1, a highly conserved NAD-dependent deacetylase localized to the nucleus, cytoplasm, and mitochondria, plays a pivotal role in regulating reproductive system aging through its control of mitochondrial function and biogenesis. SIRT1 deficiency leads to increased oxidative stress and cellular damage, while SIRT1 activation counters these effects by deacetylating key proteins including FOXO transcription factors and Nrf2, thereby enhancing cellular stress responses and antioxidant enzyme production. SIRT1 also promotes autophagy through the AMPK-mTOR signaling axis and direct deacetylation of autophagy-related proteins, processes essential for managing the oxidative stress, mitochondrial defects, and telomere shortening that characterize reproductive aging. Additionally, SIRT1 regulates DNA repair and inhibits the pro-aging transcription factor p53, while its subfamily member SIRT4 and mitochondrial sirtuins (SIRT3, SIRT4, SIRT5) regulate energy metabolism and maintain mitochondrial homeostasis. These interconnected mechanisms establish SIRT1 and the broader sirtuin family as central regulators linking mitochondrial biochemistry to the prevention of aging across reproductive and other biological systems.

title: CIRBP Enhances the Function of Yak Cumulus Cells by Activating AMPK/mTOR-Mediated Mitophagy
summary: Cold-inducible RNA-binding protein (CIRBP) enhances yak cumulus cell function through activation of AMPK/mTOR-mediated mitophagy, a selective autophagy pathway targeting damaged mitochondria. Low temperature exposure upregulates CIRBP expression and activates mitophagy, leading to increased synthesis and secretion of estradiol (E2) and progesterone (P4), the key steroid hormones for reproductive function. CIRBP overexpression significantly upregulates mitophagy-related genes including LC3 I/II, BNIP3, Beclin-1, and ATG5, while simultaneously suppressing P62, indicating enhanced mitochondrial turnover and cellular quality control. Furthermore, CIRBP overexpression substantially increases expression of steroid hormone synthesis enzymes (CYP19A1, CYP17A1, STAR) and cumulus-related factors (HAS2, COX2, PTX3), promoting cumulus cell expansion and oocyte maturation. The AMPK/mTOR signaling pathway serves as the central mechanism through which CIRBP executes these effects, as pathway inhibition with doxorubicin hydrochloride blocks CIRBP-induced mitophagy and hormone production. These findings establish CIRBP-regulated mitophagy as a critical mechanism for optimizing cumulus cell metabolic function and reproductive performance in response to environmental stress.

title: Molecular Mechanisms of Cellular Senescence in Age-Related Endometrial Dysfunction
summary:
# Summary

This study elucidates how cellular senescence drives age-related endometrial dysfunction through interconnected signaling pathways that regulate stress responses, metabolic reprogramming, and inflammation. The p53 tumor suppressor acts as a central hub that activates AMPK and suppresses mTOR, promoting energy conservation and limiting senescence; when this protective mechanism fails, accumulated senescent cells secrete pro-inflammatory factors (SASP) that disrupt endometrial receptivity, impair fertility, and promote endometriosis and endometrial cancer. Senescence is triggered by multiple stressors including DNA damage, oxidative stress, telomere shortening, and nutritional stress, while inflammatory cytokines such as IL-17RB and IL-1β activate the JNK pathway to further promote senescence and SASP secretion. Specific molecular alterations—including elevated CBLL1 expression that downregulates PTEN and increases ROS, and upregulation of CDC42 that induces premature senescence—further compromise endometrial function and implantation capacity. The study highlights that inflammaging exacerbates endometrial senescence through chronic low-grade inflammation, and identifies p53-regulated metabolic shifts toward oxidative phosphorylation and enhanced antioxidant defenses as protective mechanisms against senescence-induced tissue dysfunction.

title: Diverse roles of stress-responsive RNP granules in oogenesis and infertility
summary:
# Summary

This article examines how stress-responsive ribonucleoprotein (RNP) granules regulate oogenesis and female fertility through the integrated stress response (ISR). Oocytes respond to diverse stress signals—including endoplasmic reticulum stress, oxidative stress, nutrient deprivation, and viral infection—via four ISR kinases (PERK, HRI, GCN2, and PKR) that collectively inhibit translation and activate RNP granule assembly. Chronic stress in oocytes and supporting cells drives apoptosis and autophagy, culminating in ovarian failure, polycystic ovary syndrome, and endometriosis-associated infertility. Specialized RNP granules including stress granules, P-bodies, and nuage regulate distinct aspects of oocyte function: P-bodies and stress granules control maternal mRNA stability and translation through recruitment of regulatory proteins like DDX6 and eIF4-T, while the TOR pathway mechanistically controls P-body formation and size. Loss-of-function mutations in key granule components (DDX6, eIF4ENIF1) directly cause primary ovarian insufficiency and follicle defects, establishing RNP granules as essential regulators of female fertility. These findings suggest that metabolic stress factors including obesity, malnutrition, aging, and environmental exposures chronically activate the ISR, potentially explaining the connection between stress-related metabolic dysfunction and infertility conditions including endometriosis.

title: A Smart Nanoprobe for Visually Investigating the Activation Effect of Cyclical DOX Release on the p53 Pathway and Pathway-Related Molecules
summary:
# Article Summary

This study describes a smart nanoprobe (PDA-MB(DOX)-Pep) that selectively releases doxorubicin (DOX) in response to miRNA-34a, triggering a cyclical amplification cascade that enhances cancer cell death. The nanoprobe responds to miRNA-34a by releasing DOX, which activates the p53 pathway and upregulates miRNA-34a expression, creating a positive feedback loop that progressively releases additional DOX and intensifies apoptotic signals. The released DOX activates p53-mediated upregulation of the tumor suppressor miRNA-34a, which both enhances cellular sensitivity to chemotherapy and promotes apoptosis through caspase-3 activation. The nanoprobe demonstrated significant efficacy in inducing apoptosis in HeLa cells while maintaining good biocompatibility, with cellular uptake occurring through caveolae-mediated endocytosis. This rational design exploits the p53-miRNA-34a axis as a biomarker and amplification mechanism, offering a novel therapeutic strategy that combines smart drug delivery with targeted p53 pathway activation to overcome chemotherapy resistance and improve treatment outcomes.

title: In Silico Analysis of s-DAPK-1: From Structure to Function and Regulation
summary:
# Summary

This in silico analysis characterizes s-DAPK-1, an alternatively spliced variant of the death-associated protein kinase DAPK-1, revealing its broad involvement in cellular homeostasis and disease pathways. Both DAPK-1 and s-DAPK-1 regulate critical processes including autophagy, apoptosis, and cell survival signaling, with s-DAPK-1 additionally participating in metabolic processes, nucleic acid binding, and mRNA splicing through interactions with functionally diverse proteins including histone H2B, prion protein, and polyubiquitin-B. Transcription factors p53 and CREB1 upregulate DAPK-1 expression, while multiple microRNAs (miR-143-3p, miR-191, miR-483-5p, and miR-141-3p) suppress DAPK-1 post-transcriptionally, with miR-191 implicated in endometriosis-associated regulation. Notably, s-DAPK-1 expression is significantly reduced in cancer cell lines (HCT116, A375) compared to non-cancerous HEK-293 cells, suggesting its potential antitumor function, particularly in colorectal and skin malignancies. This structural and functional analysis establishes s-DAPK-1 as a multifunctional protein whose dysregulation through microRNA-mediated pathways may contribute to endometriosis and cancer progression.

title: Matrix Metalloproteinases and Their Inhibitors in the Pathogenesis of Epithelial Differentiation, Vascular Disease, Endometriosis, and Ocular Fibrotic Pterygium
summary:
# Summary

Matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs) are essential zinc-dependent enzymes that remodel the extracellular matrix and regulate critical physiological processes including tissue growth, morphogenesis, wound healing, and epithelial differentiation. MMPs play significant roles in the pathogenesis of endometriosis, vascular disease, fibrotic conditions, and ocular disorders, with particular relevance to ectopic endometrial tissue growth in endometriosis. The balance between MMP activity and their endogenous inhibitors is crucial; disruption of this equilibrium leads to pathological conditions including vascular disease, fibrotic disease, arthritis, and tumor progression. Specific MMPs such as MMP-9 facilitate epithelial-mesenchymal transition and can be therapeutically targeted, as inhibition of MMP-9 suppresses smooth muscle cell proliferation, migration, and EMT-associated processes. MMP inhibitors demonstrate therapeutic efficacy in treating inflammatory and vascular diseases, and broad-spectrum inhibitors can prevent pathological outcomes such as cataract formation. The dysregulation of MMP expression, often stimulated by inflammatory cytokines like TNF-α and TGF-β, underlies multiple disease mechanisms and represents an important therapeutic target across diverse pathological conditions including endometriosis.

title: Warburg-like Metabolic Reprogramming in Endometriosis: From Molecular Mechanisms to Therapeutic Approaches
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis exhibits a cancer-like metabolic reprogramming characterized by increased glucose uptake, enhanced Warburg's effect, and altered mitochondrial function that enables ectopic endometrial cells to survive in the hypoxic peritoneal environment. Multiple signaling pathways drive this metabolic shift, including hypoxia-inducible factor (HIF)-1α, the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway, and inflammatory cytokines, which converge to upregulate glycolytic enzymes like hexokinase 2 and pyruvate kinase M2 while suppressing mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation. The resulting aerobic glycolysis provides rapid ATP and biosynthetic precursors to fuel cell proliferation while increased lactate production creates an acidic microenvironment that promotes inflammation and immune evasion. Additionally, endometriotic lesions employ alternative metabolic strategies including increased fatty acid oxidation and glutamine metabolism to sustain energy production and biosynthesis. These metabolic adaptations directly facilitate the cancer-like pathological features of endometriosis—local invasion, metastasis, apoptotic resistance, and sustained proliferation—making metabolic reprogramming central to endometriosis pathogenesis and a promising therapeutic target.

title: Enhancing the therapeutic potential of FDA-approved ifosfamide and 5-fluorouracil through rational chemical modifications for endometrial cancer treatment
summary: Endometrial cancer, predominantly affecting postmenopausal women, arises through distinct molecular pathways including Type 1 disease driven by PTEN, β-catenin, and KRAS mutations, and Type 2 disease characterized by TP53 alterations. KRAS mutations activate both MAPK and PI3K/AKT signaling pathways, promoting uncontrolled cell growth and tumorigenesis, while TP53 protein normally maintains genomic stability through regulation of cell cycle arrest, DNA repair, apoptosis, and senescence. This study demonstrates that chemically modified versions of the FDA-approved drugs ifosfamide and 5-fluorouracil exhibit substantially improved binding affinities to mutated KRAS proteins compared to their unmodified counterparts, with modified 5-fluorouracil showing superior pharmacokinetic properties including water solubility, high synthetic accessibility, optimum lipophilicity, and moderate bioavailability while avoiding cytochrome inhibition. Both modified compounds effectively inhibit cell division and proliferation in endometrial cancer cells and are predicted to treat Type 1 endometrial cancer through rational chemical enhancement. These findings suggest that structural modifications of conventional chemotherapeutic agents can significantly enhance their therapeutic potential against endometrial cancers driven by specific oncogenic mutations.

title: Endostatin-based anti-angiogenic therapy and immune modulation: mechanisms and synergistic potential in cancer treatment
summary:
# Summary

Endostatin is a potent inhibitor of angiogenesis that operates through multiple complementary mechanisms to suppress tumor vascularization and enhance anti-tumor immunity. Endostatin-expressing endometrial mesenchymal stem cells inhibit angiogenesis by suppressing the PI3K/Akt/mTOR signaling pathway while reducing miRNA-21-5p levels and enhancing TIMP3 expression, demonstrating the therapeutic potential of cellular endostatin delivery. Beyond direct anti-angiogenic effects, endostatin modulates the tumor microenvironment by reducing immunosuppressive populations including myeloid-derived suppressor cells and M2-type macrophages while simultaneously promoting immune activation through increased mature dendritic cells, M1-type macrophages, and CD8+ T cell infiltration. Clinically, endostatin has demonstrated efficacy as monotherapy and in combination regimens with chemotherapy, radiotherapy, and immune checkpoint inhibitors across multiple cancer types including lung and colorectal cancers. The therapeutic mechanism involves vascular normalization that reduces hypoxia and improves immune cell infiltration while reversing VEGF-mediated immunosuppression, thereby synergizing anti-angiogenic and immunostimulatory effects for enhanced tumor control.

title: Drug Repurposing for Targeting ISL LIM Homeobox 2
summary: ISL2 (ISL LIM homeobox 2) is significantly elevated in endometrial tissues of endometriosis patients and promotes angiogenesis—a key pathological process in the disease—primarily through activation of the angiogenic factors VEGFA and ANGPT2. This elevation of ISL2 drives aberrant endometrial growth by stimulating blood vessel formation and supporting cellular proliferation and invasion. Through computational drug repurposing analysis, six candidate compounds—ivermectin, dactinomycin, paritaprevir, ergotamine, alectinib, and simeprevir—were identified as potent inhibitors of ISL2 with favorable binding interactions. Ivermectin emerged as the most promising candidate, establishing the strongest hydrogen bonding with ISL2 and demonstrating the lowest molecular fluctuation values in structural modeling. These findings suggest that ISL2 inhibition represents a novel therapeutic strategy for endometriosis by disrupting the angiogenic cascade that sustains ectopic endometrial lesions, with ivermectin warranting further preclinical investigation as a potential repurposed therapeutic agent.

title: Immunohistochemical Expression of IRE1 and PERK in Breast Cancer: Associations With Clinicopathological Characteristics and Survival Outcomes
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that IRE1 and PERK, two key mediators of the unfolded protein response (UPR), are significantly elevated in breast cancer tissues and serve as independent predictors of poor prognosis. IRE1 and PERK show strong co-expression and are associated with aggressive tumor characteristics including advanced TNM stage, high Ki-67 proliferation index, lymph node positivity, and hormone receptor expression, with high IRE1 and PERK expression linked to substantially increased mortality risk (hazard ratios >12). The IRE1-XBP1 signaling axis promotes epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition and increases estrogen receptor expression to drive resistance to endocrine therapy, while PERK phosphorylates eIF2α to upregulate ATF4 and NRF2, supporting tumor survival through oxidative stress mitigation and chemotherapy resistance via ABC transporter upregulation. Collectively, these findings indicate that moderate ER stress activates protective UPR responses that enhance cancer cell survival and treatment resistance, whereas the IRE1 and PERK pathways represent critical nodes driving breast cancer progression and could serve as potential therapeutic targets to overcome treatment resistance.

title: Targeting EphA2 suppresses the proliferation, migration and invasion of endometriosis via the AMPK signaling pathway
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies EphA2 as a key driver of endometriosis progression through activation of the AMPK signaling pathway. EphA2 expression is significantly elevated in both ectopic and eutopic endometrium of endometriosis patients, where it promotes cell proliferation, migration, and invasion. The researchers demonstrate that blocking EphA2 using RNA interference (si-EphA2) or the pharmacological inhibitor ALW-II-41-27 suppresses these pathological cellular behaviors by reducing AMPK phosphorylation, indicating that EphA2 functions upstream of AMPK signaling. Beyond direct cellular effects, EphA2 contributes to endometriosis pathogenesis through multiple immune-mediated mechanisms, including driving M2 macrophage polarization, enhancing CD8+ T cell activity, and promoting dendritic cell infiltration—all of which perpetuate chronic inflammation, neovascularization, and fibrosis. Pathway enrichment analysis reveals that EphA2 regulates metabolic processes including oxidative phosphorylation, linking the EphA2-AMPK axis to broader metabolic reprogramming in endometriosis. These findings establish EphA2 as a promising therapeutic target with the potential to suppress endometriosis progression through simultaneous inhibition of cellular proliferation and immune-driven pathological responses.

title: The Biological Role of LRPPRC in Human Cancers
journal: Cancer Control : Journal of the Moffitt Cancer Center
summary:
# Summary

LRPPRC is a multifunctional mitochondrial protein that serves as an m6A RNA reader and regulator with critical roles in cell cycle progression, mitochondrial gene translation, and energy metabolism. The protein functions as a core component of the m6A modification machinery, which is regulated by methyltransferases (METTL3, METTL14, WTAP, METTL16) that install modifications and demethylases (FTO, ALKBH5) that remove them, with m6A-binding proteins including YTHDF and IGF2BP families mediating downstream effects on mRNA processing, translation, and splicing. LRPPRC forms critical protein complexes with SLIRP to stabilize mitochondrial mRNA, with Beclin 1 and Bcl-2 to regulate autophagy, and with POLRMT to enhance mitochondrial transcription, thereby controlling mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation and autophagy. Dysregulation of LRPPRC contributes to multiple pathological conditions including mitochondrial diseases, metabolic disorders, neurodegenerative diseases (Alzheimer's and Parkinson's), and notably numerous human cancers where it is frequently upregulated and promotes cell proliferation, invasion, and anti-apoptotic mechanisms. These findings highlight LRPPRC as a central metabolic and epigenetic regulator with potential implications for understanding disease pathogenesis and developing targeted interventions.

title: IGF-1 regulates LARP6-mediated collagen metabolism in vaginal fibroblasts of POP patients via the PI3K/AKT pathway
summary: This study demonstrates that insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF-1) promotes collagen synthesis in vaginal fibroblasts through a PI3K/AKT-dependent mechanism that requires the RNA-binding protein LARP6, a pathway significantly impaired in pelvic organ prolapse (POP) patients. IGF-1 binds to its cell membrane receptor and activates the PI3K/AKT signaling cascade, which phosphorylates LARP6 and upregulates both type I and type III collagen expression in a dose- and time-dependent manner. LARP6 serves as a critical mediator of collagen biosynthesis, and its knockdown substantially reduces collagen levels while simultaneously increasing MMP2-mediated collagen degradation and decreasing the collagen-protective factor TIMP1. In POP patients, expression of IGF-1, LARP6, and both collagen types is significantly reduced in vaginal wall tissue while MMP2 levels are elevated, establishing a mechanistic basis for the compromised tissue strength underlying prolapse. Blocking AKT phosphorylation with specific inhibitors abrogates the entire IGF-1–collagen synthesis pathway, confirming that PI3K/AKT signaling is essential for this protective effect. These findings identify the IGF-1/LARP6/PI3K-AKT axis as a potential therapeutic target for restoring collagen-mediated pelvic floor tissue integrity in POP.

title: Dual functions of the ΔNp63-miR-141-3p-YAP1 regulatory axis in cervical cancer progression are dependent on histological subtype
summary: This study reveals a dual-function regulatory axis controlling cervical cancer progression that operates oppositely depending on histological subtype. The ΔNp63 transcription factor regulates miR-141-3p, which in turn targets YAP1, a key oncogenic driver controlled by the Hippo signaling pathway that promotes cell proliferation, migration, stemness, and metastasis. In squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), ΔNp63 is highly expressed and maintains epithelial integrity by upregulating adhesion molecules and suppressing epithelial-mesenchymal transition through miR-141-3p-mediated YAP1 inhibition. Conversely, in adenocarcinoma (ADC), YAP1 levels are elevated and correlate inversely with the ΔNp63-miR-141-3p axis, suggesting YAP1-driven progression in these more aggressive tumors. Functional studies confirm that ΔNp63 and miR-141-3p knockdown have opposing effects across cell lines: reducing proliferation and invasion in SCC while promoting epithelial phenotype in ADC cells. These findings demonstrate that histological subtype determines whether the ΔNp63-miR-141-3p-YAP1 axis functions as a tumor suppressor or oncogenic pathway in cervical cancer.

title: XBP1 promotes endometrial fibrosis through cGAS-STING signaling pathway in intrauterine adhesion
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that XBP1 drives endometrial fibrosis in intrauterine adhesion (IUA) through activation of the cGAS-STING pathway and mitochondrial DNA cytoplasmic leakage. XBP1 is significantly elevated in IUA patient and mouse model endometria, where it promotes epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) by triggering mitochondrial DNA release into the cytoplasm, which activates the cGAS-STING signaling cascade and downstream IRF3 phosphorylation. XBP1 knockdown reverses TGF-β1-induced EMT and reduces expression of fibrosis markers (N-cadherin, α-SMA, collagen I), while XBP1 inhibition with the drug Toyocamycin substantially reduces endometrial fibrosis and cGAS-STING pathway activity in vivo. Conversely, XBP1 overexpression and the activator IXA4 enhance fibrosis and EMT, and pharmacological cGAS-STING inhibition with C-176 prevents EMT even when XBP1 is overexpressed, confirming pathway dependence. These findings establish the XBP1-cGAS-STING axis as a central mechanism in IUA pathogenesis and identify potential therapeutic targets for treating this condition, which causes hypomenorrhea, infertility, and recurrent miscarriage.

title: Applications of CRISPR-Cas9 in mitigating cellular senescence and age-related disease progression
summary:
# Summary

This review examines CRISPR-Cas9 gene-editing technology as a therapeutic approach to mitigate cellular senescence and age-related disease progression. Multiple molecular pathways contribute to aging, including telomere shortening through reduced telomerase activity, accumulation of mutations in DNA repair and metabolic genes, inflammatory gene dysfunction, and epigenetic alterations in DNA methylation and histone modifications that can be triggered by lifestyle factors such as diet, exercise, and stress. CRISPR-Cas9 works by employing guide RNA to direct the Cas9 enzyme to target DNA sequences, creating double-strand breaks that are repaired through non-homologous end joining or homology-directed repair pathways. Preclinical studies demonstrate the technology's effectiveness, including successful reduction of progeria symptoms by targeting the LMNA gene and inhibition of osteosarcoma cells through manipulation of TERT promoter activity. Additionally, epigenetic modifications such as m6A methylation can regulate telomerase activity, offering another CRISPR-addressable mechanism for delaying cellular aging. This comprehensive molecular understanding positions CRISPR-Cas9 as a promising tool for treating diverse age-related conditions and genetic diseases.

title: CCN5 negatively regulates TGF-β-induced endometriosis associated fibrosis through Wnt/β-catenin signaling via Smad3-dependent mechanism
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that CCN5 functions as a critical negative regulator of TGF-β-induced fibrosis in endometriosis by disrupting Smad3-dependent signaling. While TGF-β drives fibrogenesis through activation of both TGF-β/Smad and Wnt/β-catenin pathways, elevated CCN5 expression in endometriotic lesions physically interacts with Smad3 in the cytoplasm, preventing its nuclear translocation and subsequent activation of pro-fibrotic genes. Experimental evidence demonstrates that CCN5 overexpression suppresses TGF-β-induced proliferation and fibrotic responses in human endometrial stromal cells, while CCN5 knockdown enhances these pro-fibrotic effects—a mechanism blocked by Smad3 inhibition. Since fibrosis substantially contributes to the clinical symptoms of endometriosis including chronic pelvic pain and infertility, these findings suggest that CCN5 acts as a potential endogenous brake on fibrotic progression. The study establishes CCN5 as a promising therapeutic target for managing endometriosis-associated fibrosis and its associated symptoms.

title: Macrophage WEE1 Directly Binds to and Phosphorylates NF‐κB p65 Subunit to Induce Inflammatory Response and Drive Atherosclerosis
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies macrophage WEE1, a kinase traditionally recognized for cell cycle regulation, as a direct activator of NF-κB-driven inflammation in atherosclerosis. WEE1 becomes phosphorylated at serine 642 in response to oxidized low-density lipoprotein (oxLDL) in macrophages, and this phosphorylated form directly binds to and phosphorylates the p65 NF-κB subunit at serine 536, thereby promoting inflammatory cytokine expression including TNF-α and IL-6. Critically, WEE1 acts independently of the canonical NF-κB pathway, as WEE1 deletion does not affect IKKβ phosphorylation or IκBα degradation. Both genetic deletion of WEE1 in macrophages and pharmacological inhibition with the WEE1 kinase inhibitor MK1775 substantially attenuate atherosclerotic plaque formation, reduce inflammatory cell infiltration, and promote plaque stability in high-fat diet-fed mice. These findings establish WEE1 phosphorylation as a targetable mechanism linking macrophage inflammation to atherosclerosis progression and suggest therapeutic potential for WEE1 inhibition in inflammatory vascular disease.

title: Causal Associations Between Cystatin and Lung Cancer: A Two‐Sample Mendelian Randomization Study
summary:
# Summary

Using two-sample Mendelian randomization analysis, this study establishes causal associations between cystatin family members and lung cancer development. Cystatin 8 demonstrates a significant causal relationship with squamous cell lung carcinoma, while Cystatin D shows causal association with lung adenocarcinoma after outlier removal. The cystatin family exerts multifaceted effects on lung cancer through inflammation modulation, protease inhibition, and immune regulation—Cystatin 8 regulates cancer cell proliferation and metastasis through protease inhibition, while Cystatin F secreted into the tumor microenvironment inhibits cytotoxic immune responses to promote immune evasion. Notable mechanistically, Cystatin 8 secreted by lung cancer cells degrades the endothelial glycocalyx to enhance cancer cell adhesion to brain microvasculature, promoting brain metastasis. Conversely, some cystatins exhibit tumor-suppressive properties; Cystatin D induces mesenchymal-epithelial transition and Cystatin B inhibits tumor invasion through cathepsin regulation. Elevated serum cystatin levels, particularly Cystatin C and Cystatin SN, correlate with increased lung cancer mortality, recurrence, and metastasis rates, supporting their potential clinical utility as prognostic biomarkers.

title: The cGAS-STING pathway: a dual regulator of immune response in cancer and therapeutic implications
summary:
# Summary

The cGAS-STING pathway functions as a critical dual-regulator of anti-tumor immunity by detecting cytoplasmic DNA and activating type I interferon and pro-inflammatory cytokine production, which recruits dendritic cells and T cells to the tumor microenvironment. However, this pathway simultaneously promotes immune evasion by upregulating PD-L1 on tumor cells, suppressing T cell activity and allowing tumors to escape surveillance. The pathway's activation is enhanced by autophagy and phase separation of cGAS into liquid droplets, which amplify DNA sensing capacity, while also being regulated by post-translational modifications including m6A methylation and acetylation. Multiple therapeutic strategies can be leveraged to potentiate cGAS-STING signaling, including radiation therapy, PARP inhibitors, WEE1 inhibition, and manganese supplementation, all of which induce DNA damage or enhance pathway activation to generate anti-tumor immunity. Conversely, inhibition of the SETDB1-TRIM28 complex enhances cGAS expression, while targeting Gasdermin D and inhibiting ACLY promote cGAS activation and PD-L1 downregulation. These findings underscore the therapeutic potential of modulating the cGAS-STING axis in combination with checkpoint inhibitors to overcome immune evasion and maximize anti-tumor immune responses.

title: Osteopontin Expression and Its Role in Endometrial Cancer: A Systematic Review
summary:
# Summary

Osteopontin (OPN), encoded by the SPP1 gene on chromosome 4, is a phosphorylated glycoprotein that binds integrins and CD44 receptors to regulate multiple cellular and immunological processes relevant to endometrial cancer and endometriosis. The protein activates critical signaling pathways including PI3K-AKT-GSK3β-β-catenin and MAPK cascades, which promote cell adhesion, migration, immune regulation, and inflammatory responses through macrophage recruitment and pro-inflammatory cytokine secretion. In endometrial cancer, OPN is significantly overexpressed in patient samples compared to healthy controls and correlates with increased tumor grade and advanced stage, where it promotes epithelial-mesenchymal transition, angiogenesis, invasion, and metastatic progression. OPN additionally suppresses anti-tumor immunity by modulating T cell, macrophage, and natural killer cell function to facilitate immune evasion and tumor growth. Beyond cancer, OPN is associated with endometriosis pathology and is normally expressed in uterine decidual and epithelial cells where its expression is regulated by progesterone, suggesting a dual role in both physiological reproductive processes and pathological disease progression.

title: Epigenetic Dysregulation in Cancer: Implications for Gene Expression and DNA Repair-Associated Pathways
summary:
# Summary: Epigenetic Dysregulation in Cancer—Implications for Gene Expression and DNA Repair

Epigenetic modifications—including DNA methylation, histone modifications, and chromatin remodeling—profoundly regulate gene expression and genomic stability, with dysregulation contributing to cancer development and progression. Three major mechanisms coordinate these effects: DNA methyltransferases (DNMT1, DNMT3A, DNMT3B) catalyze DNA methylation controlled by S-adenosyl methionine, while ten-eleven translocation 2 (TET2) mediates demethylation; histone-modifying enzymes including acetyltransferases, deacetylases, methyltransferases, and demethylases reversibly alter histone tails to activate or repress transcription; and chromatin remodeling complexes (SWI/SNF, ISWI, CHD) facilitate nucleosome repositioning and DNA repair. Aberrant epigenetic patterns in cancer impair critical DNA repair pathways—specifically homologous recombination, non-homologous end joining, and base excision repair—while hypermethylation silences tumor suppressors and hypomethylation promotes genomic instability. Notably, mutations in DNMT3A and TET2 associate with acute myeloid leukemia and related hematological malignancies, and specific histone marks (H3K4me3 for activation, H3K9me3 and H3K27me3 for repression) serve as epigenetic determinants of cancer-related gene expression patterns.

title: Recent Insights Into Breast Cancer: Molecular Pathways, Epigenetic Regulation, and Emerging Targeted Therapies
journal: Breast Cancer : Basic and Clinical Research
summary:
# Summary

Breast cancer encompasses distinct molecular subtypes driven by key signaling pathways including estrogen receptor, HER2, Notch, Hedgehog, Wnt, and fibroblast growth factor receptor signaling, which collectively influence tumor behavior and prognosis. Epigenetic alterations—particularly DNA methylation, histone modifications, and microRNA expression—critically modulate gene activity and tumor initiation through enzymes like DNMTs, HDACs, and EZH2. HER2 overexpression, regulated by the TFAP2C transcription factor and epigenetic mechanisms, drives tumor cell growth and metastatic potential via downstream MAPK and PI3K signaling pathways. Epigenetic dysregulation fundamentally contributes to therapeutic resistance, notably through DNA methylation, HDAC activity, and miR-221/222 expression that sustain tamoxifen resistance in hormone receptor-positive cancers. Emerging targeted therapies including CAR-T cell immunotherapy and novel epigenetic modulators targeting these pathways represent promising approaches, while established risk factors such as hormonal contraception, aging, smoking, and delayed menopause modulate cancer development risk. The integration of understanding molecular subtypes, epigenetic regulation, and stem cell pathways increasingly informs personalized therapeutic strategies for improving breast cancer outcomes.

title: ELK1 regulates BMPR1B transcriptional activity in ovine granulosa cells
journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies transcription factor ELK1 as a key regulator of BMPR1B gene expression in ovine granulosa cells, with significant implications for reproductive function. ELK1 directly enhances BMPR1B transcriptional activity by binding to specific ELK1-binding sites within the −438 to −208 bp region of the BMPR1B promoter. ELK1 overexpression substantially increases BMPR1B mRNA and protein levels in granulosa cells, while simultaneously reducing granulosa cell apoptosis through BMPR1B-mediated signaling. Since BMPR1B is a major gene controlling ovulation rate and litter size in sheep, and the BMP/Smad signaling pathway is essential for ovarian follicle development, FSH sensitivity, and endometrial receptivity, this ELK1-BMPR1B regulatory axis represents a critical molecular mechanism governing female reproductive traits. The findings suggest that ELK1-mediated upregulation of BMPR1B protects granulosa cells from apoptosis and promotes fertility, potentially explaining differential reproductive capacity between high and low-fertility animals.

title: Uncovering the Molecular Landscape of Physical Activity: Proteomic Insights from the UK Biobank
summary:
# Summary

This proteomic analysis of the UK Biobank identifies circulating plasma proteins as key molecular mediators linking physical activity to reduced risk of chronic diseases. Proteins including GDF15, PRSS8, and IGFBP4 mediate associations between various physical activity features—particularly moderate-to-vigorous activity intensity, bout frequency, and dynamic movement metrics—and improved health outcomes. Notable proteins like ITGAV and MYOM3 show strong associations across all physical activity categories, with ITGAV involved in mechanotransduction and MYOM3 essential for muscle integrity. Pathway analysis reveals that cytokine-related signaling, the PI3K-Akt pathway (regulating muscle hypertrophy and glucose homeostasis), and phagosome pathways are significantly enriched among activity-associated proteins. The findings demonstrate that increased physical activity, particularly high-intensity and frequent exercise bouts, reduces incidence of neurodegenerative diseases including Parkinson's disease, Alzheimer's disease, and vascular dementia, while prolonged sedentary behavior conversely increases risk for cardiovascular disease, diabetes, and other chronic conditions through distinct proteomic signatures.

title: Sirtuins and their role in ovarian aging-related fibrosis predisposing to ovarian cancer
summary: This review examines how sirtuins, a family of NAD+-dependent deacetylases, regulate ovarian aging and fibrosis, which collectively create a pro-cancer microenvironment. Age-related decline in NAD+ levels reduces sirtuin activity across multiple cell types, particularly in granulosa cells, leading to dysregulation of the TGF-β/Smad signaling pathway and concurrent activation of NLRP3 inflammasome and NF-κB signaling that drive fibrosis. Individual sirtuins play distinct protective roles: SIRT1 suppresses fibrosis through deacetylation of Smads and inhibition of TGF-β signaling; SIRT3 maintains mitochondrial function and oxidative stress resistance; SIRT6 supports DNA repair and collagen regulation; and others regulate oocyte maturation and metabolic balance. Progressive ovarian fibrosis increases tissue stiffness, creates a pre-metastatic niche, and promotes epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition, predisposing postmenopausal women to ovarian cancer. The article identifies therapeutic opportunities through NAD+ repletion and direct sirtuin activation via compounds like resveratrol and metformin, which attenuate fibrotic signaling and restore antifibrotic/tumor-suppressive pathways.

title: Itraconazole inhibits the Wnt/β-catenin signaling pathway to induce tumor-associated macrophages polarization to enhance the efficacy of immunotherapy in endometrial cancer
summary:
# Summary

Itraconazole (ITZ), an antifungal drug, demonstrates potent anti-tumor activity against endometrial cancer through dual mechanisms targeting both cancer cells and the tumor microenvironment. ITZ directly inhibits Ishikawa endometrial cancer cell proliferation and invasion while inducing apoptosis, and mechanistically, it suppresses the Wnt/β-catenin signaling pathway by downregulating Wnt-3a and β-catenin while upregulating the negative regulator Axin-1. Beyond direct tumor cell killing, ITZ modulates tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs) by promoting polarization from the immunosuppressive M2 phenotype toward the anti-tumor M1 phenotype, an effect mediated through increased interferon-gamma secretion and reduced interleukin-10 levels. Critically, ITZ significantly enhances the efficacy of immune checkpoint inhibitors in vivo, with the combination synergistically reducing tumor burden and shifting TAM polarization toward M1, thereby restoring anti-tumor immune responses that would otherwise be attenuated by M2 TAMs and Wnt/β-catenin signaling. These findings establish ITZ as a promising candidate for combination immunotherapy in endometrial cancer, operating through inhibition of a key pathway that normally sustains tumor-promoting macrophage populations.

title: Molecular mechanisms underlying the inhibition of cell migration and invasion in endometriosis: Advances in pharmacological research (Review)
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic gynecological disease characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue growth outside the uterus, causing pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and infertility through dysregulated cell migration and invasion. The pathology involves multiple interconnected mechanisms including elevated matrix metalloproteinase expression, altered adhesion molecule signaling, and the critical HIF-1α/autophagy axis activated by the hypoxic endometriotic microenvironment, which promotes an invasive phenotype through epithelial-mesenchymal transition. Endometrial stromal cells drive ectopic implantation by upregulating adhesion molecules and matrix-degrading enzymes, while pro-inflammatory signaling through CCL2/CXCL1 amplifies lesion progression.
