In breast cancer, NETosis facilitates epithelial-mesenchymal transition, immune evasion, angiogenesis, organotropic metastasis, and therapeutic resistance by reshaping the tumor microenvironment through polarization of macrophages and enhancement of cancer-associated fibroblast invasiveness. Three distinct NETosis pathways—suicidal, vital, and mitochondrial—differentially promote tumor growth, immune evasion, and metastatic spread, with particularly pronounced NET formation in triple-negative breast cancer correlating with increased tumor size, proliferation, and lymph node metastasis. NET components including citrullinated histone H3, myeloperoxidase-DNA complexes, and cell-free DNA serve as potential biomarkers for disease monitoring and therapeutic targeting. Therapeutic strategies targeting NETosis, such as DNase I treatment and PAD4 inhibition, show promise in preclinical models for reducing metastasis by preventing histone citrullination and chromatin decondensation. The mechanistic insights into NET-mediated capture of circulating tumor cells, blood-brain barrier disruption via protease-mediated degradation of tight junction proteins, and pre-metastatic niche formation position NETosis as both a critical pathogenic mechanism and a viable therapeutic target in breast cancer.

title: Lactate-mediated lactylation in human health and diseases: Progress and remaining challenges
summary:
# Summary

Lactate serves as both a metabolic byproduct and an epigenetic signaling molecule through a post-translational modification process called lactylation, wherein histone acetyltransferases (P300, GCN5, TIP60, KAT8) and aminoacyl-tRNA synthetases (AARS1/2) catalyze the addition of lactyl groups to histone lysine residues, while HDACs and SIRTs remove these modifications. Lactate-mediated histone lactylation, particularly H3K18 and H4K12 lactylation, regulates critical cellular processes including gene transcription, angiogenesis, immune responses, and cellular differentiation through modulation of transcription factors like HIF-1α and Oct4. This modification couples glycolytic metabolism with autophagy via KAT5/TIP60-mediated Vps34 lactylation, enhances DNA damage repair through MRE11 lactylation, and suppresses oxidative phosphorylation through lactylation of mitochondrial enzymes PDHA1 and CPT2. Beyond metabolic reprogramming, lactate-induced lactylation regulates diverse physiological processes including embryonic development, neural development, osteoblast differentiation, and myogenesis, while also contributing to pathological conditions such as pyroptosis and drug resistance in cancer cells.

title: Novel applications of gene and cell therapies in the treatment of gynecological disorders
summary:
# Gene and Cell Therapies for Gynecological Disorders

This article reviews novel gene and cell therapy approaches for treating multiple gynecological conditions, with particular emphasis on overcoming therapeutic resistance mechanisms. For endometriosis, which affects approximately 10% of reproductive-age women and causes chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and infertility, therapeutic strategies include RNA interference silencing of hormone-resistance drivers (SF-1, COX-2, IL-6) and engineered mesenchymal stem cells expressing IL-10 and VEGF to reduce fibrosis and restore hormonal responsiveness. In ovarian cancer, CRISPR/Cas9 gene editing and CAR-T cell therapies address multidrug resistance by eliminating expression of resistance genes (BCL2, ABCB1) and targeting tumor-associated antigens (MUC16, mesothelin, folate receptor-α), while CAR-NK cells show promise against platinum-resistant disease. For cervical cancer, therapies target HPV oncogenic proteins (E6/E7) through AAV-mediated shRNA delivery and TCR-T cell recognition of viral antigens. The article emphasizes that overcoming the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment through armored CAR-T cells secreting IL-12 and targeting matrix remodeling factors represents a critical frontier for improving treatment efficacy across these gynecological malignancies.

title: TTK Defines a High-Risk Oral Squamous Cell Carcinoma Subtype Through Dual mTORC1/NF-κB Activation
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies TTK (tyrosine kinase with immunoglobulin and EGF-like domains 1) as a critical oncogenic driver in oral squamous cell carcinoma (OSCC) through dual activation of the mTORC1 and NF-κB signaling pathways. TTK activates these pathways by directly interacting with the TAK1–TAB protein complex, which in turn activates NF-κB signaling, while simultaneously promoting mTORC1-mediated cell proliferation via p70S6K and 4EBP1 phosphorylation. This dual pathway activation defines a high-risk C2 tumor subtype characterized by pronounced genomic instability, elevated mutational burden, and TP53 mutations, all associated with worse survival outcomes. Functionally, TTK inhibition significantly suppresses OSCC cell proliferation and invasion while markedly enhancing cisplatin sensitivity, suggesting TTK as a therapeutic target to overcome chemotherapy resistance. These findings establish TTK as a central hub integrating metabolic and inflammatory signaling in aggressive OSCC, with important implications for risk stratification and combination therapeutic strategies.

title: Lactate and lactylation: molecular insights into histone and non-histone lactylation in tumor progression, tumor immune microenvironment, and therapeutic strategies
summary:
# Summary

Lactate, a glycolytic metabolite produced through the pyruvate-to-lactate conversion by lactate dehydrogenase, functions as both an energy source and signaling molecule that drives cancer progression through multiple interconnected mechanisms. Lactate mediates its effects through lactylation, a posttranslational modification of histone and non-histone proteins that alters epigenetic regulation and metabolic reprogramming, as well as through G-protein coupled receptor signaling, where lactic acid activates GPR81 and GPR132 to initiate downstream cascades including the PKA–AMPK–NAD⁺–SIRT1 pathway that stabilizes the MYC proto-oncogene and promotes macrophage proliferation. MYC and hypoxia-inducible factor-1 (HIF-1) coordinate metabolic reprogramming by upregulating glycolytic enzymes including hexokinase 2, pyruvate kinase, and lactate dehydrogenase A while suppressing lactate dehydrogenase B, and by inducing the expression of lactate transporters (MCT1, MCT2, MCT4) that facilitate lactate shuttling between tumor cells and the microenvironment. Cancer cells additionally leverage glutamine metabolism to produce lactate through a MYC-regulated pathway that forms a positive feedback loop with lactate accumulation. The review identifies subtype-specific lactylation profiles as promising therapeutic targets, suggesting that interventions targeting lactate production, transport, and lactylation-dependent signaling could provide novel strategies for cancer treatment.

title: The directionality of collective cell delamination is governed by tissue architecture and cell adhesion in a Drosophila carcinoma model
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that septate junction (SJ) integrity is critical for epithelial homeostasis, as loss of SJ components (M6, Anakonda, Nervana 2) triggers apoptosis and promotes collective delamination in RasV12 tumor cells. The directionality of delamination—whether cells extrude apically or basally—is determined by tissue architecture and preferential cell adhesion patterns rather than intrinsic oncogenic properties alone. Apically delaminated cells from squamous epithelia maintain E-cadherin laterally and undergo apical constriction with characteristic apical protrusions, while basally delaminated cells from pseudostratified epithelia reorganize as rosettes with centralized E-cadherin and lose basal integrin anchoring. These findings reveal that the spatial context of tumor evolution, combined with adhesion molecule localization and junctional remodeling, governs whether transformed cells are extruded as a tissue surveillance mechanism or escape to invade distally. The study highlights how disruption of cell-cell and cell-matrix junctions facilitates malignant progression and suggests that understanding junction-regulated delamination directionality is essential for predicting carcinoma invasion patterns and metastatic potential.

title: Chemoselective sulfonyl fluoride exchange (SuFEx)-induced macrocyclization of tyrosine-containing peptides in aqueous media
summary:
# Summary

This study describes the development of a chemoselective sulfonyl fluoride exchange (SuFEx) strategy for synthesizing cyclic peptides (STEMtides) through tyrosine-selective macrocyclization in aqueous conditions. The SuFEx chemistry exhibits high efficiency under mild, biologically compatible conditions and demonstrates remarkable selectivity for tyrosine residues over other nucleophilic amino acids including lysine, histidine, serine, and cysteine. Resulting cyclic peptides display substantially enhanced metabolic stability, proteolytic resistance, and cell permeability compared to their linear precursors; for example, cyclic RGD-containing STEMtide analogs inhibited integrin αvβ3-mediated cell adhesion with potency comparable to the clinical drug cilengitide while maintaining low toxicity. The method successfully produced bioactive cyclic variants of clinically relevant peptides including leuprorelin, β-MSH, and liraglutide, establishing SuFEx-mediated cyclization as a practical tool for improving the pharmaceutical properties and therapeutic potential of peptide-based drugs.

title: Identification and Characterization of m6A Regulators METTL3 and YTHDF2: Unveiling Their Biological Functions in Endometriosis
summary: This study demonstrates that N6-methyladenosine (m6A) modification, regulated by key enzymes including METTL3 and YTHDF2, plays a critical role in endometriosis pathogenesis through effects on RNA splicing, translation, and stability. Notably, METTL3 and YTHDF2 are significantly downregulated in ectopic endometrial tissue and function as suppressors of endometriotic cell behaviors, inhibiting proliferation, migration, invasion, and angiogenesis. Conversely, other m6A regulators including YTHDF1 and FTO are upregulated in ectopic endometrium, suggesting dysregulation of the m6A modification system in endometriosis. The study identified a disease-associated gene module enriched in pathways related to cell adhesion, vascular smooth muscle contraction, and developmental processes, while immune profiling revealed altered infiltration patterns of CD8 T cells, monocytes, and dendritic cells in ectopic tissue. These findings establish m6A modification as a novel regulatory mechanism in endometriosis and identify METTL3, YTHDF2, YTHDF1, RBM15B, and FTO as potential biomarkers for disease diagnosis and therapeutic targets.

title: ISEV2025 Abstract Book
summary:
# Summary

This research examines engineered extracellular vesicles (EVs) and their mechanisms in cancer biology, with particular focus on EV biogenesis and therapeutic applications. Engineered HER2-expressing EVs (PEG-EV-eHER2) derived from 293T cells effectively enhance HER2-targeted antibody-drug conjugate efficacy while notably failing to activate the oncogenic PI3K/Akt signaling pathway, suggesting a safer therapeutic approach. At the molecular level, EV biogenesis is tightly regulated by Ral GTPases and the protein EHD4, which coordinate multivesicular body maturation, size, and mobility to control exosome secretion, with EHD4 knockout delaying primary tumor growth in mouse models. ESCRT family proteins enable exosome generation through phase separation, while the RNA-binding protein NPM1 facilitates selective RNA cargo sorting into exosomes. Additionally, small EVs show promise as biomarkers in cancer diagnosis and treatment monitoring, with protease activity in circulating EVs correlating with therapeutic response in uveal melanoma patients, and circulating tumor cell-derived EVs contributing to premetastatic niche formation and angiogenesis in neuroblastoma.

title: Traditional and New Views on MSI-H/dMMR Endometrial Cancer
summary:
# Summary

MSI-H/dMMR endometrial cancer arises from defects in the mismatch repair (MMR) pathway, which normally maintains genomic stability through coordinated action of proteins including MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2, and MSH3. These defects stem from three distinct mechanisms: germline mutations in canonical MMR genes associated with Lynch syndrome, MLH1 promoter hypermethylation in sporadic cancers, and somatic mutations in both canonical and non-canonical MMR genes in Lynch-like tumors. When MMR function is compromised, microsatellite instability accumulates, increasing tumor mutation burden and generating novel antigens that trigger robust T cell-mediated immune responses through tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes. This distinctive immunogenic profile makes MSI-H/dMMR endometrial cancers highly responsive to immune checkpoint inhibitor-based immunotherapy, although approximately 50% of patients show resistance potentially due to abnormalities in non-canonical MMR genes. Understanding the molecular heterogeneity of MMR deficiency and the functional consequences of different mutation types is therefore crucial for optimizing treatment strategies and predicting clinical outcomes in this endometrial cancer subtype.

title: Neurotransmitter Regulatory Networks: A New Perspective on Cancer Therapy
summary:
# Summary

This article presents neurotransmitters as critical regulators of tumor progression through a bidirectional relationship with the nervous system: while neurotransmitters directly promote cancer development via receptor binding and classical signaling pathways (PI3K/AKT, MEK/ERK, WNT/β-catenin), tumors actively induce neural networks to create a permissive microenvironment for proliferation and metastasis. Acetylcholine and nicotine drive cancer advancement through nAChR activation, promoting proliferation, invasion, angiogenesis, and chemoresistance, while also upregulating the Warburg effect and facilitating epithelial-mesenchymal transition in lung cancer. Conversely, dopamine exhibits inhibitory effects on tumor growth, and selective muscarinic receptors show opposing roles—mAChRM1 suppresses colon cancer while mAChRM3 is associated with malignancy. Glutamate and GABA receptors similarly demonstrate context-dependent effects: mGluRs promote multiple hallmarks of cancer and facilitate lung cancer brain metastasis through EGFR stabilization, whereas GABAA receptor agonists inhibit medulloblastoma growth. These findings underscore neurotransmitter signaling as a multifaceted therapeutic target, with differential receptor subtype activities offering potential for selective cancer intervention strategies.

title: CCNA2 and CCNB3 as Early Potential Molecular Candidates of Oocyte Maturation in Cumulus-Oophorous Complex Cells from Follicular Fluid
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies CCNA2 and CCNB3 as early molecular markers of oocyte maturation in cumulus-oophorous complex cells, which correlate with embryo development capacity and live birth rates. CCNA2 regulates cell cycle progression through S and M phases by activating CDK complexes and is essential for metaphase-II spindle formation, while CCNB3 is specifically expressed in germ cells and drives the metaphase-anaphase transition required for meiosis and polar body formation. The research also characterizes microRNAs (miR-17, miR-106b, miR-1275, and miR-190a) as epigenetic regulators differentially expressed between normal (NOR) and diminished ovarian reserve (DOR) cases, with miR-1275 particularly promoting follicular atresia through granulosa cell apoptosis by suppressing LRH-1 and impairing CYP19A1 signaling. Together, these findings establish cyclins and microRNAs as complementary biomarkers for predicting oocyte developmental competence and understanding mechanisms of female infertility associated with impaired ovarian function.

title: The Role of Intracellular Lipid-Binding Proteins in Digestive System Neoplasms
summary: Intracellular lipid-binding proteins (iLBPs), including CRBPs, CRABPs, and FABPs, serve as critical mediators of lipid homeostasis and cellular signaling in digestive system neoplasms by transporting fatty acids and retinoids while regulating gene expression, inflammatory pathways, and oxidative balance. CRBP1 functions predominantly as a tumor suppressor across pancreatic, esophageal, and hepatocellular cancers by upregulating retinoic acid and activating RAR/RXR signaling, which suppresses the oncogenic Wnt/β-catenin pathway, though its expression is frequently reduced in these malignancies. In contrast, CRABP2 exhibits context-dependent roles: it promotes chemoresistance and tumor growth in pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma and colorectal cancer by enhancing IL-8 signaling and stabilizing lipid raft formation, while paradoxically suppressing liver metastasis through PINK1-mediated mitophagy that reduces reactive oxygen species. CRABP2 upregulation correlates with oxaliplatin resistance and poor differentiation, whereas its knockdown impairs cell migration and invasion by disrupting human antigen R interactions. FABPs facilitate fatty acid transport and deliver lipids to nuclear receptors including PPARs and RARs, thereby modulating signaling pathways critical for cancer cell survival. These findings underscore iLBPs as multifunctional oncogenic and tumor-suppressive factors whose expression patterns and subcellular localization determine their contribution to gastrointestinal cancer development and therapeutic resistance.

title: Crosstalk Between Inflammasome Signalling and Epithelial-Mesenchymal Transition in Cancer and Benign Disease: Mechanistic Insights, Context-Dependence, and Therapeutic Opportunities
summary:
# Summary

This review examines the bidirectional crosstalk between inflammasome signaling and epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT), with implications for both cancer and benign diseases including endometriosis and adenomyosis. The NLRP3 inflammasome represents a central hub that activates pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-1β and IL-18, which in turn promote EMT through TGF-β signaling and upregulation of EMT transcription factors such as SNAIL, SLUG, and ZEB1. Key molecular changes during EMT include downregulation of epithelial markers like E-cadherin and upregulation of mesenchymal proteins including N-cadherin, vimentin, fibronectin, and matrix metalloproteinases, with EMT transcriptional programs reciprocally modulating inflammasome activity via NF-κB signaling. The review emphasizes context-dependent activation mechanisms, including roles for HIF-1α, Notch signaling, and angiotensin II, while highlighting how IL-1β engagement of Smad pathways orchestrates this inflammatory-mesenchymal axis. This mechanistic framework provides therapeutic opportunities for targeting inflammasome-EMT crosstalk in pathological conditions where aberrant EMT contributes to disease progression, including endometriosis and adenomyosis.

title: Targeting the N-cadherin/β-catenin axis with MSAB reverses malignant phenotypes in blast crisis of CML
summary:
# Summary

N-cadherin (CDH2) is significantly elevated in chronic myeloid leukemia blast crisis and drives malignant transformation through activation of the Wnt/β-catenin signaling axis. N-cadherin overexpression promotes cell cycle progression by upregulating β-catenin, c-Myc, and cyclin-dependent kinases (CCND1, CCNE1, CDK2, CDK4, CDK6), while simultaneously suppressing myeloid differentiation and reducing expression of differentiation antigens (CD13, CD10) and the cytokine IL-6. Treatment with MSAB, a β-catenin degrader, effectively reverses these malignant phenotypes by downregulating the N-cadherin/β-catenin signaling cascade, inducing G2/M phase arrest and apoptosis while promoting differentiation and restoring IL-6 production. These findings establish the N-cadherin/β-catenin axis as a critical driver of CML blast crisis progression and identify MSAB-mediated β-catenin degradation as a novel therapeutic strategy for treating advanced-phase CML.

title: Metabolic Reprogramming in Lung Cancer: Hallmarks, Mechanisms, and Targeted Strategies to Overcome Immune Resistance
summary:
# Summary

Lung cancer exhibits profound metabolic reprogramming that fundamentally shapes tumor-immune interactions and drives immune evasion. Aerobic glycolysis, enhanced through upregulation of glucose transporters (GLUT1), hexokinase (HK2), and lactate dehydrogenase (LDHA)—often driven by MYC or NEUROD1 transcription factors—promotes tumor progression while simultaneously suppressing effector T cell function through lactate accumulation and glucose depletion in the tumor microenvironment. Beyond glycolysis, dysregulated lipid metabolism mediated by key enzymes (ACLY, FASN, CPT1A) and transcription factors (SREBP, PPAR) fuels immune evasion by promoting regulatory T cell differentiation and macrophage polarization, while altered amino acid metabolism via upregulated glutaminase, IDO1, and ARG1 depletes critical nutrients and generates immunosuppressive metabolites including glutamate and kynurenine. The convergence of these metabolic alterations—orchestrated by signaling pathways including HIF-1α, mTOR, and SREBP—creates a hostile microenvironment that impairs mTOR signaling, suppresses T cell receptor activation, and drives T cell exhaustion, while paradoxically enabling regulatory T cells to thrive through fatty acid oxidation. Targeting these metabolic vulnerabilities, particularly through glycolytic inhibition, offers a promising strategy to restore effector T cell function and overcome immune resistance in lung cancer.

title: Decreased ALKBH5 in neutrophil correlates with disease activity in rheumatoid arthritis and ALKBH5 modulates neutrophil autophagy
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that ALKBH5, an m6A demethylase, is significantly reduced in neutrophils from rheumatoid arthritis patients and inversely correlates with disease activity markers including DAS28-CRP, ESR, and neutrophil percentage. ALKBH5 suppresses neutrophil autophagy by catalyzing m6A demethylation of ATG7 mRNA; when ALKBH5 is silenced, m6A methylation of ATG7 mRNA increases, stabilizing the transcript and promoting autophagy through enhanced ATG7 expression. Conversely, elevated autophagic activity is prominent in RA synovial fluid neutrophils where ALKBH5 expression is lowest, suggesting that reduced ALKBH5 permits excessive autophagy-driven neutrophil inflammation. A novel predictive model combining ALKBH5, hemoglobin, and lymphocyte percentage effectively correlates with RA clinical severity and comorbidities including anemia and interstitial lung disease. These findings identify ALKBH5-mediated m6A modification of autophagy-related genes as a critical regulatory mechanism linking epigenetic control to neutrophil-mediated RA pathology.

title: RAD23B Promotes Colorectal Cancer Metastasis via the Talin1/Integrin/PI3K/AKT/MMP9 Axis
summary:
# Summary

RAD23B promotes colorectal cancer metastasis by upregulating and activating a coordinated signaling cascade involving Talin1, integrins αvβ1, and downstream PI3K/AKT signaling. RAD23B directly interacts with and upregulates Talin1 and integrin expression, which in turn activate PI3K/AKT signaling and enhance MMP9 expression. This integrated pathway drives enhanced cell migration, proliferation, and invasion in vitro, while RAD23B overexpression significantly increases liver metastasis in vivo mouse models. The mechanism fundamentally depends on Talin1-mediated integrin activation, which facilitates extracellular matrix degradation through MMP9 upregulation and enables the metastatic progression of colorectal cancer cells. These findings identify the RAD23B/Talin1/integrin/PI3K/AKT/MMP9 axis as a critical regulatory network controlling CRC metastatic potential.

title: Hypoxia-mediated regulation of mRNA metabolism: from transcription to stability
summary:
# Summary

Hypoxia triggers coordinated regulation of mRNA metabolism through HIF-1α-mediated transcriptional and post-transcriptional mechanisms. Upon oxygen depletion, stabilized HIF-1α accumulates in cells and activates target gene expression by recruiting RNA polymerase II and transcriptional cofactors to hypoxia response elements, simultaneously inducing proinflammatory signals (NF-κB, TLR) and metabolic reprogramming via HK1/LDHA upregulation. Hypoxia simultaneously suppresses global protein synthesis through eIF2α phosphorylation and mTOR inhibition while selectively maintaining translation of critical stress-response mRNAs like VEGFA through internal ribosome entry sites. Post-transcriptional control involves dynamic mRNA modifications including m6A, m5C, and ac4C, which are influenced by hypoxia and modulate both mRNA stability and translation efficiency through RNA-binding protein interactions and the DARS-AS1/METTL3-METTL14 axis, ultimately promoting cytoprotective autophagy. This multi-layered regulatory architecture enables cells to rapidly reprogram gene expression patterns and metabolic pathways in response to hypoxic stress, with particular relevance to endometriosis where localized hypoxia drives pathogenic inflammation and metabolic adaptation.

title: SIRT7 drives energy metabolic shifts in endometriosis via interaction with TUFM and Rhoa/Rock/Akt pathway activation
journal: Cellular and Molecular Life Sciences: CMLS
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue growth and a metabolic shift from mitochondrial respiration to glycolysis, driven by elevated SIRT7 expression in lesions. SIRT7 orchestrates this metabolic reprogramming through multiple mechanisms: it upregulates glycolytic enzymes GBE1 and PYGL while suppressing mitochondrial oxidative enzymes IDH1 and SDHB, and it inhibits NRF1 to prevent mitochondrial biogenesis. SIRT7 interacts with the mitochondrial protein TUFM and activates the RhoA/ROCK/AKT signaling pathway, promoting epithelial-mesenchymal transition and enhancing endometrial stromal cell proliferation and migration. Experimentally, SIRT7 knockdown or TUFM overexpression substantially reduces metabolic reprogramming, proliferation, and migration capacity, establishing these proteins as critical drivers of endometriosis progression. This work identifies the SIRT7-TUFM axis as a key metabolic regulator linking altered glucose metabolism to the pathophysiology of endometriosis and suggests potential therapeutic targets for intervention.

title: Clear Cell Ovarian Carcinoma and Its Distinct Coagulopathy Profile: Molecular Drivers and Clinical Implications
summary: Clear cell ovarian carcinoma (CCOC) is frequently associated with endometriosis and exhibits a distinctive hypercoagulable phenotype that significantly elevates the risk of venous thromboembolism compared to other ovarian cancer subtypes. This prothrombotic state is driven by multiple interconnected mechanisms: elevated tissue factor expression activates the extrinsic coagulation cascade, increasing thrombin production, fibrin accumulation, and platelet activation, while pro-inflammatory cytokines including interleukin-6, tumor necrosis factor-alpha, and vascular endothelial growth factor enhance clotting pathways and compromise vascular integrity. Additionally, endothelial dysfunction and tumor-derived microparticles further amplify the hypercoagulable condition and increase the risk of disseminated intravascular coagulation. The resulting coagulopathy is clinically significant because it not only promotes thrombotic complications and worse prognosis but also facilitates tumor growth and contributes to CCOC's inherent resistance to platinum-based chemotherapy, leading to elevated recurrence rates and reduced overall survival.

title: Bu Yang Huan Wu Decoction controls synovitis via HIF-1/VEGF signaling for osteoarthritis therapy
summary:
# Summary

Bu Yang Huan Wu Decoction (BYHWD) controls synovitis in osteoarthritis primarily through inhibition of the HIF-1α/VEGF signaling pathway, which normally becomes pathologically activated in the hypoxic microenvironment of osteoarthritic joints. The decoction's four key active compounds—astragaloside, kaempferol, formononetin, and paeoniflorin—collectively target multiple downstream pathways that drive joint inflammation and degeneration. These components inhibit PTGS2-mediated prostaglandin E2 production and pain sensitization, suppress abnormal T/B cell activation via the PTPRC pathway, and promote chondrocyte survival by regulating the IGF1R-PI3K/AKT axis. BYHWD's therapeutic action converges on four primary targets—SRC, PTGS2, PTPRC, and IGF1R—thereby interrupting inflammatory cytokine signaling, reducing matrix metalloproteinase activation, and preventing excessive chondrocyte apoptosis. These multi-target mechanisms make BYHWD effective for managing the inflammatory, metabolic, and degenerative pathologies underlying osteoarthritis.

title: Identified endoplasmic reticulum stress-related molecular cluster and immune characterization in endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Endoplasmic reticulum stress (ERS) emerges as a central pathogenic mechanism in endometriosis, involving dysregulation of the unfolded protein response through sensors IRE1α, PERK, and ATF6, which activate the pro-apoptotic transcription factor CHOP to promote cell death in endometrial lesions. Four hub genes—Von Willebrand factor, vascular cell adhesion molecule 1 (VCAM1), endothelial PAS domain protein 1 (EPAS1/HIF-2α), and coagulation factor VIII—are highly associated with endometriosis and drive cell adhesion and vascular dysfunction through ERS-mediated mechanisms. The study identified two distinct molecular clusters, with Cluster B displaying elevated expression of EPAS1, F8, and VCAM1, accompanied by an immune-enriched phenotype characterized by significantly higher infiltration of activated immune cells including B cells, CD8 T cells, and dendritic cells. The therapeutic agent dienogest effectively upregulates ERS and CHOP expression, thereby inducing apoptosis and restraining endometrial lesion proliferation, while suppressing pro-inflammatory cytokine production including IL-6. Multiple immune cell populations—including myeloid-derived suppressor cells, macrophages, mast cells, and various T cell subsets—are elevated in endometriosis compared to normal endometrium. These findings suggest that targeting ERS-mediated apoptotic pathways and modulating the aberrant immune microenvironment represent promising therapeutic strategies for endometriosis management.

title: Exploring the Genetic Role of Matrix Metalloproteinase-13 Variants in Pterygium Risk
summary: This study investigates the genetic contribution of MMP13 variants to pterygium risk, a common ocular disorder characterized by abnormal fibrovascular growth and extracellular matrix remodeling. While matrix metalloproteinases are well-established mediators of pterygium pathogenesis, the specific genetic role of MMP13 remained unexplored. The case-control analysis evaluated two MMP13 variants—promoter rs2252070 and intronic rs478927—but found neither showed significant genotypic or allelic associations with pterygium susceptibility, despite MMP13's known involvement in collagen degradation and ECM remodeling. MMP13 is part of a broader MMP network where MMP3 serves as a critical activator of pro-MMPs, including MMP13, MMP9, and MMP1, all of which are elevated in pterygium tissues and facilitate tissue restructuring through ECM degradation. The study identifies other MMP variants with stronger associations, particularly MMP1 rs1799705 2G and MMP11 rs738792 C alleles as potential biomarkers for pterygium susceptibility, while highlighting that MMP13 rs2252070 G allele has been implicated in various cancers and endometriosis but not pterygium in this investigation.

title: The methyltransferase-like proteins as core regulators of nucleic acid modifications and post-translation modification of proteins in disease pathogenesis and therapeutic implications
summary:
# Summary

The methyltransferase-like (METTL) protein family functions as central regulators of epitranscriptome modifications, serving as dynamic "writers" that catalyze RNA methylation (including m6A, m7G, and m3C modifications) and protein post-translational modifications to regulate gene expression, transcript splicing, and translation efficiency. Dysregulation of METTL proteins disrupts RNA stability and signaling pathways, leading to tumorigenesis, neurodegeneration, and immune dysfunction across multiple disease contexts. The METTL family acts as a key hub integrating epigenetics, metabolic reprogramming, and disease pathogenesis, with specific members like METTL16 maintaining metabolic homeostasis through SAM synthetase regulation and DNA repair coordination, while others such as METTL4 directly influence glucose, lipid, and testosterone metabolism. These proteins influence diverse biological processes ranging from embryonic development and cardiomyocyte remodeling to neurogenesis and tRNA structure integrity, with m6A modifications serving dual roles in RNA regulation, DNA demethylation, and chromatin accessibility. This multifunctional regulation positions the METTL family as critical therapeutic targets for addressing metabolic reprogramming and disease development across various pathophysiological contexts.

title: Clinicopathological significance of Kruppel-like factor 15 and epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition related factors in bladder cancer
summary: Kruppel-like factor 15 (KLF15) functions as a critical transcriptional regulator controlling cellular differentiation, proliferation, programmed cell death, and metabolic processes including carbohydrate, lipid, and protein metabolism. In bladder cancer, KLF15 expression is significantly reduced compared to normal tissues, and this downregulation is inversely associated with epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT) markers including ZEB1, Slug, and Snail, while positively correlated with the epithelial marker E-cadherin. The EMT pathway, which is central to bladder cancer progression and metastasis, is regulated by KLF15 through its interaction with ZEB1 and modulation of downstream factors like β-catenin, with EMT transcription factors (Snail, Slug, ZEB1) being elevated in bladder cancer tissues and associated with poor clinicopathological outcomes including lymphatic invasion and metastasis. Low KLF15 expression combined with high ZEB1 expression serves as an independent prognostic factor for bladder cancer, whereas patients with positive KLF15 expression demonstrate significantly improved overall survival. Collectively, these findings establish KLF15 as a crucial tumor suppressor in bladder cancer that restrains EMT progression and metastatic potential, making it a valuable biomarker for predicting prognosis and patient outcomes.

title: Revisiting IGF2BP1 in Endometrial Cancer: Ethnic-Specific Prognostic Implications and Association with Caspase-3 and pSTAT3
summary: IGF2BP1, an N6-methyladenosine reader protein, functions as a key regulator of oncogenic mRNA stability in endometrial cancer through context-dependent mechanisms that vary by ethnic background. While Western studies have consistently associated high IGF2BP1 expression with poor prognosis and aggressive tumor behavior, this study reveals paradoxical findings in Asian populations, where elevated IGF2BP1 correlated with better overall survival and increased apoptotic capacity through enhanced caspase-3 expression. IGF2BP1 exerts its oncogenic effects by stabilizing critical mRNAs including E2F1 to promote cell cycle progression, while simultaneously suppressing the phosphorylated STAT3 inflammatory pathway that normally drives tumor survival and therapeutic resistance. The study also identifies multiple ethnic-specific genetic alterations in endometrial cancer, including variants in AKT1, PIK3R1, and TP53, alongside metabolic dysregulation mediated through AMPK-INF2 signaling in mitochondrial fission. These findings underscore the importance of ethnic-stratified approaches to understanding IGF2BP1 biology and developing therapeutics targeting the PI3K/AKT/mTOR and STAT3 pathways in endometrial cancer.

title: Molecular mechanisms and Biological Functions of Autophagy in Endometrial Diseases
summary:
# Summary

Autophagy is a highly conserved cellular process essential for maintaining cellular homeostasis that plays a pivotal role in endometrial diseases, including endometriosis, endometritis, and endometrial cancer. Dysregulated autophagy contributes to disease pathogenesis through altered hormone signaling, inflammation, and metabolic reprogramming. The molecular machinery of autophagy is tightly controlled through a hierarchical signaling network where AMPK activates autophagy via ULK1 and Beclin1 phosphorylation during energy stress, while mTORC1 suppresses autophagy under nutrient-rich conditions through opposing phosphorylation events. Key autophagy components including the ULK1 kinase complex, PI3KC3 complexes, and the ATG12-ATG5-ATG16L1-LC3 conjugation system regulate autophagosome formation and cargo recognition, with these processes further modulated by metabolic hormones including insulin, glucagon, estrogen, and progesterone, as well as NAD+-dependent signaling through SIRT1. These findings underscore autophagy's critical integration point for diverse cellular stresses and its central role in endometrial physiology and pathophysiology, positioning autophagy modulation as a potential therapeutic target for endometrial diseases.

title: Regulator of chromosome condensation 1 and broad-complex, tramtrack and bric a brac domain-containing protein is involved in ovarian cancer growth and drug resistance
summary:
# Summary

RCBTB1, a regulator of chromosome condensation protein containing RCC1 and BTB domains, is significantly elevated in ovarian cancer cells and promotes tumor growth through activation of the Ras/ERK signaling axis. RCBTB1 facilitates this pathway by activating protein kinase C epsilon (PKCε), which degrades the negative regulator NF1, thereby sustaining Ras-GTP activation and downstream ERK phosphorylation. Silencing RCBTB1 substantially inhibits ovarian cancer cell proliferation, migration, invasion, and viability in vitro, while reducing tumor growth and increasing apoptotic markers (cleaved caspase-3) in vivo. Critically, RCBTB1 knockdown restores sensitivity to MEK inhibitors in resistant A2780 cells by elevating NF1 expression and reducing Ras-GTP levels, suggesting RCBTB1 is a key mediator of drug resistance in KRAS-mutated ovarian cancers. High RCBTB1 expression correlates with poor prognosis and enhanced immune infiltration, including increased CD4 cells, neutrophils, macrophages, and dendritic cells. These findings identify RCBTB1 as a potential therapeutic target to overcome kinase inhibitor resistance in ovarian cancer.

title: Breaking epigenetic shackles: targeting ARID1A methylation and the PI3K/AKT/mTOR-PD-L1 axis to overcome immune escape in gastric cancer
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies ARID1A promoter hypermethylation as a critical mechanism driving immune escape in gastric cancer through activation of the PI3K/AKT/mTOR-PD-L1 axis. ARID1A, which encodes a chromatin remodeling subunit, is frequently silenced by promoter hypermethylation in multiple cancer types including gastric, endometrial, and ovarian carcinomas. Loss of ARID1A function activates the PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling pathway and upregulates PD-L1, enabling tumor cells to evade immune surveillance. Treatment with the demethylating agent 5-aza-CdR restored ARID1A expression and suppressed malignant phenotypes including proliferation, invasion, and apoptosis resistance, with effects reversed by an AKT agonist confirming pathway dependency. ARID1A hypermethylation shows strong association with elevated immune checkpoint scores and PD-L1 expression, positioning ARID1A methylation status as a candidate biomarker for stratifying patients eligible for immune checkpoint blockade therapy. These findings establish a DNA methylation-driven epigenetic-immune checkpoint cascade as a potential therapeutic target in gastric cancer.

title: Targeting steroid receptor coactivators for the treatment of benign female reproductive disorders
summary:
# Steroid Receptor Coactivators in Female Reproductive Disorders

Steroid receptor coactivators (SRC-1, SRC-2, and SRC-3) play critical roles in regulating decidualization, placental development, and female fertility through their interactions with estrogen and progesterone signaling pathways. SRC-2 promotes glycolytic reprogramming during decidualization by upregulating PFKFB3 and fructose-2,6-bisphosphate, while also enhancing trophoblastic development through Wnt9A induction, mechanisms essential for pregnancy establishment. In endometriosis, SRC-1 becomes pathologically elevated and generates a proteolytically cleaved 70 kDa isoform that inhibits apoptosis and promotes epithelial-mesenchymal transition by upregulating EMT markers, thereby facilitating lesion progression. SRC dysregulation is broadly associated with benign female reproductive disorders: SRC-1 levels are abnormally elevated in endometriotic lesions and ovarian endometriosis, while SRC-2 and SRC-3 are upregulated in polycystic ovary syndrome endometrium and in 40-50% of uterine fibroids. Knockout studies demonstrate that SRC-2 and SRC-3 deletion significantly reduces endometriosis lesion size, suggesting that targeting these coactivators represents a viable therapeutic strategy for treating multiple reproductive pathologies driven by steroid hormone dysregulation.

title: Role of ubiquitin-proteasome system in preeclampsia (Review)
summary:
# Summary

Preeclampsia is a pregnancy disorder characterized by hypertension and proteinuria that results from abnormal placentation, immune dysfunction, and endothelial injury, with dysregulation of the ubiquitin-proteasome system emerging as a critical pathogenic mechanism. The ubiquitination cascade, mediated by E1, E2, and E3 ligase enzymes, normally regulates trophoblast function and placental development, but aberrant ubiquitination impairs trophoblast invasion, fusion, and survival in preeclampsia. Multiple E3 ligases drive disease pathogenesis: STK40 is abnormally elevated and suppresses trophoblast fusion through P57Kip2 degradation, NEDD4 promotes trophoblast dysfunction via TAK1 ubiquitination (normally inhibited by thrombospondin-1), and β-TrCP degradation of Snail and VEGFR2 impairs trophoblast migration and angiogenesis. Additionally, microRNAs including miR-218-5p and miR-135a-5p regulate these pathways, and their downregulation in preeclampsia compromises trophoblast function through increased UBE3A and β-TrCP activity. Notably, upregulation of DUXAP8 activates the AKT/mTOR pathway while inhibiting autophagy, further disrupting cellular homeostasis. Therapeutic interventions targeting necroptosis inhibitors, miRNA agonists, and GSK-3β inhibitors show promise in reversing these ubiquitin-proteasome dysregulation-mediated pathogenic mechanisms.

title: Calcium Homeostasis Machinery in the Human Uterus—A Potential Therapeutic Target in Endometrial Cancer
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that dysregulated calcium homeostasis is a critical driver of endometrial cancer progression through altered expression of multiple calcium-handling proteins. Estrogen upregulates calcium channels including CACNA1D, CACNA2D1, TRPV2, and TRPV4 through estrogen receptors and GPER signaling, leading to excessive calcium influx that promotes cancer cell proliferation, migration, and metastasis via activation of the RhoA/ROCK1 pathway and disruption of apoptotic mechanisms. Conversely, TRPV1 activation and elevated TRPM4 levels promote apoptosis and correlate with favorable prognosis, suggesting selective calcium channel targeting may offer therapeutic benefit. Elevated serum ionized calcium correlates with advanced cancer stage and histological grade, while calcium influx also disrupts mitochondrial function and autophagy pathways, compounding cancer cell survival advantages. The authors propose that calcium homeostasis represents a promising therapeutic target, with calcium channel inhibitors showing potential to restore cellular calcium balance, suppress metastatic behavior, and induce apoptosis in endometrial cancer cells.

title: Alternative Splicing of SORBS1 Affects Neuromuscular Junction Integrity in Myotonic Dystrophy Type 1
journal: Journal of Cachexia, Sarcopenia and Muscle
summary:
# Summary

Myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1) arises from CTG repeat expansion in the DMPK gene, which sequesters MBNL RNA-binding proteins and triggers widespread splicing defects contributing to muscle weakness and myotonia. This study identifies SORBS1 exon 25 as a critical MBNL-regulated splicing target whose misregulation is central to DM1 pathophysiology; in human congenital DM1 muscle, SORBS1 exon 25 inclusion is reduced by over 50% compared to controls and remains aberrantly low throughout development. Mechanistically, forced exclusion of Sorbs1 exon 25 in mice and zebrafish caused neuromuscular junction degeneration, increased denervation, postsynaptic destabilization, and impaired acetylcholine receptor clustering—effects driven by SORBS1's role in cytoskeleton modulation through interactions with paxillin, talin, and vinculin. Progressive ultrastructural analysis revealed that Sorbs1 exon 25 misregulation induces multiple pathological changes at the neuromuscular junction including reduced post-junctional folds, terminal Schwann cell invasion, enlarged synaptic clefts, and progressive loss of acetylcholine receptor area. These findings establish SORBS1 alternative splicing dysregulation as a key mechanism through which MBNL sequestration compromises neuromuscular junction integrity and contributes to DM1 motor dysfunction.

title: Identification of embryonic yield-related serum biomarkers using proteomic analysis in ovine superovulation
summary:
# Summary

Proteomic analysis of ovine serum identified multiple biomarkers predictive of embryonic yield following superovulation. In spontaneously estrous ewes, ACAA1, B2MG, and ADAMTS13 showed significant positive correlation with embryonic yield, while ADAMTS13 was negatively correlated, indicating these proteins as potential predictive markers. B2MG emerged as a key differentially expressed protein in both spontaneous and induced estrus groups, with elevated levels in high embryonic yield populations. The identified biomarkers are functionally involved in fatty acid metabolism (particularly ACAA1-mediated β-oxidation), glucose and fructose metabolism, and oxidative stress responses, all critical for oocyte and embryonic development. These metabolic pathways—including unsaturated fatty acid metabolism, amino acid metabolism, and PI3K-Akt signaling—were significantly enriched among differentially expressed proteins between high and low embryonic yield populations. These findings suggest that serum biomarker profiling reflecting enhanced metabolic efficiency and antioxidant capacity can predict reproductive outcomes in assisted reproductive techniques.

title: Biology of PEST-containing nuclear proteins as potential targets in ovarian cancer (Review)
summary: PEST-containing nuclear proteins (PEST-NPs) emerge as critical regulators of ovarian cancer progression through their control of protein stability and cellular signaling pathways. The PEST amino acid sequence functions as a recognition motif for the ubiquitin-proteasome degradation machinery and post-translational modifications, with phosphorylation, ubiquitination, and SUMOylation governing the stability, activation, and intracellular localization of these proteins. p53, a core PEST-NP, plays a pivotal role in cancer development, where its ubiquitination status directly dictates ovarian cancer cell proliferation and apoptosis; however, p53 mutations disrupt this critical post-translational regulation and drive chemoresistance. PEST-NPs regulate multiple oncogenic pathways including PI3K/mTOR and MAPK signaling, cell cycle control, and metabolic processes, while ovarian cancer cells upregulate antioxidant systems such as NRF2, NAD(P)H oxidoreductase 1, and the SLC7A11/xCT antiporter to prevent chemotherapy-induced reactive oxygen species and develop platinum resistance. Additionally, transcription factors like Myc and NF-κB, which contain PEST domains and are regulated by phosphorylation-dependent degradation, drive uncontrolled cancer growth and metastasis when dysregulated. Targeting PEST-NPs and their post-translational modifications represents a promising therapeutic strategy to overcome platinum resistance and improve treatment outcomes in ovarian cancer.

title: Vascular endothelial generating factor pathway in ovarian cancer
summary: This study elucidates the critical role of the VEGF signaling pathway in ovarian cancer progression through multiple interconnected mechanisms. VEGF and its family members (particularly VEGF-A, VEGF-C, and VEGF-D) promote neo-angiogenesis and lymphangiogenesis by binding to their cognate receptors (VEGFR-1, VEGFR-2, and VEGFR-3) on endothelial cells, facilitating tumor vascularization and lymphatic spread. Beyond angiogenesis, VEGF signaling drives invasion, metastatic dissemination, and chemoresistance through activation of the PI3K/AKT/mTOR and ERK pathways, which converge on the nucleus to promote DNA replication, cell proliferation, and survival. High VEGF expression independently predicts malignant ascites formation and correlates strongly with advanced disease, invasiveness, and poor chemotherapy response, making it a robust prognostic marker. The pathway is further amplified by co-receptors neuropilins (NRP1 and NRP2) that enhance VEGF-receptor binding, while endogenous inhibitors such as soluble VEGFR-1 and sVEGFR-2 can competitively suppress VEGF activity. These findings establish the VEGF pathway as a central driver of ovarian cancer progression and identify multiple therapeutic targets for intervention.

title: Comprehensive analysis of the BID gene to uncover the role of novel alternative splicing isoforms in colon adenocarcinoma: a systematic review of literature and bioinformatics analysis
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive analysis elucidates the multifaceted role of the BID gene and its alternatively spliced isoforms in colon adenocarcinoma (COAD) development and progression. BID functions as a critical link between extrinsic and intrinsic apoptotic pathways, mediating death receptor signaling through Fas/FasL and Apo2/TRAIL pathways, which activate death receptors DR4 and DR5 leading to caspase-8 activation and the cleavage of full-length BID into its active truncated form (tBID). The activated tBID translocates to mitochondria where it activates pro-apoptotic proteins BAX and BAK, triggering mitochondrial outer membrane permeabilization, cytochrome c release, and ultimately apoptosis through caspase-9 activation. Notably, specific BID isoforms (BID-L, BID-EL, and BID-S) are significantly upregulated in COAD tissue, whereas BID expression is paradoxically reduced in advanced-stage and node-positive disease and inversely correlates with tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes, suggesting complex prognostic implications. Under hypoxic conditions, HIF-1-mediated downregulation of BID contributes to reduced drug-induced apoptosis and therapeutic resistance, with microRNAs hsa-miR-194-3p and hsa-miR-149-5p identified as potential regulators of BID expression in COAD.

title: Non-canonical splice variant of PFKFB4 in hepatocellular carcinoma activates AKT through direct interaction
summary:
# Summary

A non-canonical splice variant of PFKFB4 (PFKFB4-ΔEx6), generated through exon 6 skipping, emerges as a critical driver of hepatocellular carcinoma progression. This variant directly binds to and phosphorylates AKT, activating the PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling pathway independent of its canonical glycolytic functions, as perturbation of PFKFB4-ΔEx6 levels did not significantly impact glucose uptake or glycolysis. Functionally, PFKFB4-ΔEx6 expression dramatically enhanced HCC cell proliferation, migration, and in vivo tumorigenicity compared to full-length PFKFB4, while knockdown induced cell death. Clinically, PFKFB4-ΔEx6 upregulation correlated with poor overall survival and sensitized HCC cells to mTOR inhibitor everolimus, positioning this variant as both a prognostic biomarker and therapeutic target. These findings reveal an unexpected protein kinase function of PFKFB4 that bypasses metabolic reprogramming to directly promote oncogenic signaling in HCC.

title: Multiple entanglements of different cell death pathways, in which caspase-8 and BID interact with cardiolipin*, have been identified
journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
summary:
# Summary

This article elucidates how a cardiolipin/caspase-8/BID activation platform at the mitochondrial outer membrane coordinates multiple forms of cell death including apoptosis, ferroptosis, and necroptosis. Upon Fas receptor ligation, caspase-8 is recruited to the death-inducing signaling complex (DISC) where it becomes activated and subsequently binds cardiolipin, creating a platform for further signaling amplification. Caspase-8 then cleaves BID to generate truncated BID (tBID), which inserts into the mitochondrial outer membrane via cardiolipin interactions and activates BAX/BAK oligomerization to induce mitochondrial outer membrane permeabilization and cytochrome c release. This intrinsic pathway activation triggers the apoptosome-mediated cascade of executioner caspases, while simultaneously causing electron transfer chain inhibition that generates reactive oxygen species critical for BAX oligomerization. Cardiolipin is essential for this process, as cardiolipin-deficient cells show enhanced apoptosis with rapid membrane potential collapse, highlighting the phospholipid's central role in integrating bioenergetic stress with cell death pathways during mitochondrial dysfunction.

title: The Pseudomonas aeruginosa effector protein TesG regulates alternative activation of macrophages through NLRC5
summary:
# Summary

Pseudomonas aeruginosa's TesG effector protein promotes alternative M2 polarization of macrophages through upregulation of the NLRC5 protein, which is required for this immunomodulatory effect. TesG increases NLRC5 expression in macrophages, and NLRC5 subsequently suppresses inflammatory responses by inhibiting NF-κB and interferon signaling pathways through interactions with IKK proteins. By shifting macrophage polarization toward the M2 phenotype, which promotes tissue repair and remodeling rather than inflammation, TesG facilitates bacterial immune evasion and persistence during chronic P. aeruginosa lung infections. The TesG-NLRC5 axis also impairs neutrophil recruitment and cytokine production through suppression of RhoGEF-Rho GTPase signaling. This mechanism represents a pathogenic strategy whereby the bacteria skew the host immune response toward an immunosuppressive state that enables chronic infection while preventing excessive tissue damage.

title: ZMIZ1 and estrogen receptor α form an essential partnership in endometrial biology
summary: ZMIZ1 functions as an essential coregulator of estrogen receptor α (ESR1) that coordinates critical endometrial processes required for reproductive function. In the endometrium, ZMIZ1 acts as a ligand-dependent amplifier of ESR1-driven transcription, selectively enhancing estrogen-responsive genes including cell cycle regulators CCNA2 and FOXM1 that are necessary for stromal proliferation, decidualization, and the proliferative-to-decidual transition fundamental to implantation. ZMIZ1 deficiency impairs this ESR1/E2F signaling interface while simultaneously enhancing SMAD/TGF-β signaling, leading to suppressed epithelial and stromal proliferation, enhanced fibrotic remodeling, and disruption of progesterone receptor expression. Clinically, reduced ZMIZ1 expression in women with endometriosis is associated with impaired stromal progesterone receptor activity and progesterone resistance, while ZMIZ1 deficiency in mouse models causes infertility, accelerated fibrosis, and compromised endometrial integrity. These findings position ZMIZ1 as a critical molecular integrator that balances proliferative and fibrotic pathways to safeguard uterine plasticity, with potential therapeutic relevance as both a biomarker and treatment target for endometriosis and endometrial pathologies.

title: Vasorin at the crossroads: charting new paths in preeclampsia research
summary:
# Summary

This review examines vasorin (VASN) as a regulatory protein with emerging relevance to preeclampsia pathophysiology, though the article title addresses preeclampsia while the extracted relationships reveal broader reproductive and metabolic significance. Preeclampsia develops through placental dysfunction and ischemia, which trigger syncytiotrophoblast injury and release of pathogenic extracellular vesicles (EVs) into maternal circulation. These EVs mediate endothelial dysfunction and vascular damage through multiple mechanisms, including delivery of anti-angiogenic factors; notably, vasorin deficiency in plasma EVs from severe preeclampsia patients contributes to this endothelial dysfunction. Vasorin functions as a TGF-β pathway inhibitor through its soluble extracellular domain, reduces reactive oxygen species generation, and regulates processes including ovulation and antral follicle survival through TGF-β modulation. The article also highlights that chronic oxidative stress—driven by excessive ROS production—contributes to endometriosis and other female reproductive disorders, positioning vasorin's ROS-reducing properties as potentially protective in reproductive pathologies. These findings suggest vasorin represents a key nexus between placental dysfunction, vascular homeostasis, and reproductive metabolic regulation across both normal and pathological pregnancy states.

title: Epigenetics in Ovarian Cancer: A Review of Current Knowledge and Future Perspectives
summary:
# Summary

This review synthesizes current knowledge of epigenetic mechanisms in ovarian cancer development and progression. Multiple epigenetic modifications—including DNA methylation, histone modifications, chromatin remodeling, and non-coding RNAs—collectively drive ovarian cancer pathogenesis by altering expression of tumor suppressors and oncogenes. DNA methylation silences critical DNA repair genes like BRCA1/2 and regulators of cell cycle progression, while hypomethylation of proto-oncogenes such as BORIS and IGF-2 promotes malignant transformation; notably, HOXA10 promoter hypomethylation in high-grade serous ovarian cancer correlates with poor survival. Histone modifications, particularly through histone deacetylases (HDACs), promote tumor progression by suppressing E-cadherin, activating epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition, and enhancing survival signaling pathways including PI3K/Akt and RAS, while EZH2-mediated H3K27 trimethylation drives cell proliferation and fibroblast activation. Non-coding RNAs including microRNAs (miR-9, miR-205, miR-96) and long non-coding RNAs regulate tumor cell behavior and immunosuppression. These findings support therapeutic targeting of epigenetic regulators such as DNMT inhibitors, HDAC inhibitors, and EZH2 antagonists as promising strategies for ovarian cancer treatment, with particular relevance for endometriosis-associated ovarian cancers harboring PTEN and ARID1A mutations.

title: The Roles of Sirt1 in Breast and Gynecologic Malignancies
summary:
# Summary

SIRT1, a NAD+-dependent deacetylase, plays a multifaceted role in regulating critical cellular processes including DNA repair, cell survival, and stress response through deacetylation of histone proteins (H3K9, H3K14, H3K56, H4K16, H2AX, H1K26) and non-histone targets such as p53, FOXO, and NF-κB. In gynecological and breast malignancies, particularly triple-negative breast cancer and ovarian cancer, SIRT1 promotes tumor progression by inhibiting p53 and FOXO1-mediated apoptosis, thereby suppressing pro-apoptotic genes like Bax and p21 while simultaneously facilitating epithelial-mesenchymal transition, metastasis, and chemoresistance. SIRT1 also suppresses anti-inflammatory signaling by deacetylating the p65 subunit of NF-κB, reducing transcription of genes encoding IL-6, TNF-α, and COX-2, and interacts with inflammasome regulators NLRP3 and ASC. Pharmacological SIRT1 inhibitors such as EX-527 and cambinol restore chemosensitivity, whereas activators like resveratrol and BML-278 enhance histone hypoacetylation and alter transcriptional patterns, suggesting SIRT1 modulation as a potential therapeutic target in cancer management.

title: ‘‘Non-Invasive Extracellular Vesicle Biomarkers in Endometriosis, Molecular Signatures Linking Pelvic Inflammation, Oocyte Quality, and IVF Outcomes’’
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis impairs fertility through multiple interconnected mechanisms involving extracellular vesicles (EVs) as central mediators of pathological signaling. The disease generates oxidative stress and excessive cytokine production that damage oocyte competence through impaired granulosa-cumulus signaling, while simultaneously promoting progesterone resistance and extracellular matrix remodeling that compromise endometrial receptivity. Endometriotic implants act as secretory centers where hypoxia and inflammatory signals activate HIF-1α, NF-κB, and AP-1 to increase EV generation; these vesicles transport specific microRNA signatures (miR-22-3p, miR-320a, miR-200 family, miR-145-5p) that either support or—in endometriosis—suppress reproductive outcomes by modulating critical pathways including STAT3/SOCS1, PI3K/AKT, and NOTCH signaling. EV-derived miRNAs also enhance neuro-immune connections through TRPV1 nociceptor sensitization and promote pathological angiogenesis and fibrosis via altered metabolic programming and collagen deposition. Non-invasive EV biomarkers from serum, menstrual blood, and uterine fluid can differentiate endometriosis-associated inflammatory-fibrotic signatures and abnormal receptivity programs, offering diagnostic and prognostic tools for predicting implantation outcomes and guiding IVF management in endometriosis patients.

title: Glycolytic Reprogramming in Uterine Fibroids: Genetic, Transcriptomic, Proteomic, and Metabolomic Insights
summary:
# Summary

Uterine fibroids are characterized by glycolytic reprogramming, a metabolic shift that supports fibroid growth and fibrotic phenotype development. Genetic mutations in genes such as MED12 and fumarate hydratase (FH) drive this metabolic rewiring; FH mutations are particularly important as they stabilize HIF-1α by preventing its degradation, leading to upregulation of glucose transporters (GLUT1, GLUT4) and glycolytic enzymes (hexokinase II, pyruvate kinase M2). Enhanced glycolysis activates transforming growth factor-beta and Wnt/β-catenin signaling while providing biosynthetic precursors for nucleotide, amino acid, and lipid synthesis necessary for fibroblast proliferation and collagen production. Additional metabolic regulators including mTOR, AMPK, and PI3K/Akt pathways modulate glycolytic flux and contribute to the fibrotic phenotype, which is clinically manifested as heavy menstrual bleeding, pelvic pain, and abdominal bloating. This metabolic reprogramming toward aerobic glycolysis mirrors patterns observed in other fibrotic diseases including pulmonary, renal, and cardiac fibrosis, suggesting glycolytic metabolism as a unifying mechanism in fibrotic pathology.

title: Regulatory roles of circular RNAs in poultry diseases: mechanisms and research advances
summary:
# Summary

Circular RNAs (circRNAs) are emerging as critical regulators of poultry disease pathogenesis, functioning through multiple molecular mechanisms including microRNA sponging, protein scaffolding, and transcriptional regulation. These highly stable, non-coding RNA molecules are differentially expressed in infected tissues and demonstrate significant involvement in immune responses to viral pathogens such as Marek's disease virus, avian leukosis virus, and Newcastle disease virus, as well as parasitic infections like Eimeria tenella. Specific circRNAs exhibit disease-promoting or protective functions—for example, circRUNX2.2 promotes lymphoma cell proliferation in Marek's disease by activating RUNX2, while circ-EZH2 inhibits Newcastle disease virus replication. Beyond infectious disease, circRNAs regulate metabolic processes including lipid metabolism, myoblast proliferation and differentiation, and stress responses, with circFOXO3 notably inhibiting cellular senescence through interactions with stress-associated proteins. The stability and tissue-specific expression patterns of circRNAs position them as promising biomarkers for early disease detection and monitoring, offering new therapeutic targets for managing avian diseases and potentially translatable insights for other mammalian disease models.

title: Host angiogenic reprogramming by Echinococcus multilocularis protoscoleces protein via PDGFR/PI3K/AKT cascade
summary:
# Summary

Echinococcus multilocularis infection causes alveolar echinococcosis characterized by tumor-like liver growth and pathological angiogenesis, a process essential for parasite survival and lesion expansion. The parasite's protoscoleces protein (EmP) directly induces angiogenesis by activating the PDGFR/PI3K/AKT/FAK signaling cascade, mimicking endogenous PDGF-BB signaling to enhance endothelial cell proliferation, migration, and tube formation. In infected tissues, multiple angiogenesis-related genes including VEGF, PDGF-BB, Angpt1, Id1, and Lyve1 are significantly upregulated, while PDGFR-β expression increases in proximity to lesions, establishing a pro-angiogenic microenvironment. Pharmacological inhibition of PDGFR, PI3K, AKT, or FAK effectively blocks EmP-induced endothelial responses and vascular formation, demonstrating that this signaling axis is both necessary and sufficient for parasite-driven angiogenesis. These findings parallel angiogenic mechanisms in other parasitic infections and suggest that targeting the PDGFR pathway may represent a therapeutic strategy for controlling E. multilocularis-associated vascular pathology.

title: Research progress on m6A RNA methylation modification in human reproduction related diseases
summary:
# Summary

m6A RNA methylation, catalyzed by writer proteins including METTL3, METTL14, and METTL16, and reversed by erasers such as ALKBH5 and FTO, is a critical epigenetic regulator of multiple reproductive processes including spermatogenesis, oogenesis, embryo implantation, and parturition. Dysregulation of this modification is associated with a spectrum of reproductive disorders in both sexes, including asthenozoospermia, idiopathic azoospermia, premature ovarian insufficiency, polycystic ovary syndrome, endometriosis, and spontaneous abortion. In male fertility specifically, elevated m6A methylation mediated by METTL3 and METTL14 impairs sperm motility and quality through mechanisms involving PPM1A translation enhancement and subsequent AMPK inhibition, which suppresses protective autophagy pathways during testosterone synthesis in Leydig cells. Genetic variations in FTO are associated with declined semen quality, while m6A methylation inhibitors such as STM2457 show therapeutic potential in reversing spermatogenic disorders. These findings underscore m6A methylation as a central regulatory mechanism in reproductive health with implications for understanding and treating multiple infertility conditions and related disorders.

title: The role of mitophagy in female reproductive system diseases: from molecular mechanisms to therapeutic strategies
summary:
# Summary

Mitophagy, the selective autophagy of mitochondria, plays critical roles across multiple stages of female reproduction, including follicle development, oocyte maturation, fertilization, and embryo implantation. The pathway is initiated when PINK1 phosphorylates ubiquitin and recruits Parkin to damaged mitochondria, where autophagy receptors (OPTN, NDP52, p62, NBR1, TAX1BP1) bind ubiquitin and interact with LC3 to direct mitochondria into autophagosomes. Dysregulation of mitophagy is implicated in pathogenesis of endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, primary ovarian insufficiency, and ovarian aging, with FSH playing a central regulatory role that suppresses excessive mitophagy under normoxic conditions while activating it during hypoxia. Key signaling nodes include the Gas6-mediated pathway essential for oocyte maturation, SIRT family proteins (SIRT1 and SIRT5) that regulate mitochondrial quality control and meiotic spindle assembly, and FSH-activated PI3K-AKT-MTOR signaling that downregulates autophagy and promotes estradiol production. These findings establish mitophagy as a fundamental mechanism linking mitochondrial homeostasis to reproductive function and female reproductive disease pathogenesis.

title: Clotrimazole as a new frontier: Drug repurposing and its efficacy in cancer therapy
summary: Clotrimazole, an antifungal agent repurposed for cancer therapy, exerts potent anticancer effects through multiple mechanistic pathways targeting metabolic and signaling networks critical to tumor survival. The drug primarily disrupts cancer cell energy metabolism by inhibiting key glycolytic enzymes phosphofructokinase and hexokinase, thereby reducing glucose absorption and ATP production while simultaneously blocking calcium-dependent potassium channels to disrupt cellular ion homeostasis. Clotrimazole suppresses oncogenic signaling cascades including PI3K/Akt, ERK, AMPK/mTOR, and Wnt/β-catenin pathways while inhibiting matrix metalloproteinase-9 and membrane receptors such as EGFR, collectively impairing cancer cell invasion, epithelial-mesenchymal transition, and metastatic potential. Through these combined effects, clotrimazole induces G1-phase cell cycle arrest, upregulates pro-apoptotic factors, and triggers mitochondrial dysfunction via permeability transition pore interactions, ultimately promoting cancer cell apoptosis. Demonstrating broad-spectrum efficacy across multiple cancer types including lung, colorectal, breast, endometrial, hepatic, and melanoma models both in vitro and in vivo, clotrimazole also enhances chemotherapy sensitivity, positioning it as a promising therapeutic candidate for cancer treatment through metabolic reprogramming.

title: Ferredoxins: master regulators in mitochondrial redox homeostasis and programmed cell death
summary: Ferredoxins (FDX1 and FDX2) are mitochondrial iron-sulfur proteins that function as master regulators of cellular redox homeostasis and programmed cell death pathways. FDX1 primarily mediates steroid hormone biosynthesis, protein lipoylation, and copper redox cycling through interactions with cytochrome P450 enzymes and the LIAS complex, while FDX2 drives iron-sulfur cluster assembly and maturation through its interaction with the NFS1 complex and other Fe-S biogenesis factors. Both proteins acquire electrons from NADPH via the reductase FDXR and participate in essential metabolic processes including oxidative phosphorylation and the tricarboxylic acid cycle. Dysregulation of ferredoxins disrupts mitochondrial integrity and redox balance, triggering multiple cell death pathways including apoptosis, ferroptosis, cuproptosis, and autophagy; notably, FDX2 impairment leads to iron-sulfur cluster collapse and iron overload-driven ferroptosis, while FDX1-mediated copper reduction under conditions of copper overload triggers cuproptosis through aggregation of lipoylated proteins. These findings establish ferredoxins as critical nodes integrating metabolic homeostasis with cell death decisions, with therapeutic implications for diseases involving redox imbalance and metal dysregulation.

title: Comparison of asprosin immunoreactivity in endometrial hyperplasia and grade-1 endometrial adenocarcinoma: A retrospective case-control study
journal: Turkish Journal of Obstetrics and Gynecology
summary:
# Summary

This study investigated asprosin, a glucogenic adipokine produced primarily by white adipose tissue, as a potential biomarker for distinguishing endometrial pathology. Asprosin activates the G protein-cAMP-PKA pathway to regulate blood glucose and is widely distributed across metabolic tissues including liver, skeletal muscle, heart, and lung. The researchers found that asprosin immunoreactivity was significantly elevated in grade I endometrial adenocarcinoma compared to normal proliferative endometrium, whereas immunoreactivity in endometrial hyperplasia (both atypical and non-atypical variants) remained similar to normal tissue. The carcinogenic effects of asprosin appear mediated through multiple pathways: it binds Toll-like receptor 4 (TLR4) to activate proinflammatory signaling and also engages PTPRD, whose downregulation in endometrial cancer promotes cell proliferation and migration. Additionally, unopposed estrogen signaling and obesity—both linked to elevated asprosin production from adipose tissue—represent key risk factors for endometrial cancer progression. These findings suggest asprosin immunoreactivity may serve as a useful marker to differentiate grade I endometrial adenocarcinoma from benign endometrial hyperplasia, with potential therapeutic implications for metformin-based interventions that reduce circulating asprosin levels.

title: GalNAc in vaginal secretions: a potential non-invasive risk indicator associated with the risk of premature rupture of membranes
summary: This study identifies reduced N-acetyl-D-galactosamine (GalNAc) in vaginal secretions as a non-invasive biomarker for premature rupture of membranes (PROM), a major risk factor for preterm birth. The researchers developed and validated a colorimetric assay demonstrating that GalNAc levels were significantly lower in PROM cases (AUC 0.821), with excellent diagnostic performance. Mechanistically, GalNAc depletion disrupts the galectin-3 signaling pathway, which normally maintains fetal membrane integrity through regulation of epithelial-mesenchymal homeostasis, cell-matrix adhesion, and glycocalyx architecture. GalNAc supplementation in cell models upregulates galectin-3 and galectin-1 expression, enhancing epithelial repair and reducing inflammation. The authors propose that GalNAc-related glycocalyx remodeling serves as a biochemical precursor to membrane rupture, offering an earlier detection window compared to current diagnostics relying on amniotic fluid proteins like IGFBP-1. This work bridges glycosylation biology and reproductive immunology to establish GalNAc quantification as a predictive signature for subclinical membrane vulnerability.

title: Sirtuin 1 and Sirtuin 2 Gene Expressions in Colorectal Cancers
journal: Journal of Clinical Practice and Research
summary:
# Summary

This study examined sirtuin expression patterns in colorectal cancer and their associations with oncogenic mutations and clinicopathological features. SIRT1 expression was significantly associated with K-RAS mutations and showed elevated levels in advanced tumors, lymph node involvement, and metastatic cases, correlating with poorer survival outcomes. SIRT2 expression exhibited an inverse relationship with disease progression, showing significantly lower levels in cancer specimens compared to normal tissue and associations with earlier TNM stages and reduced metastasis, while SIRT2 inhibition restricted tumor angiogenesis and reduced drug resistance in KRAS-mutant cancers. Co-expression of both SIRT1 and SIRT2 demonstrated significant correlations with TNM stage, metastasis, and RAS mutations, suggesting their combined roles in colorectal cancer progression. These findings indicate divergent roles for these NAD+-dependent histone deacetylases in cancer biology, with SIRT1 functioning as a potential oncogenic driver and SIRT2 acting as a tumor suppressor, highlighting their potential as prognostic markers and therapeutic targets in colorectal cancer.

title: UBE2S-mediated deubiquitination of GLUT1 via USP10 regulates glucose metabolic reprogramming and immune microenvironment to promote fibrosis in endometriosis
summary: This study reveals a molecular axis linking glucose metabolism to fibrosis in endometriosis through protein ubiquitination. UBE2S interacts with USP10 to mediate deubiquitination of GLUT1, stabilizing the glucose transporter and enhancing glycolytic activity in ectopic endometrial stromal cells. The resulting lactate accumulation acts as an immunoregulatory metabolite that drives M2 macrophage polarization and TGF-β1 secretion, which together promote fibroblast-to-myofibroblast transition and accelerate lesion fibrosis. Notably, inhibition of this axis with the UBE2S inhibitor cephalomannine suppressed glycolysis, blocked M2 polarization, and reduced fibrosis, suggesting therapeutic potential. These findings demonstrate how metabolic reprogramming and immune dysregulation—two hallmark features of endometriosis—are mechanistically connected through the UBE2S–USP10–GLUT1 axis, offering a potential target for treating endometriosis-associated pain and fibrosis.

title: Lipidomic profiling of endometrial cancer using desorption electrospray ionization mass spectrometry imaging
journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
summary:
# Summary

This study employed desorption electrospray ionization mass spectrometry imaging (DESI-MSI) to characterize the lipidomic landscape of endometrial cancer and identify potential biomarkers. The analysis revealed that multiple phospholipid species, particularly phosphatidylethanolamine (PE), phosphatidic acid (PA), phosphatidylserine (PS), and phosphatidylinositol (PI), are significantly elevated in endometrial cancer tissues compared to controls, with specific species such as PE(O-38:4), PA(36:2), and PE(32:1) emerging as discriminatory features. Notably, PE(O-38:4) was identified as a potential biomarker capable of detecting cancer risk in histologically benign endometrial tissue, suggesting its clinical utility for early detection. Proteomic analysis revealed that these phospholipid alterations correlate with dysregulation of key signaling pathways including PI3K/AKT/mTOR, MAPK/RAS, and Wnt signaling, with elevated B-catenin and downregulation of multiple MAPK pathway proteins such as C-Raf and CREB in cancer cases. The strong correlation between specific phospholipids and downstream signaling proteins suggests that aberrant lipid metabolism directly fuels the malignant phenotype through activation of oncogenic pathways. These findings establish lipid profiling as a promising diagnostic and prognostic approach for endometrial cancer while illuminating the mechanistic link between metabolic reprogramming and cancer development.

title: Nano-Drug Delivery Systems Targeting MMPs: A Promising Treatment for Gliomas
summary:
# Summary

Matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs), particularly MMP-2 and MMP-9, are central drivers of glioblastoma progression through multiple interconnected mechanisms. Elevated MMP expression in glioma tissues promotes tumor microenvironment remodeling, angiogenesis, invasion, and metastasis by degrading extracellular matrix components and cleaving key signaling molecules including laminin-5, TGF-β, and IL-6 receptor.
