MMP activation is regulated through several converging pathways: the FAK/Src/JNK axis, the RAS/RAF/MEK/ERK cascade leading to AP-1 activation, the JAK/STAT3 pathway, and the PI3K/AKT/HIF-1α pathway under hypoxic conditions. These MMPs facilitate glioma progression by enhancing endothelial cell mobility, increasing pro-angiogenic factor release, modulating tumor cell proliferation signaling, and promoting invasion through MMP-2 interactions with α5β1 integrin and IL-6/STAT3 survival signaling. Nano-drug delivery systems targeting MMP inhibition represent a promising therapeutic strategy, with compounds like demethoxycurcumin showing potential to reduce glioblastoma invasion through PI3K/AKT pathway inhibition, offering hope for overcoming the treatment resistance and poor prognosis characteristic of this aggressive malignancy.

title: The immunotherapy era in ovarian clear cell carcinoma: current evidence and future perspective
summary:
# Summary: Immunotherapy in Ovarian Clear Cell Carcinoma

Ovarian clear cell carcinoma (OCCC) frequently arises from malignant transformation of endometriosis and is characterized by recurrent mutations in ARID1A (50% of cases) and PIK3CA, which collectively hyperactivate the PI3K/Akt/mTOR pathway to drive cellular proliferation and chemoresistance. ARID1A loss impairs interferon signaling and increases HDAC6 expression, promoting tumor invasion and immune evasion, while ARID1A and PIK3CA mutations synergistically elevate IL-6, which maintains JAK/STAT3 signaling and induces HIF-1α nuclear translocation to enhance glycogen accumulation and metabolic adaptation. The metabolic reprogramming is further supported by HNF-1β-driven glycolysis and is complemented by VEGF overexpression and angiogenesis through Akt/mTOR signaling. OCCC tumors exhibit upregulation of immune checkpoint molecules (PD-1, PD-L1, LAG-3) and reduced ferroptosis sensitivity through PI3K/Akt pathway activation, creating a hostile immunological microenvironment. MSI-high OCCC tumors show increased tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes and PD-L1 expression, positioning immune checkpoint inhibitors as promising therapeutic targets to overcome the immune evasion mechanisms that drive poor clinical outcomes.

title: Targeting synthetic lethality: an effective therapeutic approach in ovarian and endometrial cancers
summary:
# Summary

This article examines synthetic lethality as a therapeutic strategy for ovarian and endometrial cancers by exploiting specific genetic vulnerabilities. Ovarian and endometrial cancers frequently harbor mutations in BRCA, PTEN, ARID1A, and TP53, which create targetable dependencies for selective drug combinations. PARP inhibitors effectively treat BRCA-mutated cancers by preventing single-strand break repair and triggering accumulation of lethal double-strand breaks, while ATR and WEE1 inhibitors provide alternative approaches for ARID1A- and TP53-mutated tumors respectively. The authors emphasize that hypoxic tumor microenvironments suppress homologous recombination repair through downregulation of RAD51 and BRCA proteins, enhancing sensitivity to PARP inhibitors through hypoxia-induced synthetic lethality. Resistance mechanisms including secondary mutations and activation of pro-survival pathways (RAS, PI3K/AKT) emerge during treatment, which may be overcome through combination strategies such as ATR inhibitors with PARP inhibitors. This synthetic lethality approach represents a paradigm shift in exploiting cancer-specific genetic dependencies to improve therapeutic efficacy in gynecological malignancies.

title: A New Look at the Role of Radiation-Related Epigenetic Mechanisms in Diagnosis and Anticancer Therapies
summary:
# Summary

This review examines how radiation-induced epigenetic modifications drive cancer progression and therapeutic resistance, with implications for improving anticancer treatments. Ionizing radiation triggers epigenetic changes through multiple pathways: direct DNA double-strand breaks and indirect generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) that induce DNA methylation alterations, histone modifications, and RNA methylation changes. These epigenetic modifications collectively regulate gene expression and genome stability, ultimately promoting radioresistance and cancer cell survival. Key regulatory mechanisms include the m6A methylation machinery—where METTL3 and METTL14 control tumor progression and stem-like cell persistence, while demethylases ALKBH5 and FTO enhance radioresistance by suppressing DNA repair genes—and DNA methyltransferases (DNMTs), whose activity directly correlates with aggressive tumor phenotypes. The article emphasizes that targeting these epigenetic pathways pharmacologically, using DNMT inhibitors (psammaplin A, 5-aza-2′-deoxycytidine, zebularine) or FTO inhibitors (FB23-2) combined with radiotherapy, can sensitize resistant cancers and increase DNA damage responses, offering promising strategies to overcome radiation resistance across multiple cancer types.

title: Fueling the Seed: Growth Factors and Cytokines Driving Cancer Stem Cells in Gynecological Malignancies
summary:
# Summary

This review examines the growth factors and cytokines that drive cancer stem cell (CSC) expansion and plasticity across gynecological malignancies. Multiple signaling axes—including EGFR, IGF, TGFβ, and HGF/c-Met—critically regulate CSC expansion in breast and ovarian cancers, while additional factors such as VEGFA and bone morphogenetic proteins (BMPs) promote CSC plasticity and therapeutic resistance. In endometrial cancer, CSCs are specifically supported by TGFβ, BMP2, Netrin-4, and c-Myc signaling, whereas cervical cancer stem-like phenotypes are enhanced by VEGF, IGF-1, Gremlin-1, and TGFβ circuits. The tumor microenvironment sustains CSCs through chronic inflammation via interleukins (IL-3, IL-6, IL-8, IL-10) and chemokines (CCL5), which activate STAT3 and NF-κB pathways, while hypoxia-induced HIF-1α and HIF-2α stabilize stemness genes (OCT4, NANOG, SOX2). The review also addresses molecular subtypes and therapeutic strategies, including hormone therapies, CDK4/6 inhibitors, HER2-targeted monoclonal antibodies, PARP inhibitors for BRCA-mutated tumors, and checkpoint immunotherapy for triple-negative breast cancers.

title: YTHDF2 enhances proliferation and metastasis of nasopharyngeal carcinoma by mediating m6A modification in destabilizing FOXO1 mRNA
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that YTHDF2, an m6A reader protein, promotes nasopharyngeal carcinoma (NPC) progression by mediating N6-methyladenosine modification to destabilize FOXO1 mRNA. YTHDF2 is significantly upregulated in NPC tissues and malignant cells, where it binds directly to m6A-methylated FOXO1 mRNA and accelerates its degradation, leading to reduced FOXO1 expression. This downregulation of FOXO1, a key tumor suppressor involved in cell cycle regulation, apoptosis, and autophagy, activates downstream AKT/ERK signaling and enhances NPC cell proliferation, migration, and invasion. YTHDF2 knockdown suppresses these aggressive phenotypes and restores FOXO1 expression, while YTHDF2 overexpression enhances them. High YTHDF2 expression is associated with poor patient prognosis, indicating its role as an important oncogenic driver in NPC through the m6A-YTHDF2-FOXO1 axis.

title: A Tumor-homing nanoplatform for the co-delivery of triptolide and siRNA-A4B2 conspicuously overcomes peritoneum metastasis of ovarian cancer
summary:
# Summary

The integrin α4β2 (ITGA4B2) heterodimer plays a critical role in ovarian cancer peritoneal metastasis through a mechanistic pathway involving its interaction with asparagine endopeptidase (AEP). ITGA4 and ITGB2 are significantly overexpressed in peritoneal metastatic tissues compared to primary tumors and form a ternary complex with AEP that activates both IL-17 and NF-κB signaling pathways to promote cell migration, invasion, and epithelial-mesenchymal transition. The research demonstrates that ITGA4B2 inhibition reduces these metastatic processes by downregulating IL-17, p50, p65, and EMT markers, establishing integrin signaling as a key driver of metastatic spread. To therapeutically target this pathway, the authors developed Si/TP@Exos, an exosome-based nanoplatform co-delivering triptolide and siRNA-A4B2, which significantly enhanced apoptosis and suppressed peritoneal metastasis in vivo. This tumor-homing strategy represents a novel approach to overcome the peritoneal dissemination tendency of ovarian cancer by simultaneously inhibiting integrin-mediated signaling while delivering cytotoxic therapeutic agents.

title: Research advances in epigenetic modifications and post-translational modifications in endothelial-mesenchymal transition
summary:
# Summary

Endothelial-mesenchymal transition (EndMT) is a critical developmental and pathological process involving the loss of endothelial markers such as VE-cadherin and acquisition of mesenchymal markers including α-smooth muscle actin, fibronectin, and N-cadherin. EndMT is regulated by multiple epigenetic mechanisms—DNA methylation, histone modifications, and non-coding RNAs—as well as post-translational modifications including protein phosphorylation, acetylation, and ubiquitination. Key signaling pathways controlling EndMT include TGF-β/Smad, Wnt/β-catenin, and Notch, which activate transcription factors such as Snail family members (SNAI1, SNAI2) and ZEB proteins that function as master switches driving the transition. DNA methyltransferases (DNMT1, DNMT3A, DNMT3B) establish methylation patterns that suppress endothelial genes, while TET enzymes promote demethylation and gene reactivation, and histone modifications controlled by enzymes like JMJD2B further modulate EndMT-related gene expression. EndMT plays significant roles in multiple pathological conditions including tissue fibrosis, cardiac development, tumor metastasis, atherosclerosis, ischemic heart disease, pulmonary arterial hypertension, and diabetes-associated vascular dysfunction.

title: Expression characteristics, prognostic value, and immune-related analysis of PPP2R1A in lung adenocarcinoma
summary:
# Summary

PPP2R1A, the regulatory subunit of protein phosphatase 2A (PP2A), is significantly elevated in lung adenocarcinoma tissues and serves as a prognostic marker associated with poor progression-free and overall survival, particularly in advanced-stage disease and N2 lymph node involvement. Through protein-protein interactions with PPP2CA, PPP2R2A, and other partners, PPP2R1A regulates key oncogenic processes including protein dephosphorylation, cell cycle progression, and metabolic pathways that promote lung cancer malignancy. Experimental knockdown of PPP2R1A substantially inhibits lung adenocarcinoma cell proliferation, migration, and invasion, while elevated PPP2R1A expression correlates with increased macrophage and CD4+ T cell infiltration, suggesting immune microenvironment modulation. The oncogenic role of PPP2R1A extends beyond lung cancer, as elevated expression has been detected across 26 cancer types and specific mutations (P179R and S256F) drive tumorigenesis and metastasis in uterine cancer. These findings establish PPP2R1A as a potential dual-function biomarker and therapeutic target in lung adenocarcinoma and other malignancies.

title: Emerging roles and therapeutic potential of tRNA-Derived small RNAs in reproductive system diseases: a review
journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
summary: tRNA-derived small RNAs (tsRNAs) represent an emerging class of regulatory molecules with diverse roles in reproductive physiology and disease. Generated through precise cleavage by ribonucleases including angiogenin, RNase T2, Dicer, RNase Z, and ELAC2, tsRNAs regulate gene expression, mRNA stability, and translation through interactions with Argonaute proteins and RNA-binding proteins like YBX1 and PIWI. In the reproductive system, tsRNAs are essential for gametogenesis, oocyte development, sperm maturation, and embryo implantation, with dysregulation implicated in polycystic ovary syndrome, endometriosis, oligospermia, and azoospermia. Specific tsRNAs such as 5'-tiRNA-Ala and 5'-tiRNA-Cys inhibit translation initiation and protein synthesis under stress conditions, promoting stress granule formation and triggering apoptosis and DNA repair responses. tsRNAs in follicular fluid correlate with oocyte quality and fertilization success, while sperm-derived tsRNAs mediate transgenerational epigenetic inheritance of environmental information affecting offspring metabolism and health. These findings establish tsRNAs as promising therapeutic targets for reproductive diseases through modulation of gene expression and stress response pathways.

title: Ribosome dysregulation and intervention in age-related infertility
summary:
# Summary

Age-related infertility is driven by dysregulated ribosome biogenesis and protein synthesis in oocytes and cumulus cells, with elevated ribosomal genes (RPS15, RPL36, RPS5) and increased mRNA translation occurring alongside epigenetic changes including DNA hypomethylation and altered heterochromatin deposition. Concurrent downregulation of critical meiotic genes, actin, and cohesin proteins impairs chromosome segregation and kinetochore-microtubule attachment, leading to aneuploidy and embryonic arrest through mechanisms involving sister chromatid separation errors, chromosome misalignment, and spindle defects. In cumulus cells, aging is characterized by elevated phosphorylated mTOR, suppressed autophagy (reduced LC3-II), decreased lysosomal activity, and accumulation of protein aggregates, reflecting proteostatic failure. Rapamycin effectively reverses these age-related changes by inhibiting mTOR-mediated protein synthesis and ribosome biogenesis, restoring protein homeostasis and autophagy while suppressing cellular senescence, thereby enabling successful embryo development in patients with previously failed in vitro fertilization cycles. This work identifies ribosome dysregulation and mTOR hyperactivation as therapeutic targets for age-related reproductive decline.

title: Bioinformatics Analysis of Hypoxia‐Related Mechanisms in Endometriosis: DDR2 as a Potential Diagnostic and Therapeutic Biomarker
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is strongly associated with a hypoxic microenvironment that drives disease progression through multiple interconnected mechanisms. The study identifies DDR2, a receptor tyrosine kinase, as a key hypoxia-regulated gene and promising diagnostic and therapeutic biomarker in endometriosis. DDR2 mediates pathological cell behaviors by interacting with collagen and hypoxia-inducible factors (HIFs) to activate matrix metalloproteinases, angiogenesis, and cellular migration, while DDR2 knockdown significantly suppresses endometriosis cell proliferation, migration, and invasion. The hypoxic microenvironment activates HIF-1α, which orchestrates a metabolic reprogramming shift from oxidative phosphorylation to glycolysis and upregulates proinflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α) and pro-invasive pathways including epithelial-mesenchymal transition and tissue remodeling. Bioinformatic analysis of 2,834 differentially expressed genes identified six hub genes, with DDR2 validated as a robust diagnostic marker, while molecular docking studies revealed that natural compounds including baicalein, cavidine, sitogluside, and stigmasterol may serve as potential DDR2 inhibitors.

title: Hsp90 co-chaperone FKBP4 facilitates CCT8 folding and connects Hsp90 to chaperonin-dependent proteostasis
summary: This study reveals that FKBP4, a co-chaperone of Hsp90, serves as a critical regulator of proteostasis by facilitating proper folding of CCT8 and other chaperonin complex subunits. FKBP4 prevents aggregation of multiple CCT components (CCT1, CCT2, CCT3, and CCT8) and thereby maintains stability of their client proteins, including the cell cycle regulator CDK2 and the cytoskeletal protein α-tubulin. When FKBP4 is depleted, CCT8 aggregation occurs and client protein stability is compromised, leading to decreased E-cadherin expression and impaired cell cycle progression. The research identifies FKBP4 as part of a broader proteostasis network that interacts with diverse substrates including FLNA, DDX3X, TALIN-1, FASN, and MRE11, many of which are dysregulated in cancer. FKBP4 is upregulated in multiple cancer types and its expression correlates with altered E-cadherin levels, implicating it in epithelial-mesenchymal transition and cancer progression. This work establishes FKBP4 as a central hub connecting Hsp90-dependent chaperoning to chaperonin-mediated protein folding pathways essential for cellular proteostasis and tumor biology.

title: HBV X protein regulates cancer stemness and tumor invasiveness through SENP1 in hepatocellular carcinoma
summary:
# Summary

The hepatitis B virus X (HBx) protein drives hepatocellular carcinoma progression by activating SENP1, a SUMO-specific protease that promotes cancer stemness and tumor invasiveness. HBx upregulates SENP1, which subsequently stabilizes and enhances the activity of OCT4, a key stemness factor, while also upregulating epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) markers including N-cadherin, SNAIL, and TWIST. This HBx-SENP1-OCT4 axis expands the CD133+ cancer stem cell population, increases tumor sphere formation, and enhances cell migration and invasion. Elevated SENP1 expression correlates with poor overall survival, early disease recurrence, and advanced TNM stages in HBV-related HCC, and SENP1 silencing effectively reduces HBx-induced metastases and sorafenib resistance in vivo. Additionally, SENP1 regulates PIN1 activity through deSUMOylation, linking this pathway to drug resistance and supporting SENP1's role as a biomarker for predicting early recurrence and metastatic potential in HBV-associated tumors.

title: THBS1 identificated as an endometriosis biomarker through evidence from single-cell and bulk transcriptomic profiling
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies thrombospondin-1 (THBS1) as a key biomarker in endometriosis through integrated single-cell and bulk transcriptomic analysis. THBS1 is significantly enriched in ectopic endometrial tissues and is primarily expressed in stromal cells, where it actively promotes endometrial cell proliferation, migration, and invasion. Functional experiments demonstrated that THBS1 knockdown suppresses these pathogenic behaviors, including cell-cycle arrest at G1 phase and increased apoptosis, while THBS1 silencing in xenograft models effectively inhibits ectopic lesion growth. The research links THBS1 to the TGF-β signaling pathway, a critical contributor to endometriosis pathophysiology that regulates cell growth, differentiation, extracellular matrix production, and immune modulation. Together, these findings establish THBS1 as a central pathogenic modulator in endometriosis and suggest its potential as both a diagnostic biomarker and therapeutic target for this debilitating condition.

title: cGAS–STING signaling pathway as a therapeutic target in human diseases
summary:
# Summary

The cGAS–STING signaling pathway serves as a critical innate immune sensor that detects cytoplasmic DNA—including exogenous pathogens, self-derived mitochondrial DNA, and nuclear DNA fragments—and orchestrates robust antimicrobial and antitumor immune responses. Upon cytoplasmic DNA detection, cGAS synthesizes the second messenger cGAMP, which activates the adaptor protein STING localized at the endoplasmic reticulum, triggering two major downstream cascades: the TBK1–IRF3 axis promoting type I interferon and interferon-stimulated genes, and the NF-κB pathway driving proinflammatory cytokine production. Beyond canonical immune functions, the STING–PERK–eIF2α signaling axis mediates cellular senescence and organ fibrosis, while dysregulation of cGAS–STING signaling—particularly through mitochondrial dysfunction, impaired autophagy and mitophagy, or genomic instability—drives pathology in neurodegenerative diseases including Parkinson's, Alzheimer's, and Huntington's diseases, as well as acute brain injuries. Notably, STING inhibitors show therapeutic promise in reducing both neuroinflammation and neurodegeneration in disease models, positioning the cGAS–STING pathway as a compelling therapeutic target for treating multiple human diseases characterized by aberrant innate immune activation.

title: Chemokines and their receptors in oral squamous cell carcinoma: mechanisms, clinical significance, and therapeutic implications
summary:
# Summary

Chemokines are key regulators of immune cell migration and tumor progression in oral squamous cell carcinoma, functioning through binding to G protein-coupled receptors to mediate directed cell trafficking, proliferation, invasion, and metastasis. Specific chemokine-receptor axes drive distinct tumor-promoting mechanisms: the CXCL12/CXCR4 axis promotes invasion and distant metastasis; CXCL9/CXCR3 activates Akt signaling and epithelial-mesenchymal transition; and the CCL2-CCR2 axis drives angiogenesis, tumorigenesis, and metastasis while recruiting monocytes and regulating immune cell behavior. CCL2 is a particularly significant biomarker, with elevated expression and serum levels correlating with disease progression, and the chemokine works through multiple pathways including upregulation of EMT-associated transcription factors like Snail and matrix metalloproteinases. Bacterial infections such as Fusobacterium nucleatum and Porphyromonas gingivalis upregulate CCL5, which induces MMP9 and VEGF-A to promote tumor invasion and angiogenesis while suppressing antitumor T cell activity. Therapeutic targeting of these pathways, such as Oroxylin A inhibition of CCL2 transcription or CXCR4 blockade, represents a promising strategy to interrupt chemokine-driven tumor progression.

title: Multi-omics reveals the immune and metabolic characteristics and associations in non-mucinous ovarian cancer
summary:
# Summary

This multi-omics study reveals that non-mucinous ovarian cancer exhibits distinct immune and metabolic alterations that promote immune evasion and tumor progression. Glycosphingolipid metabolism is significantly dysregulated, with elevated sphingomyelin species and increased expression of metabolic enzymes (GM2A, HEXA, HEXB, SUMF1) correlating with disease severity. A critical PPIA-BSG signaling axis mediates communication between CD20+ B cells and SUMF1+ malignant cells, driving extracellular matrix remodeling and immune evasion. The tumor microenvironment exhibits metabolic reprogramming through multiple pathways including Warburg effect-driven lactate accumulation that suppresses anti-tumor immunity and promotes regulatory T cell expansion, while HIF-1α-dependent glycolysis and the pentose phosphate pathway support hypoxia-adaptive metabolism. Notably, glutamine metabolism-derived N-acetylaspartate induces macrophage polarization toward an immunosuppressive M2 phenotype and upregulates PD-L1 expression. These integrated immune and metabolic characteristics provide therapeutic targets, as glutamine metabolism inhibition shows promise in suppressing tumor proliferation and enhancing antitumor immunity.

title: E-Cadherin Regulates HIF1-α In Vitro in Induced 3D Spheroid Models of Human Breast Cancer Through Both mTOR and Notch1 Signaling
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that E-cadherin regulates hypoxia-inducible factor 1-alpha (HIF-1α) expression in three-dimensional breast cancer spheroid models through two primary signaling pathways: PI3K/AKT/mTOR and Notch1. E-cadherin-positive cell lines (MCF-7, HTB-126, HTB-27) express substantially higher HIF-1α levels during induced spheroidgenesis compared to E-cadherin-negative lines, and loss of E-cadherin through either knockout or calpain-mediated proteolysis dramatically reduces HIF-1α expression. The mTOR pathway contributes to HIF-1α upregulation independently of canonical hypoxia-sensing mechanisms involving prolyl hydroxylase domain proteins or VHL, as evidenced by rapamycin and PI3K inhibitor studies. Additionally, Notch1 signaling acts as a parallel mechanism to increase HIF-1α, with E-cadherin partially inhibiting this pathway while calpain inhibition reduces both Notch1 activity and HIF-1α levels. These findings indicate that E-cadherin serves as a critical node integrating cell adhesion with metabolic reprogramming during spheroid formation and suggests potential therapeutic targets in E-cadherin-positive cancers.

title: The Anti-EMMPRIN Monoclonal Antibody hMR18-mAb Induces Tumor Dormancy and Inhibits the EMT Process in Human Carcinoma Cell Lines Co-Cultured with Macrophages
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that EMMPRIN, a membrane glycoprotein involved in cell-cell interactions, acts as a "gatekeeper" that prevents tumor cells from entering dormancy and promotes epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT). When macrophages were co-cultured with carcinoma cell lines, they enhanced EMT-inducer cytokines (by 2.5-fold) and suppressed dormancy markers (NR2F1, p21, p27) by 30-60%, while simultaneously reducing E-cadherin expression and increasing vimentin and stemness markers (SOX2, Nanog). Treatment with the anti-EMMPRIN monoclonal antibody hMR18-mAb reversed these pro-tumoral effects by reducing EMT-inducer cytokines by 55-70%, restoring E-cadherin levels, decreasing vimentin and stemness markers, and elevating dormancy markers by 1.5-3.5-fold. hMR18-mAb also significantly reduced tumor cell proliferation, migration, and angiogenic potential. These findings position hMR18-mAb as the first antibody capable of inducing dormancy in tumor cells and inhibiting metastatic progression, offering a novel therapeutic strategy to suppress cancer dissemination.

title: Integrative Single-Cell and Machine Learning Analysis Develops a Glutamine Metabolism–Based Prognostic Model and Identifies MSMO1 as a Therapeutic Target in Osteosarcoma
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies glutamine metabolism as a central driver of osteosarcoma progression through integrated single-cell and machine learning analyses. The glutamine transporter SLC38A5 is overexpressed in osteosarcoma and activates the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway, which subsequently activates SREBP1/SCD-1 signaling to inhibit ferroptosis and promote cell proliferation, migration, and invasion. A five-gene prognostic model incorporating MSMO1, GPX7, COL11A2, CPE, and SGMS2 stratifies patients into distinct risk groups, with MSMO1 identified as a key therapeutic target whose knockdown suppresses tumor cell proliferation, migration, and invasion while increasing apoptosis through dampened Wnt/β-catenin signaling. Glutamine metabolism also shapes the tumor microenvironment by modulating immune evasion and angiogenesis through immune-metabolic crosstalk involving ceramide metabolism, LOX-expressing cancer-associated fibroblasts, and regulatory dendritic cells. These findings position glutamine metabolism and MSMO1 as promising targets for osteosarcoma treatment, with potential applications extending to metabolic reprogramming in other malignancies.

title: NUMB in Endometrial Pathology: From Adenomyosis Expression Patterns to Endometrial Cancer Survival Implications
summary:
# Summary

NUMB functions as a critical negative regulator of Notch signaling and a key regulator of endometrial stem cell fate determination and homeostasis. The study reveals that NUMB is significantly elevated in the eutopic endometrium and myometrium of adenomyosis patients and is associated with dysregulated endometrial stem cells that exhibit enhanced self-renewal and invasiveness, contributing to adenomyosis pathogenesis and the clinical symptoms of dysmenorrhea, menorrhagia, and chronic pelvic pain. NUMB exerts its tumor-suppressive effects through multiple mechanisms, including binding to Notch receptors to inhibit their activation, inhibiting Mdm2-mediated p53 ubiquitination to stabilize TP53, and regulating Syndecan-1 trafficking to modulate cell adhesion and migration. Bioinformatics and TCGA analyses establish NUMB as a central hub protein with dysregulation implicated in both endometrial cancer tumorigenesis and altered survival outcomes, providing mechanistic insights into how dysregulated Notch signaling and stem cell dysfunction contribute to the pathogenesis of invasive endometrial diseases. These findings position NUMB as both a potential biomarker for endometrial stem cell dysregulation and a therapeutic target for adenomyosis and endometrial malignancies.

title: Proline-, Glutamic Acid-, Leucine-Rich Protein 1 (PELP1): Diversity, Structural Conservation, and Evolutionary Origins Across the Species
summary:
# Summary

PELP1 is a multifunctional nuclear protein with highly conserved structural domains that mediate ribosome biogenesis, steroid receptor signaling, and chromatin remodeling across eukaryotic species. The protein contains functionally distinct domains, including the RIX1 domain that facilitates rRNA processing and contains critical signaling motifs (LXXLL and PXXP), and the NUC domain associated with nucleolar function. PELP1 serves as an epigenetic scaffold that coordinates multiple chromatin-modifying complexes, including HDAC2, KDM1, and PRMT6, while interacting with estrogen receptor alpha, androgen receptor, and glucocorticoid receptor to orchestrate receptor-dependent gene regulation and activate downstream MAPK/ERK1/2 signaling pathways upon Src kinase phosphorylation. Evolutionary analysis reveals that PELP1 is conserved exclusively within Opisthokonta (eukaryotes), with 600 orthologs in Chordata and declining representation across other metazoan and fungal phyla, but is completely absent from bacteria, viruses, plants, and oomycota. Beyond transcriptional regulation, PELP1 participates in DNA damage response by modulating p53 function and promotes proliferation and metastasis in multiple cancers, particularly breast, prostate, ovarian, and pancreatic malignancies, where its overexpression drives therapy resistance.

title: Sirtuins in Women’s Health
summary:
# Summary: Sirtuins in Endometriosis and Women's Health

SIRT proteins, particularly SIRT1, SIRT2, and SIRT3, regulate critical metabolic and autophagy pathways implicated in endometriosis pathogenesis. SIRT1 activation normally suppresses inflammation by inhibiting NF-κB and promotes metabolic remodeling through FOXO1 and PDK4 activation; however, in endometriotic tissues, SIRT1 is pathologically elevated and facilitates disease progression by triggering excessive autophagy, promoting epithelial-mesenchymal transition, and contributing to progesterone resistance through deacetylation of FOXO1. In polycystic ovary syndrome, hyperandrogenism and elevated microRNA-339-5p inhibit the SIRT1/AMPK/PDK4 signaling axis, which multiple natural compounds (resveratrol, quercetin, sulforaphane) and pharmacological agents (metformin, diacerein, semaglutide) can restore to improve metabolic function. SIRT2 and SIRT3 offer complementary protective mechanisms—SIRT2 regulates glucose metabolism and granulosa cell survival while SIRT3 mitigates oxidative stress through deacetylation of antioxidant enzymes—and both are downregulated in PCOS but can be upregulated by metformin, berberine, and other compounds. The therapeutic potential of sirtuin modulators lies in correcting the dysregulated sirtuin-dependent autophagy and metabolic reprogramming that characterize endometriosis and related reproductive disorders.

title: Epigenetic reprogramming as the nexus of cancer stemness and therapy resistance: implications for biomarker discovery
summary:
# Summary

Epigenetic reprogramming serves as a central mechanism driving cancer stem cell (CSC) formation and therapeutic resistance through alterations in histone modifications, DNA methylation, and chromatin architecture. Multiple signaling pathways including Notch, HIF, and adenosine receptor signaling recruit epigenetic co-regulators like KDM6A and p300 to establish pluripotency-associated chromatin landscapes that sustain stemness properties. Therapy-induced stresses such as chemotherapy and targeted kinase inhibition paradoxically promote stemness through mechanisms including cell dedifferentiation, senescence-associated secretory phenotype activation, and metabolic reprogramming, with senescent cells capable of reprogramming neighboring cells into stem-like states. The tumor microenvironment amplifies these effects through inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, IL-8) that upregulate chromatin modifiers and activate pathways like Wnt/β-catenin and NF-κB to enhance CSC phenotypes. These interconnected epigenetic and inflammatory mechanisms collectively create a resistant state that limits therapeutic efficacy, suggesting that targeting epigenetic reprogramming and microenvironmental inflammation may represent critical strategies for overcoming therapeutic resistance in cancer.

title: The role of bHLH-PAS transcription factors in endoplasmic reticulum stress
summary:
# Summary

The bHLH-PAS transcription factor family—including AHR, HIF, SIM, NPAS1-4, and CLOCK—regulates endoplasmic reticulum (ER) stress alongside their roles in hypoxia response, circadian rhythms, metabolic homeostasis, and xenobiotic detoxification. ER stress arises from protein overproduction, hypoxia, redox imbalance, or nutrient deprivation and triggers the unfolded protein response (UPR) through three main sensors—PERK, IRE1, and ATF6—which are normally sequestered by the chaperone BiP. The UPR transiently inhibits translation while upregulating molecular chaperones and activating ER-associated protein degradation to restore proteostasis; however, prolonged or excessive ER stress activates pro-apoptotic pathways involving CHOP and caspases, ultimately leading to cell death. Chronic ER stress contributes to the pathogenesis of multiple diseases including diabetes, neurodegenerative disorders, cancer, and inflammatory conditions, highlighting the critical role of bHLH-PAS proteins in maintaining cellular homeostasis and preventing disease.

title: Clinical relevance of PLK1 in epithelial ovarian cancer
summary:
# Summary

PLK1 (Polo-like kinase 1) is a serine/threonine protein kinase that plays critical roles in cell division, genomic stability, and DNA damage response through phosphorylation of multiple substrates including BUBR1, CDC25C, and Cyclin B1. PLK1 is significantly overexpressed in epithelial ovarian cancer (EOC), where it promotes cancer cell proliferation, invasion, and survival while suppressing apoptosis through direct regulation of tumor suppressors (TP53, PTEN) and oncogenes (KRAS, c-MYC). Inhibition of PLK1 via small molecule inhibitors (volasertib, onvansertib, BI2536, rigosertib) potently suppresses EOC cell proliferation by inducing cell cycle G2 phase arrest, DNA damage, and apoptosis. PLK1 inhibitors demonstrate synergistic antitumor effects when combined with standard chemotherapeutic agents (paclitaxel, cisplatin, doxorubicin) and PARP inhibitors, offering a promising therapeutic strategy for improving treatment outcomes in EOC patients, particularly those with early-stage disease where PLK1 expression correlates with prognosis.

title: Downregulation of BST2 Rescues Cochlear Nerve Demyelination in Age‐Related Hearing Loss via Enhancing Schwann Cell Migration
summary:
# Summary

Age-related hearing loss (ARHL) involves progressive demyelination of cochlear nerves, and this study identifies bone marrow stromal antigen 2 (BST2) as a key pathogenic factor. Elevated BST2 expression in Schwann cells during aging suppresses the transcription factor POU6F1 via NF-κB pathway activation, which in turn reduces myelin protein zero (MPZ) expression and impairs Schwann cell migration capacity. Knockdown of BST2 in aged mice reversed these effects by restoring N-cadherin expression and enhancing Schwann cell migration, leading to improved myelin sheath integrity and significantly better auditory thresholds and neural conduction velocity. These findings establish BST2 as a therapeutic target for ARHL, with the BST2–NF-κB–POU6F1 axis representing a novel regulatory pathway controlling Schwann cell function and myelin maintenance in the aging cochlea.

title: Circular RNAs in cancer immunology: Immune escape, therapeutic resistance, and nanomedicine synergies
summary:
# Summary

Circular RNAs (circRNAs) orchestrate immune escape in cancer through multiple interconnected mechanisms, primarily by stabilizing and elevating PD-L1 expression on tumor cells. Specific circRNAs such as circATAD2, circRHBDD1, and circIGF2BP3 regulate PD-L1 stability via m⁶A-dependent RNA-protein interactions, microRNA sequestration, and deubiquitinase recruitment, collectively suppressing anti-tumor immunity and promoting CD8+ T-cell exhaustion while expanding regulatory T cells and myeloid-derived suppressor cells. circRNAs packaged into exosomes mediate intercellular communication that drives immune checkpoint inhibitor resistance, with specific exosomal circRNAs like circUSP7 directly impairing CD8+ T-cell activation and circ_0001947 inducing T-cell exhaustion. Therapeutic strategies targeting oncogenic circRNAs using nanoparticle-delivered antisense oligonucleotides or siRNAs restore antitumor immunity and drug sensitivity, while engineered synthetic circRNAs encoding immunostimulatory factors such as IL-12 demonstrate remarkable capacity to remodel the tumor microenvironment and activate NK cells and CD8+ T-cell expansion. These findings establish circRNAs as critical regulators linking tumor metabolic reprogramming with immune dysfunction and identify nanomedicine-based circRNA silencing and engineered circRNA delivery as promising therapeutic approaches to overcome immunotherapy resistance.

title: MicroRNA-mediated regulation of the immune response in Calu-3 cells infected with a SARS-CoV-2 E gene variant
summary:
# Summary

A SARS-CoV-2 E protein variant (F8) with enhanced stability remodels the host microRNA landscape in respiratory epithelial cells through a coordinated two-pronged mechanism. F8 infection upregulates miR-361-3p while simultaneously downregulating let-7b-5p, establishing a unique miRNA regulatory network distinct from wild-type E protein. The upregulated miR-361-3p inhibits TSPAN1 to activate the PI3K-Akt pathway and promote PTEN expression, enhancing immune signaling, while the suppressed let-7b-5p releases inhibition on inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, IL-8, PTX3) and impairs tight junction protein regulation. Additionally, the E protein directly activates the NLRP3 inflammasome and TLR2 signaling to amplify IL-1β, TNF-α, and IL-6 secretion while suppressing tight junction proteins, thereby compromising epithelial barrier integrity. This multifaceted modulation of both innate immune responses and epithelial barriers represents a sophisticated viral adaptation strategy, with the F8 variant's enhanced structural stability enabling more robust immunological manipulation and sustained airway inflammation compared to the wild-type E protein.

title: Molecular mechanism of bisphenol A in promoting esophageal carcinoma based on network toxicology and molecular docking
summary:
# Article Summary

This study employs network toxicology and molecular docking to elucidate how bisphenol A (BPA), a pervasive endocrine-disrupting chemical, promotes esophageal squamous cell carcinoma (ESCA) progression through heat shock proteins HSP90AA1 and HSP90AB1. BPA directly binds to both HSP90 isoforms with strong affinities and activates downstream signaling cascades including MAPK/ERK and PI3K/AKT pathways, which collectively enhance tumor cell proliferation, migration, and drug resistance while suppressing apoptosis. HSP90AA1 and HSP90AB1 are significantly overexpressed in ESCA tissues and function as central hub genes coordinating protein kinase regulation, telomerase activity, and immune-inflammatory responses, with elevated expression correlating with poor overall survival. The study reveals that BPA-related genes are enriched in critical pathways including antigen processing, IL-17 signaling, estrogen signaling, and Th17 cell differentiation, implicating both immune dysregulation and reproductive endocrine disruption. Additionally, the deubiquitinase JOSD2 stabilizes HSP90AB1 through deubiquitination, providing a mechanistic link for sustained HSP90 activity in cancer progression. This integrative analysis demonstrates that BPA facilitates ESCA development through a multi-pathway mechanism centered on HSP90-mediated oncogenic signaling and immune dysregulation.

title: Progress in peptide and protein therapeutics: Challenges and strategies
summary:
# Summary

Peptides and proteins represent promising therapeutic agents due to their high specificity in biological activity, which substantially reduces off-target effects and toxicity compared to conventional small-molecule drugs. Clinical applications of peptide and protein therapeutics are diverse and well-established, including leuprolide acetate for endometriosis and prostate cancer treatment, insulin and GLP-1 analogs for diabetes and obesity management, and erythropoietin for anemia. However, widespread clinical application of these therapeutics is severely constrained by inherent biological and physicochemical limitations, including rapid enzymatic degradation by gastric and intestinal proteases (pepsin, trypsin, chymotrypsin), poor membrane permeability due to their high molecular weight and hydrophilicity, low systemic bioavailability, rapid renal clearance for molecules below 60 kDa, and immunogenic responses. Additional degradation pathways include oxidation, deamidation, and aggregation, with aggregated proteins particularly prone to activating antigen-presenting cells and triggering adaptive immune responses. Advanced delivery strategies utilizing liposomes, nanoparticles, hydrogels, and penetration enhancers are being developed to overcome these obstacles and improve the therapeutic efficacy of peptide and protein therapeutics.

title: Acellular fishbone scaffolds loaded with bone marrow mesenchymal stem cell-derived exosomes for bone defect repairing
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that bone marrow mesenchymal stem cell-derived exosomes (HO-Exos) loaded onto acellular decellularized fishbone (DFB) scaffolds effectively promote vascularized bone regeneration. HO-Exos, enriched with osteogenic factors including BMP-2, BMP-4, and VEGFA, stimulate osteogenic differentiation of BMSCs and induce robust angiogenic effects through the Wnt/β-catenin signaling pathway, while also enhancing cellular migration and vascular network formation. The DFB scaffold provides a biomimetic microenvironment with hierarchical pore structure and low immunogenicity due to complete decellularization, preserving native collagen and extracellular matrix components that support cell-matrix crosstalk. In vivo, the DFB/HO-Exos composite significantly accelerated both bone formation and vascularization in rat femoral defects by synergistically coordinating osteogenic and angiogenic processes, establishing this composite scaffold as a promising therapeutic approach for treating critical bone defects.

title: Derazantinib enhances gemcitabine efficacy in PDAC by attenuating the NF-κB and MAPK pathways to suppress MUC5AC expression
summary: This study demonstrates that derazantinib, a fibroblast growth factor receptor (FGFR) inhibitor, enhances gemcitabine efficacy in pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma by suppressing the FGFR-MAPK/NF-κB-MUC5AC signaling axis. Elevated FGFR2 and FGFR3 expression in gemcitabine-resistant PDAC tissues and cells activates downstream MAPK and NF-κB pathways, which transcriptionally upregulate MUC5AC, a mucin that promotes chemotherapy resistance and poor prognosis. Derazantinib inhibits FGFR2 and FGFR3 (but not FGFR1) and concurrently suppresses both MAPK and NF-κB signaling, thereby downregulating MUC5AC expression and restoring gemcitabine sensitivity. The combination of derazantinib and gemcitabine synergistically inhibited proliferation, induced apoptosis, suppressed migration, and reduced tumor growth in gemcitabine-resistant PDAC cell lines and xenograft models. These findings establish FGFR2/3 and MUC5AC as key drivers of gemcitabine resistance in PDAC and suggest that derazantinib may serve as a promising therapeutic adjuvant for overcoming chemotherapy resistance in this aggressive malignancy.

title: ACPA prevents lung fibroblast-to-CAF transformation by reprogramming the tumor microenvironment through NSCLC-derived exosomes
summary:
# Summary

ACPA, a synthetic cannabinoid, prevents the transformation of lung fibroblasts into cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs) by reprogramming tumor-derived exosomes. ACPA directly inhibits multiple metabolic pathways in NSCLC cells—including the Akt/PI3K and JNK signaling cascades, glycolysis, the citric acid cycle, and the urea cycle—while simultaneously reducing oncogenic microRNA species (miR-21 and miR-23) in cancer cell-derived exosomes. When NSCLC cells are treated with ACPA, their secreted exosomes lose the capacity to reprogram fibroblasts, instead disrupting critical metabolic processes in fibroblasts including the pentose phosphate pathway, lipid metabolism, and amino acid metabolism. These ACPA-treated exosomes dramatically reduce the expression of CAF-specific markers (α-SMA, FAP, and PDPN) and decrease overall fibroblast viability within 12 hours. By blocking exosome-mediated fibroblast transformation, ACPA interrupts a key mechanism by which CAFs promote tumor progression, chemotherapy resistance, metastasis, and immunosuppression in the NSCLC microenvironment.

title: Association between serum γ-Glutamyltransferase and the risk of cervical cancer: Evidence from the national health and nutrition examination survey
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that elevated serum γ-glutamyltransferase (GGT) levels are independently associated with increased cervical cancer risk, even in HPV-negative patients. GGT promotes cervical tumorigenesis through a pro-oxidant mechanism: the enzyme catalyzes extracellular glutathione metabolism and generates reactive oxygen species (ROS), which activate the NF-κB signaling pathway to drive tumor cell survival, proliferation, and metastasis. While high-risk human papillomavirus infection remains the principal cause of cervical cancer through E6/E7-mediated degradation of tumor suppressors p53 and Rb, the analysis reveals that GGT-associated oxidative stress and chronic inflammation represent additional independent risk factors. Notably, individuals with GGT levels ≥50 U/L exhibited a 76% increased cervical cancer risk, and the association was particularly pronounced in HPV-negative women, suggesting GGT may serve as a biomarker for cervical cancer susceptibility in populations without classical viral risk factors. The findings identify GGT as a potential therapeutic target and support its role as an independent predictor of cervical cancer risk across multiple cancer types.

title: Small Extracellular Vesicles: Unraveling Their Roles in Ovarian Cancer Progression and Tapping Into Clinical Application Potential
summary:
# Summary

Small extracellular vesicles (sEVs) serve as critical mediators of intercellular communication in ovarian cancer progression, functioning as cargo delivery vehicles that promote multiple hallmarks of malignancy including cell proliferation, metastasis, angiogenesis, and chemotherapy resistance. The oncogenic effects of sEVs are driven by numerous miRNA cargo species—such as miR-181d-5p, miR-125b-5p, and miR-21-3p, which are upregulated under HIF-mediated hypoxia—that directly enhance cancer cell proliferation and M2-type macrophage polarization, thereby suppressing anti-tumor immunity. Beyond miRNAs, sEVs deliver long non-coding RNAs (including PANDAR and CATED) and circular RNAs (circATP2B4, circPUM1) that activate pro-survival and pro-metastatic signaling pathways such as JAK1/STAT3, TGF-β, and NF-κB, while simultaneously conferring cisplatin resistance through mechanisms including PDHA1 inhibition and YAP1 nuclear translocation. Beyond their pathogenic roles, sEVs show significant clinical promise as minimally invasive biomarkers for diagnosis, prognosis, and disease monitoring, with surface modifications like CD47 expression enhancing their stability for potential therapeutic application. Collectively, targeting sEV biogenesis, composition, or uptake represents an emerging therapeutic avenue to combat ovarian cancer progression and chemotherapy resistance.

title: Betulinic Acid Suppresses UBE2T Expression via MAPK/ERK Inhibition to Block FANCI and FANCD2 Monoubiquitination in Glioblastoma
journal: Journal of Cellular and Molecular Medicine
summary: Betulinic acid suppresses glioblastoma growth by inhibiting the MAPK/ERK signaling pathway, which leads to transcriptional downregulation of UBE2T, a critical E2 ubiquitin-conjugating enzyme. This UBE2T suppression prevents the monoubiquitination of FANCI and FANCD2 proteins, blocking their activation and nuclear accumulation required for homologous recombination-mediated repair of DNA interstrand crosslinks. Consequently, betulinic acid causes persistent DNA damage and sensitizes tumor cells to cisplatin chemotherapy, with combined treatment significantly reducing tumor growth in xenograft models. The study demonstrates that ERK activation normally sustains Fanconi anemia pathway readiness through UBE2T transcription, a process that betulinic acid effectively interrupts. These findings establish the ERK/UBE2T axis as a therapeutic target for overcoming platinum resistance in glioblastoma and potentially other cancers dependent on robust DNA repair capacity.

title: Piezo-type mechanosensitive ion channel component 1: a mechano-bioenergetic transducer in the tumour microenvironment
summary:
# Summary

Piezo1, a mechanosensitive ion channel widely expressed throughout the tumor microenvironment in endothelial cells, immune cells, and fibroblasts, functions as a critical mechanotransducer that converts physical stimuli into oncogenic biochemical signals. Elevated tissue stiffness and interstitial pressure in tumors activate Piezo1, triggering calcium influx that activates downstream signaling cascades including Src and extracellular signal-regulated kinase pathways, ultimately promoting tumor cell proliferation, migration, and invasion. Piezo1 activation also drives pathological angiogenesis and immune evasion, while stiffness-induced Piezo1 engagement upregulates matrix metalloproteinases that facilitate extracellular matrix remodeling, collectively creating a permissive microenvironment for cancer progression. High Piezo1 expression correlates strongly with adverse clinical outcomes across multiple cancer types including prostate, ovarian, and gastric cancers. The mechanistic integration of integrin clustering, focal adhesion formation, and actin cytoskeleton remodeling further amplifies Piezo1-dependent mechanotransduction in the rigid tumor microenvironment. These findings position Piezo1 as a potential therapeutic target, with inhibitors like GsMTx-4 offering strategies to disrupt mechanically-driven tumor progression.

title: PTPN22 Dephosphorylates CBL to Inhibit PD-L1 Ubiquitination and Drive Immunosuppression in Renal Cell Carcinoma
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals a novel immunosuppressive mechanism in renal cell carcinoma wherein the protein tyrosine phosphatase PTPN22, which is highly elevated in RCC cells, suppresses anti-tumor immunity by dephosphorylating the E3 ubiquitin ligase CBL at tyrosine 700. This dephosphorylation prevents CBL from binding to and ubiquitinating PD-L1, stabilizing PD-L1 protein levels and promoting T cell exhaustion through enhanced PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint signaling. PTPN22 expression correlates positively with PD-L1 levels in patient tissues and drives immunosuppression by recruiting regulatory T cells and macrophages to the tumor microenvironment. Pharmacologically, curcumin directly binds and degrades PTPN22, which combined with immune checkpoint inhibitors significantly enhances T cell activation, reduces regulatory T cell infiltration, and improves anti-tumor efficacy. These findings identify PTPN22 as a critical post-transcriptional regulator of PD-L1 stability and a promising therapeutic target for overcoming immunotherapy resistance in RCC.

title: Endocrine Examples of Phase Separation in Biology
summary:
# Summary

Steroid hormone receptors including estrogen receptor alpha (ERα), androgen receptor (AR), and progesterone receptor regulate transcription through liquid-liquid phase separation, a process where proteins with intrinsically disordered regions form transcriptional condensates at gene loci. These condensates, mediated by coregulators like MED1 and BRD4, facilitate chromatin remodeling and enhance gene transcription by recruiting RNA polymerase II and promoting chromatin accessibility. However, transitions to liquid-gel phase separation or formation of pathological condensates—such as Eleanor clouds in estrogen-resistant breast cancers—reduce hormone responsiveness and contribute to therapy resistance. Notably, polyglutamine expansions in AR's N-terminal domain increase aberrant foci formation while reducing protein mobility and transcriptional activity, providing mechanistic insights into neuromuscular disease. The critical role of intrinsically disordered regions in driving phase separation is demonstrated by the finding that deletion of MED1 or GATA3 IDRs markedly impairs hormone-mediated transcription, highlighting phase separation as a fundamental regulatory mechanism underlying endocrine function and dysfunction in disease states.

title: Plasma levels of sirtuin 1 and sirtuin 2 in ascending aortic aneurysm patients — a comparative study
summary:
# Summary

This comparative study demonstrates that sirtuins, particularly SIRT1 and SIRT2, play important roles in vascular homeostasis and aortic aneurysm pathophysiology through their involvement in cell proliferation, apoptosis, DNA repair, and metabolic regulation. Plasma levels of both SIRT1 and SIRT2 were significantly elevated in patients with ascending aortic aneurysm compared to controls, and the two proteins showed strong positive correlation with each other. While SIRT1 is essential for maintaining vascular wall integrity and its overexpression prevents aneurysm development in experimental models, the elevated circulating levels in aneurysm patients suggest a compensatory response to vascular injury. Additionally, SIRT2 levels were inversely correlated with total and LDL cholesterol, and reduced SIRT2 was observed in aneurysm patients with concurrent chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, indicating complex interactions between sirtuins and systemic comorbidities. These findings support the therapeutic potential of SIRT1 activators for cardiovascular and metabolic disorders.

title: Mitochondria at the intersections of RNA modifications and metabolism reprogramming implications in cell death, tumor microenvironment, and immunotherapy
summary: This comprehensive review examines the intersection of mitochondrial function, RNA modifications, and metabolic reprogramming with implications for cell death pathways and cancer immunotherapy. Mitochondria orchestrate critical cellular processes including energy production through ATP generation and glucose metabolism, while also regulating programmed cell death and signal transduction. RNA modifications, particularly m6A methylation catalyzed by the METTL complex (METTL3, METTL14, WTAP, and other subunits), emerge as key posttranscriptional regulators that influence oxidative phosphorylation, cuproptosis, and antitumor immunity by modulating mitochondrial function and glucose metabolism. The article highlights that S-adenosylmethionine from methionine metabolism drives both RNA modifications and methylation reactions, thereby impacting tumor progression and redox maintenance. Notably, METTL3 inhibitors such as STM2457 reduce m6A levels and suppress BRD4 expression, demonstrating therapeutic efficacy across multiple malignancies (AML, SCLC, NSCLC, hepatocarcinoma, pancreatic cancer, medulloblastoma, osteosarcoma, and intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma) by impacting mitochondrial glucose metabolism, ferroptosis, and mitochondrial dynamics. This framework reveals how manipulating RNA modifications can reprogram cellular metabolism and activate immunogenic cell death pathways, offering promising therapeutic targets for cancer treatment.

title: Beyond VEGF and TGF-β: A Comprehensive Review of Growth Factor Pathways in the Pathophysiology of Uterine Leiomyomas
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive review examines multiple growth factor pathways driving uterine fibroid (leiomyoma) pathophysiology beyond the well-established VEGF and TGF-β mechanisms. Uterine fibroids cause significant clinical complications including heavy bleeding, anemia, and pain through dysregulated growth factor signaling. Estrogen promotes fibroid development by upregulating growth factors (IGF-1, EGF, PDGF) and binding estrogen receptors, while paradoxically inhibiting the anti-apoptotic factor bcl-2. Progesterone counterintuitively exacerbates fibroid pathology by promoting TGF-β and Smad signaling pathways, enhancing angiogenesis via VEGF, and activating fibrotic mediators (Activin A, TGF-β, EGF) that increase extracellular matrix deposition. Additional growth factors including FGF and PDGF activate distinct signaling cascades (MAPK, PI3K/AKT, JAK/STAT) that promote angiogenesis, cell proliferation, and smooth muscle cell survival. Therapeutic interventions such as mifepristone and vitamin D3 show promise by downregulating VEGF expression and blocking TGF-β-responsive pathways, respectively, offering potential treatment strategies for fibroid management.

title: A Review of ARID1A’s Role in Breast Cancer Progression: Context-Dependent Mechanisms and Therapeutic Implications
summary:
# Summary

ARID1A, a key subunit of the SWI/SNF chromatin remodeling complex, functions as a context-dependent regulator in breast cancer with both tumor suppressive and oncogenic roles. Loss of ARID1A disrupts critical cellular processes including DNA repair, cell-cycle control, and chromatin regulation, leading to genomic instability and dysregulation of cell-cycle checkpoints. ARID1A deficiency promotes epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition, activates oncogenic pathways including PI3K/AKT signaling and the Hippo pathway, and drives luminal-to-basal lineage conversion in estrogen receptor-positive breast cancer, resulting in endocrine therapy resistance. Conversely, ARID1A overexpression can provoke oxidative stress through CYP450 activation, suggesting dual mechanisms in tumor progression. The therapeutic targeting of downstream effectors such as the PI3K/AKT pathway through inhibitors like buparlisib and MK-2206 offers potential strategies to counteract ARID1A loss-driven oncogenic signaling and restore sensitivity to endocrine therapies.

title: Trichosanthin elicits antitumor activity via MICU3 mediated mitochondria calcium influx
summary: Trichosanthin, a bioactive glycoprotein from the Chinese medicinal herb *Trichosanthes kirilowi*, exerts potent antitumor activity in lung adenocarcinoma through a well-defined epigenetic-metabolic axis. The compound inhibits the DNA methyltransferase DNMT3B, leading to increased expression of the transcription factor FOSL2, which subsequently activates transcription of the MICU3 gene—a mitochondrial calcium uptake regulator that is characteristically underexpressed in lung adenocarcinoma. MICU3 upregulation promotes mitochondrial calcium influx, triggering cancer cell apoptosis while reducing tumor growth, cell migration, and angiogenic markers (Ki67, α-SMA, CD31) while enhancing immune infiltration. MICU3 overexpression alone is sufficient to reduce tumor size and increase cell death, and pathway analysis reveals that MICU3-mediated antitumor effects involve enrichment of immune-regulatory pathways including cytokine-receptor interactions, JAK-STAT signaling, and antiviral responses. These findings establish MICU3 as a critical effector of trichosanthin's therapeutic mechanism and identify high MICU3 expression as a favorable prognostic indicator associated with improved survival rates in lung adenocarcinoma patients.

title: Bevacizumab in ovarian cancer: Clinical data and predictive and prognostic biomarkers
summary:
# Summary

This article examines bevacizumab, a monoclonal antibody targeting VEGF-A, as a therapeutic agent for ovarian cancer with emphasis on mechanistic pathways and predictive biomarkers. The VEGF/VEGFR signaling axis drives angiogenesis under hypoxic conditions, sustaining tumor growth and creating an immunosuppressive microenvironment, particularly in homologous recombination deficiency (HRD)-positive disease. Bevacizumab inhibits VEGF-mediated signaling by suppressing endothelial proliferation and promoting vascular normalization, which enhances T-cell infiltration and improves anti-tumor immunity. Clinical trials demonstrate bevacizumab efficacy in multiple settings: initial treatment reduces progression-free survival risk, combination with olaparib particularly benefits HRD-positive patients, and rechallenge benefits platinum-sensitive recurrent disease. Emerging resistance mechanisms involve alternative angiogenic pathways including FGF, PDGF, and angiopoietins, while predictive biomarkers such as high PlGF and VEGF-D levels predict bevacizumab failure, whereas signatures including activin A, IL-1β, ANGPTL4, and HGF associate with improved response.

title: Emerging role of histone and non-histone lactylation in metabolic reprogramming of female-specific malignancies
summary: Lysine lactylation (Klac), a post-translational modification catalyzed by enzymes including p300, KAT2A, and CBP, has emerged as a critical epigenetic driver in female-specific malignancies including breast, ovarian, cervical, and endometrial cancers. Histone Klac modifications, particularly H3K18lac and H4K12lac, localize to gene promoters and directly couple glycolytic metabolism to transcriptional reprogramming, simultaneously activating oncogenic programs while suppressing immune surveillance through macrophage polarization and tumor-associated macrophage immunosuppression. The lactate-to-Klac axis operates through ACSS2-mediated lactyl-CoA synthesis, which serves as the substrate for histone lactylation, while opposing deacetylases (SIRT1/2, HDAC1-3) maintain dynamic equilibrium. This metabolic-epigenetic crosstalk drives multiple hallmarks of cancer progression including cellular proliferation, migration, invasion, metastasis, and chemotherapy resistance, with elevated H4K12lac in triple-negative breast cancer specifically linked to poor prognosis. Non-histone lactylation of proteins like ACAA2 further amplifies these effects by activating fatty acid metabolism and autophagy. Collectively, Klac represents a fundamental mechanistic bridge between the Warburg effect and oncogenic gene expression, offering novel therapeutic targets for endometrial and other female cancers.

title: PRMT3-mediated FOXO1 arginine methylation exacerbates oxidative stress-induced decidualization defects in the eutopic endometrium of endometriosis
journal: Cellular and Molecular Life Sciences: CMLS
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals a pathogenic mechanism in endometriosis where elevated PRMT3 protein in the eutopic endometrium directly methylates FOXO1 at arginine 253, promoting its ubiquitin-proteasome degradation and nuclear exclusion. Consequently, reduced FOXO1 levels impair the antioxidant capacity of endometrial stromal cells, exacerbating oxidative stress and severely compromising decidualization—the critical endometrial transformation required for embryo implantation and successful pregnancy. Administration of SGC707, a selective PRMT3 inhibitor, successfully suppresses PRMT3 activity, restores FOXO1 expression and function, alleviates oxidative stress, and rescues decidualization defects in both cellular and in vivo endometriosis models, while also improving embryonic development outcomes. The study establishes PRMT3 as a key driver of endometriosis-associated infertility and identifies PRMT3 inhibition as a promising therapeutic target for restoring reproductive function in endometriosis patients. These findings integrate arginine methylation, oxidative stress regulation, and metabolic reprogramming as interconnected mechanisms underlying endometriosis pathogenesis.

title: EGLN1 (PHD2) role in tumor microenvironment: insights for therapeutic targeting
summary:
# Summary

EGLN1 encodes PHD2, a critical oxygen sensor that regulates the hypoxia-inducible factor HIF-1α through hydroxylation of proline residues, targeting it for proteasomal degradation via the pVHL pathway. PHD2 plays multifaceted roles in the tumor microenvironment by controlling blood vessel formation, immune cell activity, and survival of cancer cells, with HIF-1α generally exhibiting tumor-suppressive effects while HIF-2α promotes oncogenic processes. Reducing PHD2 activity normalizes blood vessel structure and improves oxygen delivery to tumors, while EGLN1 haplodeficiency specifically induces endothelial cell normalization that enhances tumor oxygenation and drug diffusion while decreasing metastasis. Beyond HIF regulation, PHD2 hydroxylates alternative substrates including p53, AKT1, and BRD4, expanding its influence on cancer development and metabolic reprogramming. The context-dependent roles of EGLN1 vary by cancer type, functioning as a tumor suppressor in colorectal and pancreatic cancers but exhibiting pro-oncogenic effects in acute myeloid leukemia and ovarian carcinoma, highlighting the complexity of targeting this pathway therapeutically.

title: Genome-wide transcriptional analysis of rapeseed (Brassica napus L.) responses to heat stress: an integrated RNA-Seq meta-analysis, gene co-expression, and gene regulatory network study
summary:
# Summary

This genome-wide transcriptional study of rapeseed heat stress responses reveals a coordinated metabolic reprogramming centered on three functionally distinct gene modules. The upregulated green module promotes energy conservation and proteostasis through enhanced protein folding and heat shock responses, while downregulated black and brown modules suppress photosynthesis, carbon fixation, and secondary metabolism to redirect resources toward stress protection. Four heat-stress-activated transcription factors—HSFA3, HSFA8, BZIP69, and GATA11—orchestrate this adaptive program by upregulating chaperone proteins, reactive oxygen species detoxification enzymes, and ion transport regulators, while simultaneously suppressing growth and basal immunity through repression of key photosynthetic regulators like GLK2 and RuBisCO activase. The coordinated downregulation of photosynthetic machinery, carbohydrate metabolism, and defense-related ion transport in the black and brown modules reflects a strategic energy-sparing mechanism during heat stress. This integrated response demonstrates how plants reprogram their transcriptome through selective activation of protective pathways and suppression of energy-expensive processes, with strong positive correlations between the green module and heat stress conditions (r = 0.93) confirming the robustness of this adaptive strategy.

title: Expanding the landscape of the unfolded protein response: The roles of secondary transcription factors in development and disease
summary: The unfolded protein response (UPR) expands beyond its canonical primary transcription factors to encompass a complex cascade of secondary transcription factors that orchestrate cellular adaptation to endoplasmic reticulum stress. Under basal conditions, the chaperone GRP78 binds and inhibits three major UPR sensor proteins—PERK, IRE1α, and ATF6—but upon ER stress, these sensors activate distinct downstream pathways: PERK phosphorylates eIF2α and activates ATF4, while IRE1α splices XBP1 to generate the active XBP1s isoform, and ATF6 translocates to the nucleus. ATF4 acts as a critical hub, activating at least fifteen secondary transcription factors including the C/EBP family members (CEBPB, CEBPD, CEBPG), which collectively regulate inflammatory responses, metabolic homeostasis, immune cell differentiation, and lipid metabolism—processes highly relevant to tissue remodeling and metabolic reprogramming in endometriosis. Other ATF4-regulated secondary factors such as PRDM15, DPF2, BRF2, and TAF15 control additional critical functions including metabolic signaling pathways (PI3K/AKT/mTOR, glycolysis), cell cycle progression, and anti-inflammatory gene expression through NRF2. This multi-tiered transcriptional architecture reveals how UPR activation integrates stress responses with developmental, metabolic, and immune processes that are dysregulated in endometriotic lesions.

title: An AI-driven multi-omics framework identifies lactylation-mediated therapeutic targets to overcome drug resistance in ovarian cancer
summary:
# Summary

This study employs an AI-driven multi-omics framework to identify lactylation as a critical driver of chemotherapy resistance in ovarian cancer. Lactate dehydrogenase A (LDHA) and the lactate transporter SLC16A3 promote excessive lactate accumulation through glycolytic metabolism, which undergoes lactylation—a histone modification that reprograms transcription, enhances DNA repair, stimulates autophagy, and suppresses anti-tumor immunity. LDHA-high tumors exhibit elevated glycolytic flux, acidic microenvironments, and increased expression of drug resistance genes (ABCB1, GSTP1, TOP2A) alongside immunological exhaustion marked by high PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint expression and low interferon-gamma levels, collectively associating with poor overall survival, particularly in TP53-mutant cases. The LDHA inhibitor FX11 reverses this resistance phenotype by reducing lactate accumulation and histone lactylation, restoring cisplatin sensitivity and reactivating CD8+ T cell-mediated anti-tumor immunity. These findings identify lactylation-mediated pathways as actionable therapeutic targets to overcome drug resistance in ovarian cancer.

title: N6-methyladenosine: a key regulator in ocular disease mechanisms and treatment
summary: This review examines N6-methyladenosine (m6A) modification, a reversible epigenetic mark on RNA that serves as a critical regulator of gene expression, cell proliferation, differentiation, and apoptosis through multiple post-transcriptional mechanisms including splicing, stability, trafficking, and translation. The m6A modification system comprises writer proteins (METTL3/METTL14/WTAP complex guided by VIRMA and ZC3H13), eraser proteins (FTO and ALKBH5 demethylases), and reader proteins (YTHDF and HNRNP families) that collectively control mRNA fate and function. Under physiological conditions, m6A modification maintains tissue homeostasis; however, dysregulation of this system is associated with various ocular diseases including keratitis, cataracts, glaucoma, retinopathy, and thyroid ophthalmopathy, as well as broader pathological conditions such as cancer, neurological disorders, and metabolic diseases. Different reader proteins confer distinct outcomes: YTHDF1 and YTHDF3 promote translation, YTHDF2 directs RNAs to degradation via the CCR4-NOT complex, while YTHDC1 regulates splicing and export and HNRNP proteins modulate splicing and cleavage. The review emphasizes that understanding m6A-mediated regulatory mechanisms and dysregulation patterns is essential for developing targeted therapeutic interventions for diseases where aberrant m6A modification drives pathogenesis, particularly in ocular diseases with inflammatory and proliferative components.

title: Otubain deubiquitinases: Multi-layered regulators of cell fate, development, and stress responses across kingdoms
summary: Otubain deubiquitinases (OTUB1 and OTUB2) are multifunctional regulators that control cell fate, development, and stress responses through both catalytic and non-catalytic mechanisms. These enzymes selectively cleave different ubiquitin chain linkages—OTUB1 targets K48-linked chains while OTUB2 processes both K48- and K63-linked chains—and can inhibit ubiquitination by directly binding E2 enzymes like UBC13. OTUB1 exhibits context-dependent functions, promoting apoptosis through p53 stabilization while simultaneously suppressing it via mitochondrial complex I stabilization and ferroptosis inhibition through SLC7A11 protection. In contrast, OTUB2 generally promotes pro-survival signaling by enhancing K63-linked ubiquitination and activating the AKT/mTOR pathway, though it also drives necroptosis via RIPK3 deubiquitination. Both enzymes regulate DNA repair through MSH2 and CHK1 stabilization and modulate immune checkpoint signaling by stabilizing PD-L1, highlighting their broad importance in proteostasis, metabolic adaptation, and immune regulation across biological systems.

title: Metabolic reprogramming in the post-metastatic tumor microenvironment: multi-omics insights into determinants of immunotherapy response
summary:
# Summary

Metabolic reprogramming in the tumor microenvironment drives immune dysfunction and resistance to checkpoint immunotherapy through multiple interconnected pathways. Lactate accumulation, a hallmark of glycolytic tumors, stabilizes HIF-1α and activates NF-κB signaling to upregulate PD-L1, while simultaneously suppressing T-cell function, promoting M2 macrophage polarization, and inhibiting natural killer cell cytotoxicity. Lipid metabolic rewiring similarly establishes immunosuppression through fatty acid oxidation-mediated PD-L1 expression, generation of immunosuppressive lipid mediators like prostaglandins, and selective metabolic advantages for regulatory T cells and myeloid-derived suppressor cells over effector T cells. Novel cell death pathways including ferroptosis and cuproptosis contribute additional layers of immune regulation, with ferroptotic tumor cells promoting antitumor immunity while excessive ferroptosis in immune cells compromises their function. Therapeutic targeting of these metabolic nodes—including LDHA and MCT transporter inhibition, FAO suppression, ferroptosis induction, and copper modulation—shows promise in reversing metabolic immunosuppression and restoring responsiveness to checkpoint blockade. Integrated multi-omics approaches combining metabolomics, transcriptomics, and spatial analyses reveal that metabolite-rich niches within tumors create distinct immunological landscapes, with lactate- and cholesterol-enriched regions corresponding to immune "cold zones" resistant to current immunotherapies.

title: Impaired nuclear PTEN function drives macrocephaly, lymphadenopathy and late-onset cancer in PTEN hamartoma tumour syndrome
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that impaired nuclear localization of the PTEN-R173C pathogenic variant, commonly found in PTEN hamartoma tumor syndrome (PHTS), drives disease phenotypes through defective DNA repair rather than cytoplasmic signaling functions. While PTEN-R173C retains or even enhances phosphatase activity toward PIP3, the mutation causes protein instability through reduced phosphorylation at sites S380/T382/T383, leading to increased ubiquitination and degradation. Pten+/R173C mice exhibit macrocephaly, lymphoid hyperplasia, and increased genomic instability characterized by elevated γH2AX levels following DNA damage, reflecting compromised homologous recombination capacity. Notably, these mice maintain normal glucose homeostasis and basal PI3K/AKT signaling despite cytoplasmic PIP3 phosphatase activity, indicating that impaired nuclear PTEN functions—specifically double-stranded DNA damage repair and cell cycle control—are the primary drivers of PHTS phenotypes. These findings suggest that late-onset cancer susceptibility in PHTS patients arises from accumulated secondary genetic alterations facilitated by chronic defective DNA repair, linking nuclear PTEN dysfunction to both developmental and malignant manifestations of the syndrome.

title: Exploring the role and therapeutic potential of RNA N6-methyladenosine modification in abortion disease pathology: a comprehensive review
summary: N6-methyladenosine (m6A) modification represents a critical epigenetic mechanism regulating reproductive and developmental processes, with dysregulation implicated in pregnancy complications and reproductive disorders. The m6A pathway comprises writer proteins (METTL3, METTL14, WTAP, RBM15, VIRMA, ZC3H13), eraser proteins (FTO, ALKBH5), and reader proteins (YTHDF and YTHDC families, IGF2BP, HNRNP) that coordinately control mRNA fate through methylation, demethylation, and recognition of adenosine modifications. These proteins are essential for gametogenesis, follicular development, oocyte maturation, spermatogenesis, and early embryonic development, with METTL3/METTL14 promoting methylation and FTO/ALKBH5 enabling maternal mRNA degradation through demethylation. In reproductive pathology, m6A dysregulation compromises implantation-related processes, particularly affecting long non-coding RNAs and factors like LIF in patients with recurrent implantation failure, while reduced WTAP expression in early-onset preeclampsia impairs trophoblast migration by destabilizing HMGN3 mRNA. These findings position m6A modification as a therapeutic target for reproductive disorders, suggesting that modulating writer, eraser, and reader protein activities could restore normal implantation and pregnancy outcomes.

title: MUC1/CA15-3 identifies a clear cell renal carcinoma characterized by Sunitinib response with a specific metabolic signature
summary: MUC1 overexpression in clear cell renal carcinoma (ccRCC) drives a distinctive pathological phenotype characterized by increased angiogenesis, epithelial-mesenchymal transition, and activation of metabolic reprogramming pathways including glucose and lipid metabolism alterations. The soluble form of MUC1, CA15-3, serves as a serum biomarker that correlates with tumor burden and predicts clinical response to the tyrosine kinase inhibitor sunitinib in metastatic ccRCC patients. MUC1-overexpressing tumors exhibit enhanced microvascular density and engagement of the kynurenine pathway, which promotes cancer cell survival, migration, and chemoresistance through metabolic reprogramming. Sunitinib-responsive tumors demonstrate a characteristic metabolic signature marked by impaired oxidative phosphorylation alongside alterations in glucose and lipid utilization, distinguishing treatment-sensitive from resistant disease. Elevated serum CA15-3 levels independently predict poor survival outcomes and treatment response, establishing this MUC1-derived biomarker as a valuable clinical tool for patient stratification and prognostic assessment in metastatic ccRCC.

title: Dysregulated autophagy in endometriosis: molecular mechanisms, controversies, and clinical implications
summary:
# Summary

Dysregulated autophagy emerges as a central pathological mechanism in endometriosis, a chronic inflammatory estrogen-dependent disease characterized by chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, infertility, and depression. Autophagy, a conserved cellular degradation process essential for maintaining homeostasis, is aberrantly elevated in ectopic endometrial tissue, particularly in response to hypoxia through HIF-1α-dependent pathways that also upregulate pro-migratory microRNAs (miR-210, miR-150-5p) and long non-coding RNAs (lncRNA-MALAT1).
