FBXW7 overexpression potently suppresses ovarian carcinoma cell proliferation and migration by upregulating the epithelial marker E-cadherin while simultaneously downregulating mesenchymal markers N-cadherin and vimentin, thereby inhibiting EMT progression. Conversely, FBXW7 knockdown promotes EMT and aggressive cell behavior, with direct protein interactions between FBXW7 and vimentin suggesting a mechanistic role in regulating the mesenchymal phenotype. These findings establish FBXW7 as a critical tumor suppressor in endometriosis-associated ovarian carcinoma that counteracts EMT-driven malignant transformation, positioning it as a potential therapeutic target for this aggressive cancer subtype.

title: Roles of ubiquitin-specific peptidase 22 in cellular fate: From embryonic survival to tissue repair, inflammation and metabolism (Review)
summary:
# Summary

USP22, a deubiquitinase enzyme, plays multifaceted roles spanning embryonic development, tissue repair, and metabolic homeostasis through its ability to stabilize and deubiquitinate key signaling proteins. The enzyme is essential for embryonic viability and placental angiogenesis via stabilization of SIRT1 and HIF-1α, which suppress apoptosis and promote vascular growth respectively; conversely, USP22 deletion causes embryonic lethality and impairs placental vascular development. Beyond development, USP22 regulates diverse pathological processes including tumorigenesis through E2F6 and AKT signaling activation, immune evasion via PD-L1 stabilization, and metabolic dysfunction in diabetes where high-glucose conditions upregulate USP22 to promote ferroptosis and podocyte depletion through ACSL4 and HMGB1 accumulation. These context-dependent functions highlight USP22 as a critical node in cellular homeostasis, with dysregulation contributing to embryonic disease, cancer progression, and metabolic complications, suggesting therapeutic potential through selective USP22 inhibition in disease contexts while preserving its developmental functions.

title: Editorial: The role of neutrophil extracellular traps formation in tumor microenvironment – from basic research to clinical applications
summary:
# Summary

Neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs) are dysregulated DNA-protein structures that drive multiple pathological processes in cancer and other diseases, including endometriosis. Dysregulated NET formation contributes to autoimmunity, thrombosis, organ damage, and cancer progression by promoting metastasis, treatment resistance, and epithelial-mesenchymal transition through NET-associated DNA and proteases that activate key signaling pathways including TGFβ/Smad, NF-κB/NLRP3, and Notch1. Hypoxia-induced NET formation is mediated by HIF-1α-dependent glycolysis and PADI4 upregulation, and elevated NET levels in tumor tissue and blood are consistently associated with advanced disease stage, metastasis, and poor overall survival. Preclinical evidence demonstrates that multiple therapeutic strategies—including DNase I treatment, PAD4 inhibition, neutrophil elastase inhibition, and pathway-specific inhibitors targeting NET-driven mechanisms—can reduce NET-mediated invasion, EMT, metastasis, and treatment resistance. Combining NET-modulating agents with standard and immunotherapies offers promising clinical potential for improving cancer outcomes and managing NET-associated complications in endometriosis and other NET-driven diseases.

title: Unravelling the Contribution of Matrix Metalloproteinase-3 Genotype-Phenotype to Pterygium Risk
summary:
# Summary

This study investigates the role of matrix metalloproteinase-3 (MMP-3) genetic variation in pterygium, a fibrovascular ocular disease characterized by excessive extracellular matrix remodeling. The MMP-3 rs3025058 polymorphism, particularly the 5A allele and 5A/5A homozygous genotype, shows strong association with elevated pterygium risk, with the 5A/5A genotype conferring a 4.36-fold increased risk compared to wild-type 6A/6A carriers. Individuals carrying 5A alleles demonstrate significantly higher MMP-3 messenger RNA and protein expression levels, which correlates with enhanced enzymatic activity in degrading multiple extracellular matrix components including collagen, fibronectin, laminin, and elastin. MMP-3 acts as a key node in a proteolytic cascade, being upregulated by pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-1β and TNF-α while simultaneously activating downstream matrix metalloproteinases (MMP-1, MMP-7, MMP-9, MMP-13) that drive tissue remodeling, angiogenesis, and fibroblast migration characteristic of pterygium lesions. The rs3025058 variant thus serves as a genetic biomarker for pterygium susceptibility, suggesting that MMP-3 genotype-phenotype relationships fundamentally contribute to disease pathogenesis through dysregulated extracellular matrix remodeling and inflammation.

title: NAT10 and ac4C modification in cancer immunity and metabolism: emerging mechanisms and therapeutic potential
summary: NAT10, a key acetyltransferase enzyme, catalyzes ac4C modifications on transfer RNA and serves as a critical regulator of both immune suppression and metabolic reprogramming in tumor cells. The NAT10-ac4C axis promotes an immunosuppressive microenvironment by activating inflammasomes, suppressing T-cell and cytotoxic T-cell function, and driving M2 macrophage polarization, thereby facilitating immune evasion. Metabolically, this axis drives the Warburg effect by stabilizing mRNA of glycolytic enzymes hexokinase 2 and lactate dehydrogenase A, while also regulating amino acid and lipid metabolism and conferring ferroptosis resistance. Additionally, NAT10 functions as a protein acetyltransferase that acetylates p53 to activate the p53-p21 pathway and acetylates the chromatin remodeling protein MORC2 to suppress CDK1 and cyclin B1 expression in breast cancer cells. High NAT10 expression is associated with chemotherapy and radiotherapy resistance across multiple tumor types, and NAT10 shows altered subcellular localization from the nucleus in healthy tissues to the cytoplasm in tumor tissues, correlating with tumor progression. The NAT10 inhibitor Remodelin demonstrates synergistic antitumor effects when combined with immune checkpoint inhibitors, suggesting therapeutic potential for targeting this axis in cancer treatment.

title: Dual Roles of Ubiquitin-Specific Peptidase 10 (USP10) in Cancer
summary: USP10, a deubiquitinase enzyme, plays dual and often opposing roles across multiple cancer types including hepatocellular carcinoma, lung cancer, breast cancer, and gastric cancer. USP10 deubiquitinates and stabilizes numerous signaling proteins including TP53, PTEN, G3BP1/2, AMPK, and autophagy regulators Beclin1 and LC3, thereby regulating critical cellular processes such as cell proliferation, stress responses, and immune function. Under normal conditions, USP10 localizes to the cytoplasm and stabilizes the tumor suppressor TP53 by removing ubiquitin chains, promoting cell cycle arrest and apoptosis; however, this protective function can be disrupted by hypoxia-induced regulatory RNAs like circWSB1, which sequesters USP10 and promotes TP53 degradation. Conversely, USP10 also promotes tumor progression by stabilizing oncogenic proteins and immune checkpoint molecules such as PD-L1, YAP1, and NICD1, while simultaneously suppressing anti-tumor immune responses through M2 macrophage polarization. These opposing roles highlight USP10's context-dependent functions as both a tumor suppressor and oncogenic factor, making it a critical node in cancer pathogenesis and a potential therapeutic target.

title: Identification and Validation of MTFP1 as a Mitochondrial Target Restoring Dynamics and ECM Remodeling in Acute Myocardial Infarction
summary:
# Summary

Acute myocardial infarction results from mitochondrial dysfunction triggered by myocardial ischemia, which impairs ATP generation, calcium buffering, and reactive oxygen species homeostasis, ultimately compromising cardiac contractility and promoting inflammatory responses. MTFP1 exerts cardioprotective effects by restoring mitochondrial fission balance through upregulation of phosphorylated DRP1, which enhances mitochondrial dynamics and prevents accumulation of damaged organelles. Additionally, MTFP1 regulates extracellular matrix remodeling by simultaneously upregulating both MMP9 and its inhibitor TIMP1, thereby maintaining ECM steady-state while limiting harmful matrix degradation. AAV9-mediated MTFP1 overexpression significantly improved cardiac function, reduced infarct size, attenuated fibrosis through suppression of collagen I and α-SMA, and decreased oxidative stress in animal models. These findings identify MTFP1 as a dual-action mitochondrial target that addresses both the bioenergetic crisis and pathological remodeling characteristic of acute myocardial infarction, suggesting potential therapeutic applications for restoring cardiac homeostasis following ischemic injury.

title: Mitochondrial Long Non-Coding RNAs in Gynecological Cancers: Pathogenic Signaling Pathways and Therapeutic Opportunities
summary:
# Summary

Mitochondrial and nuclear long non-coding RNAs (lncRNAs) play critical roles in gynecological cancer pathogenesis through distinct yet interconnected mechanisms. Mitochondrial lncRNAs—including lncND5, lncND6, and lncCytB—regulate metabolic reprogramming, oxidative respiration, mitochondrial biogenesis, and apoptosis, while dysregulation of these molecules contributes to tumor bioenergetic reprogramming and altered mitochondrial integrity. Nuclear lncRNAs such as HOTAIR, MALAT1, PVT1, and H19 promote tumor progression, treatment resistance, and metastasis by driving cell proliferation, tumor invasion, and angiogenesis through chromatin remodeling and transcriptional regulation. The nuclear-mitochondrial regulatory axis, encompassing anterograde signaling (nucleus-to-mitochondria) and retrograde signaling (mitochondria-to-nucleus), coordinates mitochondrial biogenesis and metabolic programming through key regulators like PGC-1α, NRF1, NRF2, and TFAM. Notably, while primary gynecological tumors suppress PGC-1α signaling to favor glycolysis, metastatic and chemoresistant subpopulations upregulate PGC-1α to enhance mitochondrial function, suggesting that targeting the lncRNA-mediated nuclear-mitochondrial communication axis represents a promising therapeutic opportunity for overcoming treatment resistance in gynecological malignancies.

title: Ligand-Dependent and -Independent Functions of Activation Function 1 of Progesterone Receptor in Genome-Wide Gene Regulation and in Cell Proliferation and Apoptosis of Breast Cancer Cells
summary:
# Summary

This study elucidates how the Activation Function 1 (AF1) domain of progesterone receptor (PR) regulates gene expression and cellular responses through post-translational methylation of three key residues (K464, K481, and R492). The AF1-FFF methylation mimic mutation, which mimics hypomethylation, generates a hypoactive PR that fails to regulate approximately 60% of PR target genes and significantly impairs cell proliferation and apoptosis responses to progestin treatment, despite paradoxically exhibiting greater chromatin binding. Conversely, the AF1-QQQ hyperactive mutation enhances these cellular responses. The impaired activity of AF1-FFF PR correlates with defective regulation of critical genes involved in mitochondria-mediated apoptosis (BNIP3, BNIP3L, ENDOG, and HtrA2/Omi) and hypoxia response pathways, explaining the reduced growth inhibition and delayed apoptosis observed in these cells. This research demonstrates that AF1-mediated ligand-dependent gene regulation is essential for progesterone's well-established protective roles in preventing endometriosis and endometrial cancer through suppression of luminal epithelial cell proliferation. The findings suggest that dysregulation of PR AF1 function could contribute to progesterone resistance in endometrial pathologies.

title: BRG1 exacerbates myocardial fibrosis after myocardial infarction by interacting with ZEB1
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that BRG1, a chromatin remodeling protein, exacerbates myocardial fibrosis following myocardial infarction by forming a complex with the transcription factor ZEB1. TGF-β1 signaling elevates BRG1 levels in cardiac fibroblasts and enhances BRG1-ZEB1 interaction, which suppresses expression of Ppp2r1a, a gene encoding the PP2A phosphatase subunit. Reduced PP2A activity permits sustained Smad3 phosphorylation and nuclear translocation, thereby amplifying pro-fibrotic TGF-β/Smad3 signaling and promoting collagen production, fibroblast proliferation, and migration. In vivo, BRG1 knockdown via AAV9-mediated gene delivery reduced infarct size, improved cardiac function, and attenuated fibrosis while decreasing post-MI mortality in mouse models. These findings identify the BRG1-ZEB1-PP2A-Smad3 axis as a critical fibrotic pathway and suggest that targeting BRG1 or ZEB1 represents a therapeutic strategy to limit cardiac fibrosis and prevent heart failure progression after myocardial infarction.

title: Decoding m6A: a new frontier in maternal-foetal immunology
summary:
# Summary

N6-methyladenosine (m6A) is an epigenetic modification that plays a critical role in regulating gene expression through control of RNA metabolism, including selective splicing, transport, stability, and translation. This modification is dynamically orchestrated by writer proteins (METTL3, METTL14, and accessory factors), eraser proteins (FTO and ALKBH5), and reader proteins (YTH domain, IGF2BP, and hnRNP families) that collectively coordinate m6A deposition and removal on target RNAs. Beyond its fundamental roles in spermatogenesis, early embryonic development, and stem cell differentiation, m6A modification is increasingly recognized as essential for shaping the maternal-foetal immune microenvironment and supporting successful fertilization and embryo implantation. Dysregulation of m6A modification is associated with multiple pregnancy-related disorders including gestational diabetes mellitus, preeclampsia, foetal growth restriction, abortion, and reproductive diseases such as polycystic ovary syndrome and premature ovarian failure. These findings establish m6A as an important epigenetic frontier in maternal-foetal immunology with potential therapeutic implications for improving pregnancy outcomes and treating reproductive endocrine diseases.

title: GSK3β as a potential regulator in AML: A pan-cancer multi-omics analysis
summary:
# Summary

This pan-cancer multi-omics analysis reveals glycogen synthase kinase 3 (GSK3) isoforms as critical regulators with distinct roles across malignancies, particularly in acute myeloid leukemia (AML). GSK3β is significantly elevated in 23 cancer types including high-risk AML subtypes and outperforms GSK3α as a pan-cancer diagnostic biomarker, while GSK3α shows elevation in 19 solid tumors but suppression in AML. The two isoforms exhibit functional antagonism in immune regulation: GSK3α correlates with protumorigenic immune infiltration and predicts poor overall survival in certain cancers, whereas GSK3β suppresses cytotoxic effector function and independently stratifies cytogenetic-risk AML with strong prognostic value. Pharmacological GSK3 inhibition via CHIR-99021 effectively suppresses AML cell proliferation, induces apoptosis, causes S-phase cell cycle arrest, and downregulates oncogenic c-Myc expression. GSK3 integrates multiple signaling pathways—including insulin/IGF, Wnt/β-catenin, Hedgehog, NF-κB, and TGF-β—suggesting that targeting GSK3β could represent a therapeutic strategy for AML and other GSK3β-dependent malignancies.

title: Apoptotic vesicles in cancer: research progress in physiology and therapy
summary:
# Summary

Apoptotic extracellular vesicles (ApoEVs) are released during programmed cell death and serve critical roles in both physiological homeostasis and disease pathology. Their biogenesis depends on caspase-3 activation, which triggers a molecular cascade including ROCK1 and LIM kinase 1 signaling that drives actomyosin contraction and membrane blebbing, while simultaneously inducing phosphatidylserine externalization to mark cells for clearance. Under normal conditions, ApoEVs transport bioactive molecules—including proteins, nucleic acids, and miRNAs—that promote anti-inflammatory responses, M2 macrophage polarization, and tissue regeneration through signaling pathways such as JAK/STAT and PI3K/Akt. However, in pathological contexts including cancer, osteoarthritis, and atherosclerosis, tumor-derived and disease-associated ApoEVs paradoxically enhance malignant phenotypes, promote metastasis and drug resistance, and drive chronic inflammation through mechanisms like miR-21-5p-mediated matrix metalloproteinase upregulation. Therapeutic strategies including engineered ApoEVs loaded with immunostimulatory molecules (cGAMP) or anti-inflammatory agents, alongside Pannexin-1 inhibitors to enhance clearance, represent promising interventions to harness the regenerative potential of ApoEVs while mitigating their pathogenic contributions.

title: NDUFS1‐Mediated Mitochondrial Complex I Activity Maintains Pancreatic Cancer Stemness by Promoting PAX2 Hypomethylation
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that mitochondrial Complex I activity, particularly through its core subunit NDUFS1, is essential for maintaining pancreatic cancer stem cell (CSC) stemness via a metaboloepigenetic mechanism. The CD147-NDUFS1 axis drives oxidative phosphorylation and NAD+ production, which fuels SIRT1-DNMT1 signaling to decrease PAX2 promoter methylation and increase PAX2 expression, thereby reinforcing CSC properties. Pancreatic CSCs exhibit elevated Complex I activity and depend critically on OXPHOS for energy and self-renewal, as demonstrated by the potent inhibitory effects of Complex I inhibitors (metformin, rotenone) and ATP synthase inhibitors (oligomycin A) on stemness markers and tumorsphere formation. NDUFS1 and CD147 are individually elevated in CSCs and their co-expression strongly correlates with poor patient survival, lymph node invasion, and reduced median survival times. These findings establish metabolic reprogramming as a direct regulator of epigenetic landscape in CSCs and identify the CD147-NDUFS1-OXPHOS axis as a critical therapeutic target in pancreatic cancer.

title: IGF‐1 Deficiency Serves as an Integrated Biomarker Pathogenic Driver and Predictor in Poor Ovarian Response
summary: This study reveals that insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF-1) deficiency is a pathogenic driver and integrated biomarker of poor ovarian response (POR). Significantly reduced IGF-1 levels in serum and follicular fluid impair the IGF-1 signaling axis, which is essential for follicle-stimulating hormone responsiveness, follicular development, and oocyte competence. IGF-1 activates the PI3K/AKT and MAPK pathways in granulosa cells to promote proliferation, steroidogenesis, and oocyte maturation. Elevated follicular fluid IGF-1 concentrations positively correlate with critical in vitro fertilization outcomes, including metaphase II oocyte count, zygote formation, embryo quality, and clinical pregnancy achievement, while low IGF-1 associates with blastocyst formation failure and poor embryo availability. Mechanistically, IGFALS gene deletion in mice reduced circulating and ovarian IGF-1 expression and replicated the POR phenotype, demonstrating that systemic IGF-1 bioavailability is crucial for ovarian function. Both serum and follicular fluid IGF-1 levels serve as effective diagnostic and prognostic biomarkers for identifying POR patients and predicting pregnancy outcomes.

title: Tamoxifen differentially modulates endometrial hyperplasia via wild-type and mutant p53 regulation of the ALKBH5-REG1A axis
summary:
# Summary

This study elucidates a critical p53-dependent mechanism controlling tamoxifen's effects on endometrial hyperplasia through m6A RNA methylation regulation. Wild-type p53 activation by tamoxifen promotes transcriptional upregulation of ALKBH5, an m6A demethylase that removes methyl marks from REG1A mRNA, stabilizing it and preventing YTHDF2-mediated degradation. Elevated REG1A protein subsequently inhibits cell proliferation, providing negative feedback restraint on tamoxifen-induced growth. In contrast, the p53 R248Q hotspot mutation retains DNA-binding capacity but fails to activate ALKBH5 transcription, resulting in increased m6A methylation of REG1A mRNA, accelerated YTHDF2-dependent decay, and uncontrolled hyperproliferation. These findings establish that p53 allelic status critically determines whether tamoxifen triggers protective anti-proliferative responses or pathogenic endometrial hyperplasia through differential control of the ALKBH5-REG1A axis, linking hormonal signaling, p53 status, and m6A-dependent post-transcriptional regulation in endometrial disease progression.

title: CD147: a critical factor in normal and pathological pregnancies
summary: CD147, a transmembrane glycoprotein abundantly expressed in trophoblastic and endometrial tissues, functions as a critical regulator of normal pregnancy through multiple interconnected mechanisms. The protein orchestrates embryo implantation and placental development primarily by binding cyclophilin A to activate downstream signaling pathways including ERK/MAPK, PI3K/Akt, NF-κB, and Wnt/β-catenin, which collectively promote trophoblast invasion, angiogenesis, and epithelial-mesenchymal transition via induction of matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs) and VEGF. CD147 additionally sustains glycolytic metabolism by chaperoning monocarboxylate transporters MCT1 and MCT4, and amplifies inflammatory responses through upregulation of cytokines including IL-1α, IL-1β, and chemokines CCL3, CCL20, CXCL2, and CXCL5. Reduced CD147 expression in the endometrium is associated with recurrent implantation failure and pre-eclampsia, while complete CD147 deficiency causes embryonic lethality, fetal growth restriction, and impaired angiogenesis, establishing the protein as essential for pregnancy success. Conversely, CD147 upregulation drives pathological invasiveness in choriocarcinoma and placenta accreta, suggesting dysregulated CD147 contributes to both placental insufficiency and excessive trophoblastic invasion disorders. These findings position CD147 as a central metabolic and signaling hub whose balanced expression is critical for normal pregnancy, with implications for understanding pregnancy pathologies and potential therapeutic targeting.

title: HIF2A as a prognostic and clinical therapeutic target in ovarian clear cell carcinoma
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian clear cell carcinoma is closely associated with endometriosis, a condition that creates a hypoxic microenvironment within endometriotic cysts. HIF2A, a key regulator of hypoxic responses, is highly expressed in ovarian clear cell carcinoma and drives disease progression through upregulation of downstream genes including VEGFA, NDRG1, GLUT1, and IGFBP3. High HIF2A nuclear expression correlates significantly with decreased progression-free and overall survival, establishing it as an important prognostic marker. Mechanistically, HIF2A suppresses mitochondrial function and ATP production while limiting reactive oxygen species; inhibition of HIF2A through genetic knockdown or the inhibitor NKT2152 restores mitochondrial respiration, increases ATP production, and enhances ROS accumulation, which collectively suppresses tumor growth. These findings identify HIF2A as a therapeutic target, with the HIF2A inhibitor Belzutifan currently being evaluated in clinical trials for recurrent or persistent ovarian clear cell carcinoma, offering a promising strategy to reverse the metabolic reprogramming driven by endometriosis-associated hypoxia.

title: Ubiquitin-conjugating Enzymes in Cancer
summary: Ubiquitin-conjugating enzymes (E2s) are critical regulators of protein stability and cancer hallmarks including proliferation, immune evasion, metastasis, and metabolic reprogramming through orchestration of distinct ubiquitin chain topologies. K11 and K48 ubiquitin chains direct protein degradation via the 26S proteasome, while K63 chains mediate non-proteolytic functions in autophagy, DNA repair, and cell cycle control. Specific E2 enzymes are dysregulated across multiple cancer types, with notable examples including UBE2C overexpression driving tumor proliferation and invasion, UBE2A mutations causing loss of regulatory control in chronic myeloid leukemia and diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, and UBE2O gene amplification promoting cell proliferation through AMPKα2 degradation. E2 N-terminal and C-terminal extensions modulate their substrate specificity and catalytic efficiency through selective E3 ligase binding and subcellular localization, exemplified by UBE2M's enhancement of NEDD8 binding and UBE2G2's ER membrane targeting. Targeting E2 enzymes represents a promising therapeutic approach, as UBE2C downregulation inhibits ERK/AKT signaling and tumorigenesis while reducing cyclin B1 and CDK1 levels. These findings establish E2 enzymes as central pathogenic drivers in malignancy and potential therapeutic targets for cancer intervention.

title: Follistatin-like protein 1 (FSTL1) modulates bone remodeling and attenuates bone loss in a mouse model of postmenopausal osteoporosis
summary: Follistatin-like protein 1 (FSTL1), a secreted glycoprotein circulating in serum, is markedly reduced in postmenopausal osteoporosis and declines with advancing age, particularly after age 50. FSTL1 exerts a dual regulatory role in bone remodeling by promoting osteoblast differentiation and mineralization through upregulation of master transcription factors RUNX2 and SP7, while simultaneously suppressing osteoclastogenesis by downregulating key osteoclast markers including NFATc1, CTSK, and c-Fos. FSTL1 haploinsufficiency in ovariectomized mice results in exacerbated bone loss characterized by reduced bone mineral density, trabecular thickness and number, and elevated osteoclast abundance, whereas therapeutic administration of recombinant FSTL1 protein substantially reverses ovariectomy-induced bone loss and restores trabecular architecture. These findings establish FSTL1 as a protective factor in bone homeostasis with potential therapeutic applications for postmenopausal osteoporosis, likely operating through interactions with TGF-β signaling pathways in osteoblasts.

title: Crosstalk between YAP/TAZ and ERα in mechanical and hormonal signaling in the skeletal system
summary:
# Summary

This article examines the crosstalk between YAP/TAZ and estrogen receptor alpha (ERα) in skeletal mechanotransduction and hormonal signaling. YAP/TAZ function as central hubs of the Hippo pathway, converting biomechanical cues through the actin cytoskeleton, F-actin dynamics, and focal adhesion complexes to promote osteogenic differentiation while suppressing adipogenesis. ERα integrates both mechanical stimuli and estradiol signaling to coordinate bone remodeling by activating osteoblast differentiation while inhibiting RANKL and promoting osteoclast apoptosis. The two pathways exhibit functional crosstalk, with YAP/TAZ and ERα demonstrating both synergistic cooperation and context-dependent antagonism in regulating osteoblastogenesis and bone homeostasis. Additionally, YAP/TAZ play critical roles in osteocyte function and bone remodeling through mechanosensing of shear stress and regulation of chemokine expression. The article also identifies regulatory mechanisms including BMP-2/Smad signaling synergy with YAP/TAZ, involvement of TEAD transcription factors, and context-specific roles depending on osteoblast maturation stage, providing comprehensive insights into skeletal mechanobiology and estrogen-responsive bone regulation.

title: The TCF7L2/miR-206/Cofilin1 axis promotes the metastasis of bladder cancer cells by regulating the formation of invadopodia: TCF7L2/miR-206/Cofilin1 regulates invadopodia
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies the TCF7L2/miR-206/Cofilin1 signaling axis as a critical regulator of bladder cancer metastasis through invadopodia formation. TCF7L2, a key Wnt signaling transcription factor, upregulates Cofilin1 expression by binding to its promoter while simultaneously suppressing miR-206 maturation, thereby relieving miR-206-mediated inhibition of Cofilin1-cortactin binding. Elevated Cofilin1 promotes cytoskeletal remodeling by depolymerizing F-actin through interactions with cortactin, which drives invadopodia formation and enhances bladder cancer cell proliferation and migration. Cofilin1 is significantly elevated in bladder cancer tissues and invasive cell lines, correlates with tumor grade and reduced cancer-specific survival, and its knockout substantially inhibits malignant phenotypes. The findings establish Cofilin1 as a central node in cancer cell invasion and suggest the TCF7L2/miR-206/Cofilin1 pathway represents a targetable mechanism for controlling bladder cancer metastasis.

title: Sestrins as Biomarkers of Cellular Stress and Human Disease
summary:
# Summary

Sestrins are stress-responsive proteins regulated by multiple pathways including p53, NRF2, FOXO transcription factors, and endoplasmic reticulum stress sensors (ATF4, ATF6, XBP1), with SESN2 particularly inducible by hypoxia and oxidative stress. These proteins function as cellular guardians by directly neutralizing reactive oxygen species and indirectly supporting antioxidant signaling through NRF2 activation, while also promoting mitochondrial recycling and regulating metabolic homeostasis. Sestrins suppress protein synthesis and promote autophagy by inhibiting mTORC1 and mTORC2 complexes, with leucine serving as a regulatory factor under basal conditions. Disease associations reveal context-dependent patterns: sestrin levels decrease in aging, heart failure, metabolic disorders (diabetes, NASH), and degenerative conditions (osteoarthritis, intervertebral disc degeneration), whereas levels increase in inflammatory and neurological diseases (temporal lobe epilepsy, Parkinson's disease, COPD) and certain infections. Physical activity including resistance exercise and endurance training elevates sestrins in skeletal muscle, while immobilization reduces expression, positioning sestrins as potential biomarkers linking cellular stress responses to systemic metabolic and pathological conditions.

title: TCF21 promotes epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition and cytoskeleton reorganization in uterine development and endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

TCF21, a transcription factor highly expressed in endometriosis patients, plays a critical role in promoting epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT) and cytoskeleton reorganization in both normal uterine development and endometriosis pathogenesis. TCF21 activates the LIMK2-cofilin signaling pathway in stromal cells, leading to actin cytoskeleton reorganization that enhances cell invasion and adhesion—hallmarks of endometriotic lesion formation. Notably, Tcf21 knockout in mice alleviates endometriotic lesions while impairing normal uterine development, demonstrating its essential role in these processes. TCF21 function is further modulated through SUMOylation-mediated stabilization and suppression by FOXM1 and microRNA-92a-3p, and can form a transcriptional complex with USF2 to activate SF-1 and estrogen receptor beta expression. Pharmacological inhibition of LIMK kinase activity effectively reduces endometriosis incidence, suggesting that targeting the TCF21-LIMK2 axis represents a promising therapeutic strategy for this debilitating gynecological disease.
